CIVIL CODE OF PRACTICE OF KENTUCKY .
EDITED BY
JOHN FELAND AND JOSHUA F. BULLITT ,
COMMISSIONERS .

We , JOHN FELAND and JOSHUA F. BULLITT, Commissioners
to edit the CivilCode of Practiceof Kentucky, adopted by an
act approved the 20th day of March , 1876, certifythat we have
compared the said Code , as contained in the next, following one
hundred and seventy-six pages, with the enrollmentthereof, and
have found the formerto be a true copy of the latter, with these
exceptions :
I. The word " be " should follow the word " defendant " in the Ist line, instead of fol-
lowing the figure" I " in the second line, of ? 57.
2. " XIII " should be " XIV " in ? 116, subs.6 .
3. Subsection I of ? 117 should commence with the word " pleadings" instead of the
words " a pleading."
4. The word " purpose" should be " purposes," in ? 145.
5. The word " by " should be " upon " in the second lineof ? 163.
6. The word " is" should be the words " has been ," in the firstlineof ? 177-
7. The word " such" should be the word " all," in 2 430.
8. The word " before" afterthe word " or," in the 2d lineof ? 564, isnot in the en-
rolled bill .
9. The enrolledbillhas the word " by " between the word " or" and the word " one,"
in the 4th lineof 606, subs.2.
10. The enrolledbilldoes not containthe word " guardian" preceding the words " guar-
dian ad litem," in § 632.
11.The phrase" 7th," inline2 of 819,is,intheenrolledbill, " 8th."
12. In 837, the word " further" should be " future."
13. The word " which " afterthe word " or," in line3 of 838, is not in the enrolled
bill .
JOHN FELAND ,
JOSHUA F. BULLITT .
I certifythatthe foregoingcertificateistrue.
J. STODDARD JOHNSTON ,
Secretary of State.

EDITOR'S PREFACE .
I wished to make referencesinthe body,or atany rateatthefoot,of
each annotatedsection,to the notesthereto. But the publishershad
caused the Civiland CriminalCodes to be stereotyped, beforeI com-
menced annotatingthem ; and, upon inquiry, I concurredwith them
that the convenience to the profession, which might resultfrom such
references,would notcompensateforthe increasedcostand delaywhich
wouldhavebeencausedby thecutting,& c.,of thestereotypedplates-
delaytobe avoidedasfaraspossible,assaidCodesaretogo intoeffect
to-day .
The reader, afterfamiliarizinghimselfwith the arrangementof the
work,can readilylearnwhetheror not any sectionhas been annotated,
and findthenotesthereto,ifany. As to the arrangementof thework,
see the tableof contentsappended to thispreface.
Upon thesubjectsof ForcibleEntryand Detainer,FinalityofJudg-
ments,Assignment of Errors,and a few othersubjects, my notesrefer
to decisionsrenderedpriorto 1851. But,as a generalrule,they refer
only to reporteddecisionsof the Courtof Appeals concerningthe pro-
visions of the CivilCode of 1851, and of the Civiland Criminal Codes
of 1854 .
I had preparednotesofa number of MS . Opinionsconcerningthe
CivilCode, to which I concludednot to referin my publishednotes;
believingthat,with the exceptionof Littell'sSelectedCases,Reports of
MS .Opinionsof theCourt of Appeals have been,and thatmine would
have been,more apt to misleadunwary lawyersthanto affecttheminds
of experiencedjudges; that,thoughcircuitjudgesmay feelbound to
followthem,the Court of Appeals pays them littleor no respect,unless
theyhappen toaccordwithviewsformedindependentlyofthem; and,
in short,thatthe practiceof publishingOpinionswhich the courtdid
not chooseto rescuefrom oblivionis,as a generalrule," more honored
in the breach than the observance ."
The sectionsoftheoldCodes,ofwhichtherearecorrespondingsec-
tionsinthenew,arecontainedinbrackets. Few transpositionsof sec-
tionswere made in the Revision of the CivilCode ; but the Revision of
the CriminalCode containsa number of such transpositions,a tableof
which iscontainedin pages 147 and 148, followingthe index to the
Criminal Code .
JANUARY 1st, 1877.
J. F. BULLITT .

§ 4 [1]. Thereshallbe butoneformofaction.
§ 5 [3]. Actionsareordinaryor equitable.
§ 6 [4,5]. Unlessotherwiseprovidedby thisCode orotherstatute-
1.Actions of which courtsof chanceryhad jurisdictionbefore
the firstday of August, 1851, may be equitable; and actionsof
which such jurisdictionwas exclusivemust be equitable.
2. All other actionsmust be ordinary.
§7 [6]. An ordinaryor equitableactionmay be broughtupon a bill
of exchange, or a note or otherobligation, or upon an indorsementor
assignmentthereof,which,or upon a judgment the recordof which, is
lost,destroyed,mutilated,ordefaced,withoutfraudon the partof the
plaintiff, or of those under whom he claims. But no action shallbe
brought upon an instrumenttransferableby delivery, merely,which is
allegedto be lost,destroyed, mutilated,or defaced,withouta previous
tenderby theplaintiffto the defendant, ifhisname and placeof resi-
dence be known to the plaintiff, of an indemnifyingbond, with good
surety; nor shalljudgment be givenagainstthedefendant,insuch action,
untilsuchbond isgiven,withgood suretyapprovedby thecourt.
§8 [7]. An errorof theplaintiffastotheformof actionshallbe cause,
not for the abatement or dismissalof it, but merely fora change into
the proper proceedingsby an amendment of the pleadingsand a transfer
of the actionto the proper docket.
§9 [8]. The errormentionedinthelastsectionmay be correctedby the
plaintiffwithoutmotion, at any time beforethe defendantanswers, or
afterwardby leaveof court.
§ 10 [9 ]. 1.The defendant,by motionmade when he answers,may have
an equitableactiontransferredto the ordinarydocket, if, according
to the provisionsof section6 , it should have been an ordinaryac-
tion, and ifthe answer presenta defenseof which he isentitledto a
jury-trial.
2. The defendant,by motion made when or before he answers,
may have an ordinaryactiontransferredto the equitydocket, if,
accordingto the provisionsof section6, itshouldhave been an
equitable action.
3. The courtmay , withoutmotion,orderthetransferof an action
from one docket to another ifeitherparty be entitledto such trans-
fer; or may trythe action, or renderjudgment thereinby default,
unlessa party entitledto a transfermove therefor.
§ II [10]. In an ordinaryactionproperlycommenced assuch-
1. If there be severalissuesallof which were, before the first
day of August , 1851, cognizablein chancery, though none were

§ 12. In an equitableaction, properlycommenced as such, either
partymay , by motion, have the casetransferredto the ordinarydocket
forthe trialof any issueconcerningwhich he isentitledtoa jury-trial;
but eitherparty may requireevery equitableissueto be disposedof

§ 13. In countieswhereinthe jurisdictionof circuitcourtsisvested
in different courts-
1. The provisionsof thistitle, concerning transfersto the proper
docket, apply to transfersto the proper court.
2. Upon an order for the transferof an actionfrom one court to
another, the papersin the actionand a copy of the ordershallbe
deliveredby the clerk of the court which makes the order to the
clerkof the court to which the action is transferred; and it shall
proceed as ifithad been brought in the lattercourt.
§ 14 [11]. In an ordinaryaction,properlycommencedassuch,ifthe
defendantbe entitledto the transferof one ormore issuesto theequity
docket,and move forsuchtransfer,theplaintiffmay filehisaffidavitthat
he verilybelieveshe willsucceed in the action, and thatthe collectionof
hisclaim,afterjudgment, willbe endangeredby delayarisingfrom such
transfer; and ifsuch affidavitbe filed, the court shallnot grant the
motion of the defendantto transfer, except upon conditionthat the
defendantexecutea bond,withgood surety,approvedby thecourt, that
he willpay any judgment renderedin favorof the plaintiff: Provided,
That no such bond shallbe required ifthe trialof the case or issue
transferredtake placeduringthe term at which the transferismade ,
nor untilallissuesnot transferredare triedor disposedof in favorof
the plaintiff.
§ 15[12]. An errorastothekindofproceedingsadoptedintheaction

§ 16 [13]. The provisionsof thisCode applytoallcivilactions,unless
the contraryappears.
§ 17 [14]. A judgment obtainedin an ordinaryaction,shallnot be
annullednor modified by any orderin an equitableaction, except for a
defensewhich arisesor isdiscoveredafterrenditionof the judgment.
But such judgment does not preventthe recoveryof any claimwhich
was not, thoughitmight have been, usedas a defenseby way of set-off
or counter -claim in the action .

I.] 5PARTIES TO CIVIL ACTIONS .
§ 21 [33]. A personalrepresentative,guardian,curator,committeeofa
person of unsound mind ,trusteeof an express trust,a person with whom
or in whose name a contractis made for the benefitof another, a re-
ceiver appointed by a court, the assignee of a bankrupt, or a person
expresslyauthorizedby statuteto do so,may bringan actionwithout
joiningwithhim thepersonforwhose benefititisprosecuted.
§ 22 [34]. All personshaving an interestin the subjectof an action
and inobtainingthe reliefdemanded may be joinedas plaintiffs,unless
itisotherwiseprovided in thisCode .
§23 [35]. Any personmay be made a defendantwho claimsan interest
inthe controversyadverseto the plaintiff, or who is a necessaryparty to
a complete determinationof the questioninvolvedin the action.
*§ 24 [36]. Partieswho areunitedininterestmust be joinedasplaintiffs
or defendants; but ifthe consent of one who should be joined as plain-
tiffcannot be obtained, he may be made a defendant, the reasonbeing
statedin the petition.
§ 25 [37]. Ifthequestioninvolvea common or generalinterestof
many persons, or ifthe partiesbe numerous and it is impracticableto
bring allof them beforethe courtwithin a reasonabletime, one or more
may sue or defend forthe benefitof all.
§ 26 [38]. Personsseverallyliableupon the same contract,and par-
tiesto billsof exchange,to promissorynotesplacedupon the footing
of billsof exchange , or to common orders and checks, and suretieson
the same or separateinstruments, may all, or any of them , or the repre-
sentativesof such as may have died,be includedin the same actionat
the plaintiff'soption.
§ 27 [39]. Iftwo or more personsbe jointlybound by contract,the
actionthereonmay be brought againstall, or any of them , at the plaint-
iff'soption. If any of the persons so bound be dead, the action may
be brought againstany or allof the survivorswith the representativesof
allor any of the decedents, or againstthe latteror any of them . If all
the persons so bound be dead , the action may be brought againstthe
representativesof allorof any of them. An actionor judgment against
any one or more of severalpersons jointlybound shallnot be a bar to
proceedings againstthe others.
§ 28 [40,400]. The courtmay determineany controversybetween
partiesbeforeit, ifitcan do so withoutprejudicetoothers; ifitcannot
do so, itmust requiresuchotherpersonsto be made parties, or must
dismisstheactionwithoutprejudice.
§ 29 [41,257]. In an actionorproceedingforthe recoveryof realor
personalproperty,or forthe subjectionthereofto a demand of the plaint-
iffunder an attachment or other lien, any person claiming a right to,

§ 33 [45]. An actionto recoverthe possessionof specificpersonal
propertytaken under a distresswarrant, ifitbe broughtby thetenant,
orhisassigneeor under-tenant, may be againstthe partywho sued out
thewarrant; and the propertyclaimedin such actionmay , under the

holdingit, shallbe a sufficienttakingof the propertyto sustainthe
actionagainstthepartywho sued out thewarrant.

§ 34 [49 to52]. 1. In actionsbetween husband and wife; inactions
concerningher separateproperty; and, in actionsconcerningher
generalpropertyin which he refusesto unite, she may sue or be
sued alone .
2. In allother actionsby or againsta wife she and her husband

§ 317[347]. When thejuryhasbeensworn,thetrialshallproceedin
thefollowingorder,unlessthecourt,forspecialreasons,otherwisedirect:
1. The plaintiffmust brieflystatehisclaimand the evidenceby
which he expects to sustainit.
2. The defendant must then brieflystatehisdefenseand the evi-
dence he expects to offerin support of it.
3. The party on whom reststhe burthen of proof, in the whole
action,must firstproduce hisevidence; theadversepartywillthen
produce his evidence.
4. The partieswillthen be confined to rebuttingevidence, unless
the court, forgood reasons in furtheranceof justice,permit them to
offer evidence in chief.
5. When the evidence isconcluded,but before the argument to
the jury, eitherparty may require the court to directthe jury to
finda separate-generalverdictwith the generalverdict, or to finda
specialverdict. If a specialverdictbe so required,the questions
of law may be reservedby the court untilafterverdict; but, ifa
general verdictbe required, eitherparty may ask written instruc-
tionsto the juryon pointsof law, which shallbe given or refused
by the court before the commencement of the argument to the
jury.
6. The partiesmay then submitor argue the caseto thejury.
In the argument ,the party having the burthen of proof shallhave
the conclusionand the adversepartythe opening. If therebe
more than one speechon eitherside,orifseveraldefendantshaving
separatedefensesappearby differentcounsel,thecourtshallarrange
the relativeorderof argument.

SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS .
a . A motion pursuantto 648,upon a bond given pursuantto 645,isa special
proceeding. Watson vs.Gabby, 18 B. M. , 662. So is a bastardy-case. Chand-
lervs. Commonwealth , 4 Met ., 69 ; Francisvs. Commonwealth, 3 Bush , 4.
b. A provisionalremedy,such as an attachment or injunction, isnot a cause of action;
but ismerely an incidentto the actionin which itisobtained. Moore'sadm'r vs.
Sheppard, I Met ., 97.
c. Observe, thatsubsection34 of 732 of thisCode declaresthatitsprovisionscon-
cerningactionsshall,so faras applicable,embracespecialproceedings- a decla-
rationnot contained in the Code of 1854.
a . The Code has not changed the law concerning facts which constitutea cause of action.
Hill vs.Barret, 14 B. M. ,85 ; Richmond & Lex . T. P. Co. vs.Rogers, 7 Bush , 535.
ACTIONS AT LAW AND IN EQUITY .
a . The Code makes no distinctionbetween pleadingsin ordinaryactionsand plead-
ings in equitableactions, except that which is made by IHO as to captionsof
petitions.
b. That the Code has not abolishedthe old distinctionsbetween legaland equitable
causes of action- between actions at law and suitsin chancery- except as to
forms, isillustratedby theopinioninBrookovervs.Hurst, I Met.,665; inwhich
itwas said that, though a party cannot, in an equitableaction, recoverupon a
fraudulent contract, whether it be executory or executed; and cannot, in an
ordinary action, recover upon an executory contract, if it be fraudulent; he
may , in an ordinaryaction, recoverupon an executed contract, although fraudu-
lent. See, to same effect, Bibb vs.Bibb, 17 B. M. , 307.
c. Petitionin equityis proper to establisha right to a streetdedicated to public
use . Trusteesof Lebanon vs. Forrest, 15 B. M. , 168.
ACTIONS ON LOST WRITINGS .
a. Thissectionappliestobank-notes. CommercialBank vs.Benedict,& c.,18В. М. ,
307 .
d. The Code of 1854, § 6,declared that " an actionupon a writtenobligationwhich
has been lostordestroyedmay be prosecutedby ordinaryproceedings," without
requiringthe plaintiffto executean indemnifyingbond. Formerly, itwas the
practiceof courtsof chancery to requireindemnifying bonds in many such cases;
and in the case of Berry vs.Berry'sex'r,6 Bush ,594, which was an actionin equity
upon a lien-note,itwas held that,thoughthe note" had improperlyfalleninto
thehands of" a non-residentdefendant(againstwhom no personaljudgment could
be rendered), the plaintiffmust give a bond of indemnity.

2 CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SECS . 8 , 11 .
28 . ERROR AS TO FORM OF ACTION .
a. An error of the plaintiff, by describing, in the caption, a petition,which states
only a legal cause of action, as a " petitionin equity," is not ground fordemur-
rer; but only authorizesthe defendant to move for a transferof the action to the
ordinarydocket. Trustees,& c.,vs.Forest,& c., 15 B. М. , 171.
b. Accordingtoa dictumin Lansdalevs.Mitchel, 14 B. M. ,348,a petitioninequity,
which statesonly a legalcause of action, should not be dismissed, but the pro-
ceedings should be " changed by a transferof the action to the proper docket."
A decision,however , has gone further, and held that, in such a case, judgment
should be rendered for the plaintiff, without a transferof the action, or an
amendment of the pleadings. Robinson, & c., vs. West,& c., 14 B. M. , 3.
But , in an ordinaryaction, claiming only a legal right, which the defendant con-
troverted,and which the plaintifffailedto sustainby proof,itwas heldthatthe
court, to which the law and factswere submitted by consent, erred in rendering
judgment fortheplaintiff,upon proofshowingthatshewas equitablyentitledto
the property in controversy; and itwas said: " In such case, afteranswer filed,
unless the plaintiff, by leave of court, amends hispetition, so as therein to pre-
sent a case of equitablejurisdiction, and thus give the defendant notice of the
true nature and ground of the claim and action, the court should render judg-
ment in favor of the defendant. Garretvs. Gault, 13 B. M. , 378 ; citing 6 of
Code of 1851, which issubstantiallythe same as 28 of thisCode .
In a subsequent case,however ,itwas held that,ifthe plaintiffin an ordinaryaction
shared a rightto equitablerelief, and the case be triedby the courtbelow as an
equity-case, without objectionby eitherparty, the Court of Appeals willso con-
siderand decide it. Bates vs. Culver, 17 B. M. , 167.
II note 1 . TRANSFER OF ACTIONS .
Under 9 of the Code of 1851, and 10 of the Code of 1854, which are identical,
and forwhich & II of the presentCode issubstituted, itwas held-
(1) That ,in an ordinaryaction-
a. The defendant can have no right to a transferof the case, unless his answer
present a validequitabledefense. Jones vs. Letcher, 13 В. М. , 365 .
b. If the answerstatean equitabledefense,itmust be disposedof beforetheplaint-
iffcan have judgment ; and the defendant is entitledto a transferto the equity-
docket . Bosleyvs.Mattingly, 14 B. M. , 89 ; Hackett, vs.Schad,3 Bush , 353 .
c. If the answer statean equitableand a legaldefense, and the former be adjudged
insufficient,the lattershouldbe triedby a jury,unlessa jury-trialbe waived.
Smith vs. Moberly, 15 B. M . ,.70. Both issuesmust be disposed of, before the
plaintiffcan have judgment . The equitableissue should be submitted to the
jury for a specialverdict, if therebe no motion to transferitto the equity-docket.
Pettyvs.Malier, 15 B. M. , 591.
But, in Bennet, & c., vs. Titherington,& c., 6 Bush, 192, the defendants, having
pleaded an equitable and a legal defense, moved to transfer, not merely the
equitableissue,but thecase, tothe equity-docket. The motionwas overruled;
and,aftera jury-trialastothelegalissue, and a verdictfortheplaintiff, a judg-
ment was renderedagainstthedefendants,which seemed todisregardtheirequi-
tabledefense. Upon theirappeal,the judgment was affirmed,apparentlyupon
the grounds- 1. That the defendants did not request a postponement , for fur-
therpreparationas to theirequitabledefense. 2. That itmust be presumed that
the courtbelow passedupon the whole casepresentedby the pleadingsand
evidence. 3. That, accordingto the evidencein the case, the judgment was
right.

CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SECS . II, 15, 17. 3
(2)
d . In an action fordamages , in which the defendant not only traversesthe plaint-
iff'sallegation,but statesan equitableset-offagainstthe plaintiff, the plaintiff
is entitledto a trialof the legalissue; but a judgment in his favor should be
suspended untilthe equitableissue is determined . Geoghegan, & c., vs. Ditto,
& c., 2 Met ., 433 .
e. Partialfailureof the considerationof a note is an equitabledefense, which ,
before the Code , could not have been pleaded in an actionat law . Hackettvs.
Schad, 3 Bush , 353.
f. In an actionby the receiverof A's estateagainsta sheriff,forfailingto collecta
debt from A's administrator, a plea of the defendant, that the administratorhad
paid certaindebts to creditorsof A , forwhose benefit, in part, the receiverwas
directed to collectthe debt in question, is an equitabledefense pro tanto. Com-
monwealth for Petersvs. Basley,& c., 5 Bush , 221.
a. That, in an equitableaction, forpartitionof land, to an undividedinterestin
which the plaintiffsasserta legaltitle; and the whole of which isclaimed ad-
verselyby the defendant, or by part of the defendants- thus presenting" strictly
a legalissue," no motion being made to transferthe case to the ordinarydocket,
the courtshouldnotdismissthepetition,but shoulddecidetheissue(Sale,& c.,
vs.Crutchfield,& c.,8 Bush,644); orshouldcausea juryto tryit. Frayer,& c.,
vs.Naylor, & c., I Met ., 593.

EQUITABLE DEFENSE TO ORDINARY ACTION .
a. See sections378,518, 520, and notes thereto.
b. Before the adoption of the Code , a defendant in an ordinaryaction could not
plead an equitabledefensethereto,but was obligedtogo intoequityforrelief.
151 of the Code of 1851 (which isthe same as 125 of the Code of 1854) per-
mitted the pleading of equitabledefenses to ordinary actions, but no provision
of the Code of 1851 requiredequitabledefenses to be so pleaded. Dorsey vs.
Ruse , 14 B. M. , 157. But, under & 14 of the Code of 1854 (which is substan-
tiallythe same as 17of thisCode) a defendantcannotavailhimselfof an equi-
table defense to an ordinary action, except by pleading it therein (Chinn vs.
Mitchell,2 Met ., 92 ; Hackett vs.Schad, 3 Bush , 355 ; Emmerson's adm'r vs.Herri-
ford, 8 Id., 235; Thomassonvs. Townsend, 10 Bush, 114); unlessthe defense
arise,or be discovered,afterrenditionof thejudgment; as,thatthe plaintiff,
afterobtainingthe judgment, has become a non-resident(Moss vs.Rowland's
ex'r, 1 Duv ., 321); or insolvent(Keilyvs.Kelly, 2 Duv ., 363). The reportof
Kellyvs.Kellyfailstoshow whethertheplaintiffwas insolventwhen he obtained
thejudgment,orbecame soafterward; but itseems clearthatthedecisioncan-
notbe maintained,exceptupon theground thattheinsolvencyaroseorwas dis-
covered afterward .
c. A voidjudgment may be enjoined. Knottvs.Jarboe, IMet.,504.
RECOVERY OF USURY PAID ON JUDGMENT .
a.Though adefendant,againstwhom ajudgmentembracingusuryhasbeen rendered,
cannotobtaina modificationof the judgment, exceptupon theground of a sub-
sequentdefense(1 Duv .,321),he may ,aftersatisfyingthejudgment,recover,by
an equitableaction,the amount of the usury. Chinn vs.Mitchell,2 Met.,92;
Ross vs.Ross, 3 Id.,274 ; Scottvs.Shropshire, 2 Duv ., 153.
In Ross vs.Ross,above cited,the courtsaid: " It has been repeatedlysettledby
this courtthat usury, paid upon a judgment at law, cannot be recoveredback at

law,becausejudgment cannotbe renderedagainstjudgment ina courtof law.
Pearce vs.Hedrick, 3 Littell, 109; Thompson vs. Ware, 8 B. M. , 28. And it
has alsobeen adjudgedthatusurypaid upon a decreein equitycannotbe re-
covered back in equity, becausedecreewillnot be renderedagainstdecree.
Thompson vs. Ware, supra, and Moran vs. Woodyard, & B. M. , 538. But it
has neverbeen heldthatusurypaidupon a judgment atlaw cannotbe recovered
back in equity."
In Scottvs.Shropshire, above cited,the usurywas collectedinan equitableaction,
which was broughtupon judgmentsatlaw,afterreturnsof nullabonaupon exe-
cutionsthereon,and inwhich " the courtrenderedformaljudgments againfor
the depts,as well as forthe saleof " certainland of the defendant. Itwas held,
however,thatthe casescitedin Ross vs.Ross had no applicationto Scottvs.
Shropshire,because" the proceedinginequitywas merelyinaidof the common
law judgments , and for the solepurpose of having them satisfied."
BY WHOM ACTION TO BE BROUGHT GENERALLY .
a . Actions by assigneesof chosesin action., It has been decided by the Court of
Appeals in severalcases, that the rule of the common law , which allowed the
legal owner of a chose in action to sue upon it for the use of the beneficial
owner , was abolished by the Code . According to thosedecisions, if the owner
of a chose in action, which is not legallyassignable- such as an unwritten
agreementto pay money (Lytlevs.Lytle,2 Met., 127),ora covenanttopay rents
and make improvements(Hicks& Gillvs.Doty,4 Bush,420)-should make an
equitableassignment of it; or, if the payee of a note, the legal titleto which
he can assignby indorsementand delivery, shouldassignitby deliveryonly
(Carpentervs.Miles,17 B. M. , 598 ; Gillvs.Johnson'sadm'r, I Met .,649 ; Perryvs.
Seitz,2 Duv .,124,)-the assignorand assigneeshouldjoin inthe action; or,if
the assignorrefusetodo so, theassigneemay sue inequity,making the assignor
a defendant .
And itwas held that, if itappear that a plaintiff, who claims to be the assigneeof
a note,has no rightto it, legal or equitable,he cannot maintain an actionupon
it, though consent theretobe given by a defendant who isentitledto the note.
Worthington vs. Greer,& c., 17 В. М. , 741.
In an action by the assignee of a note, the payee is not a necessaryparty upon
a plea of usury, or of payment to the payee . True vs. Triplett, 4 Met ., 58.
b. Actionsby one for theuse of another. In Fenwick vs. Phillips, 3 Met ., 87, the court
said: " Ithaving been statedintheoriginalpetition,thatthesuitwas brought
forthe benefitof Phillips, he was thereby virtuallymade a party to the action,
and had a right, by an amended petition,to assume the attitudeof the real
plaintiffinthe action; consequently, thedefendant'sdemurrerto thepetitionas
amended was properlyoverruled."
And in Neely vs.Merritt, & c., 9 Bush , 346, which was an action by the Common-
wealth for the use of several persons, the Court, afterreferringto Fenwickvs.
Phillips, said: " The petitionin thiscasenot only statesthe names of those
forwhose benefitthe reliefis asked, but these names are also fullysetout in .
the caption. They arevirtuallypartiesplaintiff, and thetechnicalobjectionthat
theydo not sostylethemselvescannotprevailupon demurrer."
And in Commonwealth for Harris vs. Teal, & c., 14 B. M. , 29, itwas held that,
under 60 of the Code of 1851(which isthe same as ? 21 of the presentCode ,
with referencetothequestionunderconsideration),an actionupon a constable's
bond ,forofficialdefalcation,may be broughtinthename of theCommonwealth
forthe use of the person injured.

3. STATEMENTS OF LEGAL CONCLUSIONS ARE IMMATERIAL , EXCEPT IN CERTAIN CASES
AFTER A TRIAL .
a .An allegation,in an actionagainstguarantors,that,by the termsof theguar-
anty,itspublicationand delivery,the defendantswaived noticeof itsacceptance,
is immaterial, being an allegation, not of any fact, but of a legal conclusion.

b. An allegation,in an actionforthe breach of a covenant to furnisha slavewinter

c. An allegation, in an action by a cityto enforcea lienfortaxes, that the defend-

sheriff, such concurrence being necessary to prevent the plaintifffrom voting.
Caulfieldvs.Bullock, 18 B. M. , 494. (Quere.)

ment of a legalconclusionwhich need not be denied. Larue vs.Hays ,7 Bush ,53.

son,4 J. J. Marshall,649 ; and Currievs.Fowler, 5 Id.,145. Those cases, however ,

Waring's heirs, 18 B. M. , 82, the court said: " The plaintiffshaving, however ,
allegedin theirpetitionthat they were his[Waring's] heirs, and having sued in
thatcharacter,and the factthattheywere hisheirsnothaving beendeniedby
the defendants in theiranswer, the allegationhad to be taken as true, and itwas
unnecessarytoproveit." And Mr. Chitty,illustratingtherulethat" where the

20 CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SEC . 90 .
law presumes a fact,itneed not be statedin pleading," says: " So, ifa person
plead thathe isheir toA ,he need not say eitherthatA isdead ,or thathe had no
son." Chitty'sPl., sidepage, 253 ; American edition, 1837. And Mr. Chitty
gives severalillustrations, showing that the common law was liberal'inallowing
latitudeto avoid prolixity. 1 Ch . Pi., sidepages262,333, 567. There seems to
be reason for doubt , whether the Code has so changed the common law as to
requirea lawyer to statein a pleading " the genealogy of the patriarchs."
f. In an actionagainsttheindorserofa note,payablein Illinois,an allegationthat,
by the law of Illinois, the defendant was indebted to the plaintiffin the amount
of the note,was held to be inmaterial: the plaintiffshould have statedthe law
of Illinois, as a fact. Roots vs.Merriwether,& Bush , 397.
*
*
g . Our Constitutiondeclares that sheriffsshallbe electedin August , and " enter
upon the duties of their respectiveoffices on the firstMonday in
January next succeeding their election." (Art. 6, sec. 4.) And the Revised
Statutesdeclared that, if the officialbond of a sheriff" is not given within a
month of the time when his appointment ought to take effect, the
officeshallbe consideredvacant." (Ch.71, 12.) Suretiesof a sheriff, who
executedbond, February11, 1867,claimingthatthe bond was void,becauseit
was not executed " within a month of the time " required by law, alleged that
the sheriff'selection" ought to have gone into effecton the firstMonday of
January, 1867," & c.: held, that the allegationwas immaterial; and that the
plaintiffsshould have allegedthat the electionwas held in August , 1866, if they
reliedon thatfact. Commonwealth vs. Cook, 8 Bush , 220.
h. In an actionon a note by one of two payees,hisallegation, thathe was the
legalowner of the note, was held to be immaterial: he should have alleged an
assignment from his co-payee . Quisenberryvs.Artis, I Duv .,30 .
i. Allegationsthat the plaintiffpurchased an entry of land,and holds the equitable
title, without alleginga deed or assignment to him , are immaterial. Craig vs.
Horine , Bibb , 113 .
j. Itwas said,however,in Fritzvs.Tudor,2 Duv .,174: " But,asadjudgedinNew
York , in Corwin vs. Corwin, 9 Bart ., 219, under a Code just like ours in this
particular,a simpleallegationof title,untraversedby the answer,may be con-
sidered an admitted fact ."
k . A person having signed a note, as trusteeforanother,itwas held that the ques-
tion, whether he meant to bind himself, or the trustproperty, was one of law,
tobe decidedby thecourt,inviewof thelanguageof thenote,thecircumstances
under which itwas executed, and the situationof the parties; and that an alle-
gation, as to the intentionwith which it was executed,was an allegationof a
legalimplication,of which no denialwas necessary. Wells vs.Lewis,4 Met .,355-
A petitionwas held tostatea causeof action,althoughiterroneouslydescribed
a writing, on which the action was founded , as a note. Bradley vs.Mason , 6
Bush , 602 .
m . And ,even under theold practice,a partywas heldto be entitledtothebenefit
of thefacts,notwithstandinghis erroneousstatement, againsthimself,of a legal
conclusion. Trimblesvs. Harrison, I B. M. , 142 ; Oldhams vs.Jones, 5 Id.,462.
n. But,inan actionundera statute,givingdamages incertaincasesforcausingthe
deathofa person" by willfulneglect," itwas heldtobe sufficientfortheplaint-
iffto allegethatthe death was causedby the defendant's" willfulneglect,"
withoutstatingfactsshowingtheneglect. Chilesvs.Drake, 2 Met., 146.
0. If,however,a pleading,thoughitdo not statefactsconstitutinga causeofaction,
or a defense,statelegalconclusionsembracingsuch facts, the defectmay be
curedby a trial,and a verdictforthe partyso pleading; nor shouldhe, ifthe

CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SEC . 90 . 21
judgment be againsthim,be precludedfrom reversingitforerroneousinstruc-
tions. Bently vs. Bustard, 16 B. M. , 690-91 ; Skillman vs. Muir , 4 Met ., 283 ;
Barlow vs.Wiley, 3 A. K. Mar. , 460 .
4. FACTS CONSTITUTING A CAUSE OF ACTION , AND NOT EVIDENCE OF THE FACTS ,
MUST BE STATED .
a. Beforethe adoptionof the Code,itwas allowable,and usual,forplaintiffs,in
billsin chancery,though not in declarationsat law, to statethe evidence on which
they relied,as well as the factsconstitutingtheircausesof action. The Code
of 1854 did not,as ? 119 of thisCode does, forbidthe statement in a pleading of
" the evidence reliedon by a party."
But itneverwas sufficientfora partyto statein hispleadingmerelythe evidence
relied on to maintain his action or defense (Plummer 2s. Newdigate, 2 Duv .,
1 ; Haggard vs.Hays' adm'r, 13 B. M. , 176;Jonesvs.Bunn , 2 Met.,492; Muh-
ling vs. Sattler, 3 Met ., 285 ; Alexander & Lancashirevs. Quigley, 2 Duv ., 405);
unless,at any rate, the evidencestatedraisea conclusivepresumptionof a fact
entitlingthe party to relief. Gregory vs.McFarland , I Duv ., 59 ; Williams vs.
Gordon , II Bush ; see,however , Lewis vs. Quinker, 2 Met ., 284.
5. STATEMENT OF A PRIMA FACIE CAUSE OF ACTION IS GENERALLY SUFFICIENT .
a. In an actionforthe value of property convertedby the defendant, itis sufficient
toallegethatthepropertywas forciblytakenfrom theplaintiff,withoutalleging
thathe owned it,as possessionimpliestitleprimafacie. Eversolevs.Moore,3
Bush , 51 .
b. In an action by a suretyagainst the payee of a note forreliefon account of
indulgence to the principal, the defendant'sknowledge of the suretyshipmust be
presumed , and need not be alleged. Champion vs.Robertson, 4 Bush , 17.
c. In an actionfor an injurycaused by the defendant'snegligence,the petitionneed
not allegethat the plaintiffwas not negligent. Louisvilleand Portland Canal Co.
vs. Murphy , 9 Bush , 529 .
d. In an action fordower ,allegationsthat the plaintiff'shusband was legallyseized,
and sold the land without her consent, and that plaintiffisentitledto one third
forlife,& c., were held to be sufficient, though she failedto allegethat she was
the vendor's wife at the time of the sale. Fritzvs. Tudor, 2 Duv ., 174. Quere,
and see Yancy vs. Smith, 2 Met ., 411 .
e. In an action by a husband's creditor, to subject a wife's chose in action, the
plaintiffneed not allegethat the wife is not entitledto an equitablesettlement,
thatbeing matter of defense. Tobin vs.Dixon and wife, 2 Met ., 424. But see
Elliottvs. Waring , 5 Mon. , 340, 341.
f . In an action against the trusteesof a town , upon theirsubscriptionof stock,
which the Legislaturehad authorizedthem to make , with the consent of theciti-
zens, an allegationthat the citizenshad given such consent was held to be
unnecessary. ShelbyvilleTrusteesvs.Shelbyville, & c., Turnpike Co., I Met ., 54.
g. Though the payorand payeeof a notehave thesame name, the plaintiff,being
an assigneeof the note,need not allegethattheyare differentpersons. The
legalpresumptionisinfavorof thevalidityof thecontract. The factthatthey
are the same person ismatter of defense. Coopervs.Poston, I Duv ., 92.
h. A petitionin thename of A. J. Morrison& Co.,upon a billofexchangepayable
to A. J.Morrison& Co.,was held to be good on demurrer. If the firmem-
braced more than one person,the defendant should have shown it; and might then
have compelled an amendment by motion . Morrison& Co. vs. Tate, 1 Met ., 569.
i. It is settled, unless as to actionsfounded upon statutes, that limitationismatter
of defense; and thatthe plaintiffneed notshow,inhispetition,thathisaction
is not barred ..

22 CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SEC . 90 ..
A demurrer to a petition, upon the ground that itdisclosesa bar to the action,by
limitation, cannot be sustained, unless the petitionshow not only a sufficient
lapseof time, but, also, " the non -existenceof any ground of avoidance, which
a plaintiffisnot apt ever to do ." Rankin vs. Turney, 2 Bush , 555.
And in an action fordamages , caused by the killingof a person, brought under a
statute which declared that " the actions under this act shall be commenced
within one year aftersuch death," itwas held that the petitionneed not show
that the killingwas within one year before the commencement of the action.
Chiles vs. Drake , 2 Met ., 148 .
But , in an actionbrought under the act of 1856, concerning fraudulentpreferences
by debtors, the court, afterstating that the act required such actions to be
brought " within sixmonths afterthe recordingof such transfer, or the delivery
of the property or effectstransferred," said: " This fact, being essentialto the
existenceto the right[of action], must be made to appear, eitherby a direct
allegationon thesubjectinthepetition, orby statingthereinthetimethetrans-
ferwas recorded, or the salemade and the property delivered; so that, by refer-
ence to that statement, and the time the petitionwas filed,it shallappear to
have been filedwithin the time limited." Wintersmith& Young vs.Poynter&
Conway , 2 Met .,460. This, however , as the court said in Storyvs.Graham , 4
Met.,322,was a dictum; " becausethe petitionnot onlyfailedtoshow thatthe
conveyancewas made withinsixmonths,but distinctlyshowed thatitwas made
more than six months before the commencement of the action; and , conse-
quently, thatthe plaintiffhad no rightto maintainthe action." See subdi-
vision(9) of note 7 to 90, page 23 .
6. ALLEGATIONS CONCERNING NOTICE , DEMAND , READINESS , AND TENDER .
(I) Notice.
a . A petitionagainstguarantors allegedthat the plaintiffaccepted and acted upon
the guaranty, " of all which the defendants had due notice:" held, that the
averment of noticewas insufficient, and that the allegationshould be specific,
and such as would enable the court to determine from the statements of the
petitionwhether or not such noticewas given as the law requires. Steadman vs.
Guthrie, 4 Met ., 159. See 14 B. M. , 191 ; 13 B. M. , 383 .
b. In an actionagainstthe creatorof a nuisance, the plaintiffneed not allegenotice
to desistfrom the wrong , nor a request to repairthe injury: aliter, as to a con.
tinuerof the nuisance. Ray us. Sellers, I Duv ., 254.
(2) Demand .
c. It was held that an actioncould not be maintained against a sheriffor constable,
for money collected, without a previous demand . Huston vs. Hagar , I Duv .,
24. But an allegationthat he had collectedthe money , and refusedto pay itto
the plaintiff, " although the same had been demanded of him," is sufficient.
Harris vs.Perry, 2 Bush , 101.
d. Petitionagainstan attorney, formoney collected, must aver a demand . Roberts
vs.Armstrong'sadm'r, I Bush , 263 .
e. It was held that an executor could, without averring a demand , maintain an
action against the testator'swidow for the value of slaves of the testator, the
possessionof which she had held after the testator'sdeath, and parted with
before the commencement of the action. Easley'sex'rvs.Easley, 18 В. М. , 92 .
And see Divine vs. Bullock, 3 Met ., 418 .
(3) Readiness.
f . In an actionupon a contractwhich contains mutual and dependent agreements,
the petitionmust statethe plaintiff'sreadinessto perform his part of the con-
tractat the requiredtime and place. Souselyvs.Burns ' adm'r, 10 Bush ,87.

CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SEC . 90 . 23
(4) Tender.
g . In an action by a woman for an alleged breach of a contract to marry her, the
petitionwas heldtobe defectivebecauseitdid notaverthatshehad " proposed
and made the offerto fixthe time and place and fulfillthe engagement ." Fible
vs.Caplinger, 13 B. M. ,464. And seenote3e to?96,subs.2.
7 . STATEMENT OF CAUSE OF ACTION .
(I) In actionsfor injurytocharacter.
a . In an actionfor slander, the specificwords in which the slanderisconveyed must
be statedin the petition. It is not sufficientto allegemerely the effectof the
words , or that the defendant charged the plaintiffwith the commission of a
particularcrime. Taylorvs.Moran , 4 Met ., 137.
b. The words , " he , the defendant, had had sexualintercoursewith the plaintiffat
diversdifferenttimes," are actionable; and the plaintiffneed not allegeor prove
specialdamage . Adams vs.Rankin , I Duv ., 58.
aa . The petitionmust allegemalice. It isnot sufficientto allegethat the defendant
falselychargedthe plaintiffwith being " a horse-thief." The wordsmay have
been spoken in jest, or upon an occasionwhich would rebut the presumption of
malice . Williams vs. Gordon , II Bush .
bb. A judgment , upon a verdictbased on presumed lossof characterand time, was
affirmed, though therewas neitherallegationnor proof of specialdamage result-
ing from eithercause. Hill and Bergen vs.Harris,4 Bush , 450. [Quere.]
(2) In actionsfor injurytotheperson.
c. It isnot sufficientto allegethat an injurycomplainedof was causedby the
defendant'swillfulneglect: some factmust be statedshowingtheneglect. Lou-
isville& PortlandCanal Co. vs.Murphy's adm'r, 9 Bush , 522. But evidenceof the
factsneed not [and now must not, Code ? 119], be stated. It issufficientto allege
thatplaintiff'sintestatewas killedby the defendant'semployes,in carelesslyand
negligentlycausing a trainof cars to be run over him . Louisville, Cin. & Lex .
R. R. Co. vs.Case'sadm'r,9 Bush , 731.
(3) In otheractionsfor tort.
d. Allegations that defendant extorted and obtained from plaintiff$ 500, without
any consideration, and isthereforeindebted to plaintiffin that sum , do not state
a cause of action. Murphy vs.Estes, 6 Bush , 532.
(4) In actionson return of nulla bona.
e. In an actionagainstA , B , and C , upon a returnof nulla bona as to A , the peti
tion,afterstatingthe amount of the plaintiff'sjudgment againstA , allegedthat
B and C were indebted to A " in an amount sufficientto satisfysaid judgment ;
and praying that they might be required to discoverany debt due to saidA by
them severally," and for judgment againstthem " severally:" held, that the
petitiondid not authorizea judgment by defaultagainstB forthe amount of the
judgment againstA , nor,itseems,forany amount. Seatonvs.Warner,9 Bush,
217. See 5 J. J. Mar. , 641 .
(5) In actionsbyvendorof land to enforcelienfor purchase-money.
f . In an actiontoenforcea vendor'slienforthe purchase-money of land, thepeti-
tionmust statethat the vendor has complied with his contract, or that he isable
and willingto do so. Williamsvs.Abrahams , 3 Bush , 186.
(6) In actionstorescindcontractconcerningsaleof land.
g . It isnot sufficientto allege, in a petitionfor the rescissionof a contractfor the
saleof land,that the defendant never had a good and perfect title, and that the
titleisdefective. Davis vs. Dycus, 7 Bush , 4 .

24 CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SEC . 90 .
(7) In actionsupon impliedcontracts.
h . " Under the old practice, in an action of debt for the price of goods sold and
delivered, itwas not necessaryto aver a promise to pay the sum claimed (I Chit-
ty'sPl.,362); and,although,inan actionof indebitatusassumpsitforthepriceof
goods soldand delivered,itwas necessaryto avera promiseto pay,itwas not
necessaryto prove such promise; becauseproofof the saleand deliveryof
thegoods,and of theirvalue,raiseda legalimplicationof a promiseto pay the
value, though no pricehad been agreed upon ." Snodgrassvs.Broadwell, 2 Litt.,
353; Jenkinsvs.Richardson, 6 J. J. Mar. , 441 .
" Under the Code of Practice, which requiresonly a statement of the facts
constitutingthecauseof action,what the law impliesneed notbe averred(secs.
115, 144); and a petition, alleginga saleand deliveryto the defendant of goods
worth a certainsum , or the renditionof servicesworth a certainsum , or contain-
ing equivalent allegations, is sufficientto authorize a judgment by default for
thatsum , because a promise to pay itisimplied by the law . In such a case, the
value of the goods , or services, need not be proved, because, in legal contempla-
tion, the action isfounded upon the defendant's promise to pay a certainsum
of money ," Skillman vs. Muir''sadm'r, 4 Met ., 284. Acc ., Woods vs. Wells, 2
Bush , 198.
(8) In actionsupon contracts,generally.
i. In an actionformoney which the defendant promised to pay to the plaintiff" on
sight," the allegation, " which sum of money the defendant did not pay to him
on sight," was held to be a sufficientstatement of a breach of the contract.
Dehoney vs. Sanford, 2 Bush , 170.
j. In an actionfora certainsum of money allegedto be due to theplaintiff,for
goods , wares,and merchandise sold and deliveredto the defendant, at his special
instanceand request, the allegation" which sum isjust, due , and unpaid," was
held to be a sufficientstatement of a breach of the contract . Wood vs. Wells , & c.,
2 Bush , 198 .
k. A petitionallegedthat, on the day of , 1851,the defendant'stestatorpro-
posed totheplaintiffthat,ifhe would quithis tradeas a carpenter,and go and
live on the testator's farm , the testator would do better for him than he was
doing, or could do, by his trade; that the plaintiff, confidingin said promises,
did,on the - day of - , 1851,quit his trade, and go to the farm of the testa-
tor,and livewith and aid him in his businessfrom the day of , 1851,until
hisdeathin1855; and thathistradewas worth$ 1,200perannum : yetneitherthe
testator, nor the defendants, his administratorswith the willannexed , " hath or
have paidtotheplaintiffanypartofsaidsum ofmoney," althoughoftenrequested,
& c.: held bad on demurrer,I,becauseitdid not show an acceptanceofthe
testator'spropositionwithin a reasonable time; 2, because it did not show a
breach . Moxley'sadm'rsvs.Moxley, 2 Met ., 309 .
1.In an actionagainstthe directorsof a bank forthe valueof specialdepositsof
theplaintiff, allegedto have been convertedby officersof the bank to itsuse,
the plaintiffneed not stateby what officersthe conversionwas made . United
Societyof Shakersvs. Underwood, & c., 9 Bush ,609 .
m . A corporation,suingupon a contract,need notallegethatithasbeen organized
as requiredby itscharter. Hughes vs.Bank of Somerset,5 Litt., 46; Wright
vs.ShelbyR. R. Co., 16 B. M. , 7 ; Henderson & NashvilleR. R. Co.vs. Leavell,
Id. 363. Contra, Fry vs.Lexington& Big Sandy R. R. Co., 2 Met ., 323.
n . In an actionon a note,the petitionfailedto stateitsdate; but stateditsamount,
and thetimewhen itwas payable; and thenotewas filedaspartofthepetition:
held, thatitwas sufficientlydescribed. Tottenvs. Cooke, 2 Met., 275.

CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SEC . 90 . 25
o. In an action on a bail-bond , the petitionmust show that itwas executed before
the proper officer. Jones vs.Bunn , 2 Met ., 490.
p . To enforcea lienon baggage of a guest,for the priceof board, the petitionmust
allegethatthe plaintiffwas a tavern-keeper. It is not sufficientto allege that
he was " a landlord,proprietorof the Myers House." Southwoodvs. Myers,3
Bush , 681 .
9. In an actionforone thirdof the profitsarisingoutof the saleof certaingoods
by the defendants, the petitionwas held to be defective, because, though it
alleged that the profitsamounted to a certainsum , it did not allegethat they
had been collectedby the defendants. Gould vs.Bonds , I Bush , 191.
r. In an actionon an injunction-bond,conditionedto pay thejudgment enjoined,
if the injunctionbe dissolved, the petitionmust allegethatthe judgment is
unpaid. Crawfordvs.Woodworth,9 Bush,745. And see2 Met.,490.
s. The plaintiffhaving agreed to deliver100,000 bushels of charcoalfor31½ cents
per bushel, sued on the contract,allegingthat he had delivered126,463 bushels;
and claiming 3½ cents per bushel forthe excess. The defendants denied that
the plaintiffhad deliveredthe allegedquantityof coal, and statedthat they had
no knowledge , nor informationto form a belief, as to the quantityhe had deliv-
ered: held, aftera verdictand judgment forthe plaintiff, for the excess, that, if
the defendants receivedmore coal than they were entitledto, they should pay
forthe excesswhat it was worth , whether they had knowledge of the excessor
not,when the coal was delivered; but that the petitionlaid no foundation for
such recovery, because itfailedto statethe value of the coal. Caldwell, Hunter
& Co. vs. Dawson , 4 Met ., 122 .
1. Allegationthat the defendant owes “ about $ 400," does not authorizejudgment
forthat or any other sum . Garnettvs. Finnell, 2 Duv ., 166.
It was held,however , under the old practice,thata complainant who alleged, in a
billin chancery, that he could not statethe true amount which he was entitled
to recover,but thatitcould not be lessthan a certainsum ,statedin the bill,was
entitledto recoverthe sum towhich the evidence showed he was entitled, though
itexceeded the sum stated in the bill; because " he was not bound to swear to
toomuch , forthe purpose of obtainingenough ." Moore vs. Simpson, 5 Litt., 52.
u . In an actionsurcharginga guardian's settlement, made in the county court, the
petitionmust specifythe itemsobjectedto. Tannervs.Skinner, II Bush,129,
130 .
v. As to actionsby assignorsagainstassigneesof notes, see 16 B. M. , 343 ; I Duv .,
301 ; General Statutes,ch. 22, § 7.
(9) In actionsunder statutoryprovisions.
w . In an actionagainsta defendant,forkillingthe plaintiff'sfather,the petition
must followthe language of the statute, and must allegethat the killingwas
done not in self-defense(Beckervs. Crow, 7 Bush, 202); and was causedby
defendant's" willfulneglect"-an allegationof " grossneglect" being insuffi-
cient. Hansford'sadm'x vs.Payne, II Bush , 382.
x. The Revised Statuteshaving made abandonment a cause fordivorce to the party
not in fault, itwas held that the husband's petitionfor a divorce, on account of
allegedabandonmentby hiswife,must statethatitwas withouthisfault. Ep-
lingvs.Epling, I Bush , 74.
y. The RevisedStatuteshavingdeclaredthatbillsof exchange,payablein bank
notesor currency,shallbe deemed negotiable, and treatedin allrespectsasif
drawn formoney ,exceptas to thevalueof currencyinwhich theyarepayable;
in an actionon a billpayable " in currentexchange," a petition, which described
thebillaspayableinmoney ,was heldtobe good on demurrer. Morrison& Co.
vs. Tate, I Met ., 569.

z. For other cases, as to actionsunder statutes, see note i, page 28.
(10) Miscellaneous cases.
aa. A defectivepetitioncannotbe curedby a statementoffactscontainedina paper
filedwith thepetition. Murphy vs.Estes,6 Bush,532. And seenotestosections
120 and 128 .
bb. In an actionfora divorceinthe Campbellcircuitcourt,an allegationthatthe
plaintiffis a resident of said county, omitting the words " in Kentucky ," is

8. PETITIONS IN ACTIONS UPON WRITINGS .
a. In an actionon a contract, if the petitiondo not statethat it isin writing, nor
referto any writing, the court must presume that it is a verbal contract(Smith
vs.Fah , 15 В. М. , 443 ; Hocker vs.Gentry,3 Met ., 474); though the recordmay
contain what purports to be a copy of a written contractbetween the parties.
Byassee vs.Reese, 4 Met ., 372. But in Haly vs.Auditor, 4 Bush , 493, the court
said: " Itdoesnot appearfrom thepetitionin thiscase,whether thework was
done under a contract, made with the directionsand advice of the Governor or
not; and,as the petitionallegesthatthe plaintiffwas employed by thesuper-
intendent to do the work , and does not disclosethat the contract was not in
writing, itmust be presumed that the officersdid theirduty , in the absence of
factsto sustaina contrary.conclusion."
b. Upon thequestion,whetherornot a defectivepetitioncan be aidedby a writing
filedtherewith, there isa conflictof opinions.
A petition,in an actionupon a note,which was filedtherewith,failedto allege
when the notebecame due. The court, afterreferringonlytothecaseof Dodd
vs.King, I Met.,430; and afterstatingthat" thereisno essentialanalogybe-
tween these cases," said: " Here , the omission to allegewhen the note was pay-
ablewas suppliedby thenoteitself,aseffectuallyasifithad beenliterallycopied
by the petitionas a portionof itsallegations." Burtonvs.White, I Bush,10.
CONTRA : Hillvs. Barrett, 14 B. M. , 83 ; Collinsvs.Blackburn, Id. 252 ; Dodd vs.
King , 1 Met .,430 ; Riggs vs. Maltby,2 Id.,88 ; Mountjoy'sadm'r vs.Pearce, 4 Id.,
99; Allenand wifevs.Shortridge,& c.,I Duv .,34; Murphy vs.Eastis,6 Bush,532.
c. Itisonlynecessary,however,fortheplaintifftostatesomuch of thecontractas
shows that he is entitled to relief. Henderson and Nashville R. R. Co. vs. Leavell,
16 В. М. ,362 ; Tottenvs. Cooke, 2 Met ., 279.
d. A writtenaccountorcontract,upon which an actionisfounded,must be noticed,
for the purpose of learning whether or not the statements of the petitionare
true; and the statementsof the writingcontrol,ifinconsistentwith thoseof
the petition(Bush vs.Madeira'sheirs, 14 B. M. , 213 ; Francisvs. Francis, 18 Id.,
61 ; Dodd vs.King , I Met .,430 ; Haney vs. Tempest, 3 Id., 95 ; Wile vs. Sweeny, 2
Duv ., 161); unlessthewritingbe impeachedby thepetition. Bush vs.Madeira's
heirs, supra .
e. See notes to sections 120 , 128 .
9. PROOF AND ALLEGATIONS MUST CORRESPOND .
a. The causeof action, or ground of defense,must be allegedinthe pleadingsas
wellassustainedby proof. The latter,in theabsenceof the former,isinsuffi-
cientto sustaina judgment. Garretvs.Gault, 13 B. M. ,380; Kearneyvs.City
of Covington, I Met.,343; Muhling vs.Sattler,3 Id.,286; Phænix InsuranceCo.
vs. Lawrence , 4 Id ., 18 .
This doctrinewas appliedtoa caseinwhich theplaintiff, in hispetition,claimed
a legalright; and, by his evidence, showed thathe had an equitableright.
Garret vs.Gault, 13 B. M. ,380.
b . But see notes to ? 118 .

92 .
10. EFFECT OF UNNECESSARY ALLEGATIONS .
a. Generally, a party is bound by his statement of any material fact pleaded by
him , though he need not have statedit.
Thus,inan actionon a policyof insurance,which the defendantseekstoavoid,
because prohibitedarticleswere kept by the plaintiff, the defendant isnot bound
to specifythe prohibitedarticlesso kept,not being presumed to know them
[quere]; but if he do so, specifyingsome , without allegingthat others are kept
by the plaintiff, thejury cannotconsiderany exceptthosespecified. Phanix
Insurance Co. vs. Lawrence ,4 Met ., 18 .
b. So ,in an actionupon a contract, if the plaintiffstateforwhat considerationit
was made ,the legalpresumption of a sufficientconsiderationceasesto exist; and
the allegedconsideration,if controverted,must be proved by the plaintiff. Bul-
littvs.Raiston, I A. K. Mar. , 331 ; Steadman vs.Guthrie, 4 Met ., 152.
c. But in an actionfor a reward offeredby the defendant for the capture and con-
victionof certainpersons, who had been captured by the plaintiffs, the petition
allegedthat the defendant had procured the dismissalof the indictment against
those persons, afterthey had confessedtheirguiltin court; and that the defend-
ant did so with the fraudulentintent to evade payment of said reward : held,
that the lastnamed allegationwas immaterial, and need not be proved . Louis-
villeand NashvilleR. R. Co. vs.Goodnight, 10 Bush , 554.
d. And in an actionwhich a married woman had a right to bring, without joining
with her husband ,her allegationthat he refusedto join with her was held to be
not traversable. Hardin vs.Gerard, 10 Bush , 259.
11. PRAYER FOR RELIEF .
a. Observethat ? 90 of thisCode forbidsjudgment by defaultexceptfor relief
specificallydemanded .
b. And it was held, under the Code of 1854, that though , perhaps, in an action
upon an unliquidated account, a count for interestor damages is unnecessary;
yet,insuchan action,unlessinterestordamages be claimed,no judgment should
be rendered foreither. Adams Express Co.vs.Milton,II Bush ,49 .
c. A petitionhavingstatedseveralclaimsagainstthedefendant,and theamount of
each,prayedforjudgment forthe amount of saidclaims: heldtobe sufficient.
2 Bush , 102-3 .
d. In Williamsvs.Abrahams,3 Bush, 187,itwas held,in an actionby a vendor, to
enforcea lienforpurchase-money ,thatthe plaintiffwas entitledtoa judgment
forthemoney, thoughhe failedtoshow a righttoenforcethelien.
e. A plaintiffhavinggotpossession,underbond, of a horseforwhich he sued,the
defendant answered avowing titlein himself, and praying tobe hencedismissedwith
costs: held, thatthe defendant,having provedtitlein himself,was entitledto a
returnof thehorse,or toitsvalue,and to damages forthedetention. Batesvs.
Buchanan , 2 Bush , 117 .
f. See 3 Monroe,50; 5 J.J. Mar., 125; 5 Dana ,338.
SPECIAL DEMURRERS .
I. When objectiontobemade by motioninsteadof demurrer.
2. Objectiontojurisdictionof thecourt.
3. Objectiontoplaintiff'scapacitytosue.
4. Pendency of anotheraction.
5. Defectofparties.
I. WHEN OBJECTION TO BE MADE BY MOTION INSTEAD OF DEMURRER .
a. Neithera general,nor a special,demurrerlies,becausetheplaintiffhasfailedto
sweartohispetition(Harrisvs.Ray,15 B. M. ,630); norbecausehe hasbrought

28 CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SECS . 92 , 93 .
93 .
an equitable, instead of an ordinary, action; nor viceversa (Trustees, & c., vs.
Forest, 15 B. M. , 172 ; Fosterus. Watson, 16 Id., 387); nor for a misjoinderof
parties,plaintiffsor defendants (Dean vs. English, 18 B. M. , 136) ; nor because
an amended petitionsetsup a new cause of action. Hord vs.Chandler, 13 B. M. ,
403 .
Such objectionsmust be made by motion . See casessupra.
2. OBJECTION TO JURISDICTION OF THE COURT .
b. Objection to jurisdictionof theperson of the defendant is waived, unlessitbe
made by a specialdemurrer , or by answer ; aliter, as to jurisdictionof thesubject
of the action. See 2 118 , and notes thereto .
3. OBJECTION TO PLAINTIFF'S CAPACITY TO SUE.
c. See ? 118,and notes thereto.
4. PENDENCY OF ANOTHER ACTION .
d. Observethat ? 92 appliesonly to another actionpending in " thisState." Such
was the law beforethe adoption of the Code ; and such was declaredto be the
law under the Code of 1854, though it did not literallymake such a limitation.
Davis vs.Morton , Galt & Co.,4 Bush ,442 .
e. An actionto enforcea lienforpurchase-money doesnotpreventan actionon the
note therefor . Julian vs. Pilcher, 2 Duv ., 254 .
f . An action having been brought by A in W. county againstB and others,forthe
recoveryof slaves,was moved by changeof venue toC. county. Subsequently,
A sued the same partiesin W. countyforan attachmentof saidslaves; and
the petitionshowed that the firstnamed actionwas stillpending in C. county:
held,that a demurrer to the petitionshould have been sustained. Moore vs.Shep-
pard , 1 Met ., 97.
g . Under the old practice,itwas decided that an actionshould not abate on account
of a priorone,pendingwhen thesecondwas commenced ,theformerhavingbeen
dismissedbeforethe filingof the plea. Adams vs. Gardiner, 13 B. М. ,197.
5. DEFECT OF PARTIES .
h . Though the defendant cannot object, by demurrer , to a misjoinderof parties
(Dean vs.English, 18 B. M. , 136), a defectof parties, appearing in the petition,
isground fora specialdemurrer. If itdo not so appear, itmay be statedinthe
answer . If the objectionbe not made by demurrer or answer,itiswaived (Albro
vs.Lawson , 17 В. М. , 645); and the court may decide the case between those
beforeit,ifthe rightsof otherscan be saved. (Code, ?40.) Iftherebe other
indispensableparties, the courtshouldrequirethem to be broughtbeforeit.
Johnson vs.Chandler,15 B. М. , 589.
i. An objection,thattheplaintiffisbut the equitableownerof thenotesuedon,is
waived ,unlessmade by demurrer or answer. Levi vs.Mendell, I Duv ., 77.
j. But the objectionto an action by one payee, upon a note to two, isnot merely
an objectionfor the want of proper parties. A general demurrer lies, on the
ground that the petitionfailsto statea cause of action. Quisenberryvs.Artis,I
Duv ., 30 .
k. See notes to ? 118 .
GENERAL DEMURRERS .
a. A demurrerliestoa defensewhich is insufficientlypleadedina paragraphwith
one which issufficientlypleaded. Williamsvs.Langford, 15В. М. ,569.
b. A demurrerto a pleadingpresentsallpreviouspleadingsfortheconsideration
of the court,which shoulddecideagainstthepartycommittingthe firstfault.
Martin vs.McDonald , 14 B. M. ,547; Young vs.Duhme ,4 Met .,242.
But,as thedefendant,by answeringtothemerits,waivesallobjectionstothepeti
tion,excepttheobjections, thatthecourthas no jurisdictionof thesubjectof

CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SECS . 93 , 94 , 95 . 29
94 .
the action,and thatthe petitiondoes not statefactsconstitutinga causeof
action; the court, upon a demurrer to such an answer, cannot considerany other
defectsof the petition. Mitchellvs.Mattingly, I Met ., 237 .
c. If the defendant answer afterhisdemurrer isoverruled, and a verdict and judg .
ment be renderedagainsthim, he cannot, by an appeal, availhimselfof any
defectin the petition, exceptsuch as authorizesan arrestof thejudgment.
Duncan vs.Brown , 15 B. М. , 196, 197.
d . A defendant who files, at the same time, a demurrer and an answer , may waive
the latterand relyon the former. Bridgesvs.Reed, 9 Bush ,329.
e. Subsection2 of 293 declares,ineffect, asdidtheCode of 1854(sections121and
123),thatthefailureofa defendanttodemur toa petitionisnota waiverof the
objection,thatthepetitiondoesnot statea causeof action.
Itwas accordinglyheld,in Smithvs.Fah,15B.M.,443, thattheobjection, that
the defendant's undertaking to be responsiblefor another person was not in
writing thatfactappearingin the petition- was notwaivedby a failuretoan-
swer .
It is clear that a defendant, who has demurred without answering, or who has
neither demurred nor answered , may , by an appeal, obtain relieffrom a judg-
ment founded on a defective statement of a cause of action .
But ,accordingtoseveraldecisions,a petitionwhich failstostatea causeof action
may be so aided by the defendant'sanswer as to entitlethe plaintiffto a judg-
ment ; especiallyaftera jury-trial, and a verdictfortheplaintiff. There issome
conflictbetween the decisionsupon this subject. See note 6 to 295 , and note
3 to 2 134 .
AMENDMENT AFTER DEMURRER SUSTAINED .
a. A judgment upon demurrer,dismissingtheplaintiff'spetition,upon theground
of its insufficiency,cannot operate as a bar to another suit upon the cause of
actionattemptedtobe setoutin thedefectivepetition. Birchvs.Funk,2 Met.,
544 .
95 . ANSWERS .
1. Traverse. Page 29.
2. Statementof new matterof defense. Page 30.
3. Proof and allegationsmust correspond. Page 31.
4. Statementsof legalconclusions. Page 31.

1. TRAVERSE .
a. See ? 113,subsection7,fordefinitionof " Traverse;" and seenotesthereto.
b. It is not sufficientforthe defendant to say that he " knows nothing of his own
knowledge" concerningfactsalleged(Terrellvs.Jennings,I Met.,454); or that
he " does not admit a fact" alleged, " and requiresproof" (Clarkvs.Finnell,16 B.
M. ,335); or that he " has no knowledge of any of the facts" alleged, " and no
means of forming a belief, and thereforedeniesthem alland requiresproof" (Cor-
binvs.Commonwealth,2 Met.,380); orthathe hasno recollectionorbeliefthatan
allegedpayment was made to him , and thereforedenies the same . Williamsonvs.
McConnell , 4 Dana ,454 .
But ,exceptastofactswhicharepresumptivelywithinthedefendant'sknowledge,it
issufficientto say that the defendant " has not enough knowledge or information
to constitutea beliefwhether " an allegedfactexistsor not (Hutchingsvs.Moore,
4 Met.,114); and in an actionagainstdefendants,asindorsersof a bill,allega

30 CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SEC . 95 .
tionsthat itwas indorsedby the Curlew Coal Company , and that the defendants
were members of the company , are sufficientlycontrovertedby denying thatthe
defendants were members of the company . Curlew CoalCo.vs.Grief[two cases],
2 Duv .,527,528 .
c. Matters of defense averred in an answer inconsistentwith factsalleged inthe
petition,which the defendant has impliedlyadmitted by failingto deny them,
are unavailing. Corbinvs.Commonwealth , 2 Met ., 381-2 .
d. In an actionagainsta cityto recovertaxespaid, the plaintiffallegedthathe
paid them in ignoranceof hisrights,and of the factthatthe assessmentwas
illegal: theanswer,allegingthattheplaintiffpaid the taxes" withfullknowl-
edge of the law and facts," was held to be insufficienton demurrer . Cityof
Covingtonvs.Powell, 2 Met ., 226.
e.A petitionallegedthatthe defendantowed the plaintiff$ 140forthe hireofa
slavefor the year 1859 ; that the defendant had hired said slave, and had him
in possessionduring said term, and received the product of his servicesto the
amount orworth of $ 140: an answer,denyingthatthedefendanthad any slave
of plaintiffhiredascharged,or thatthedefendantagreedtopay eitherclothing
or hire; and thereforedenying that,the defendantowed the plaintiff$ 140,or
any other sum , was held to be insufficient. Baum vs.Winston, 3 Met ., 127.
f. In an actionto subjectproperty,under the act of 1856 concerningfraudulent
preferences, the petitionalleged that the debts secured by the mortgage ,which
was alleged to have been made in contemplation of insolvency, " were not
createdsimultaneously" therewith: an answer, averringthat saiddebtswere
simultaneouswith the mortgage , was held to be evasive. Terrellvs.Jennings,
I Met ., 457 .
g . A petitionallegedthat the defendant was indebted to the plaintiffin the sum
of $ 620 for " merchandise," a long account of which was filedwith the peti-
tion, without any specificationof the articlessold: an answer denying that the
defendant was indebted to the plaintiffmore than $ 500 on the account presented
by the petitionwas held to be sufficientlyresponsive; and held, that,upon the
pleadings, theplaintiffwas only entitledto judgment for$500. Webb vs.Jef.
fries, 2 Bush , 222.
h . Under theoldpracticeitwas heldthatan allegationof a bill,which was denied
by answer,must be proved,though itwas repeatedinan amended billtowhich
there was no answer. Dittovs. Young ,3 J. J. Mar. , 189 ; Greenwade vs.Green-
wade ,3 Dana ,495. Acc ., Robinson vs. Williamson,7 Bush ,604 .
i. As to the plea of non estfactum, see note to Form 12.
j. As to plea of no consideration, see note to Form 21 .
2. STATEMENT OF NEW MATTER OF DEFENSE .
a . Averment in an answer that a certainfact" did in fact, as defendantbelieves,exist
and was true," isnot a sufficientaverment of the fact. Mitchelvs.Mattingly, I
Met ., 241 .
b. In an actionby an administratorfora debt, an answer, that the defendant'swife
is a distributeeof the estate, and that no distributionhas been made , does not
presentan equitabledefense: itshould show that theplaintiffhas a sufficientsum
for distributionto entitleeach distributeeto a sum equal to the amount of the
note sued on . Haggard vs.Hay , 13 B. M. , 176 .
c. In an actionupon a policy,which thedefendantseekstoavoid,on theground
thatprohibitedarticleswere kept by the plaintiff, itwas held that the defendant,
notbeing presumedtoknow what prohibitedarticleswerekeptby theplaintiff,
was not bound tospecifythem inhisanswer; but that,having done so, specify.
ing some, withoutallegingthatany otherswerekept by the plaintiff, thejury

CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SEC . 95 . 31
should not be permitted to considerany except those specified. Phænix Insur-
ance Co. vs. Lawrence , 4 Met ., 18 .
d. To a petitionon a noteforthepurchase-money of a slave,an answerthattheven-
'dors had no title, and had not, and could not convey any titleto the defendant,
presentsno defense, as itdoes not aver a warranty of title. Patrickvs.Swinney,
5 Bush ,424 .
e. Limitationmust be pleaded,though the actionbe apparentlybarred(Chilesvs.
Drake,2 Met.,146; Rankin vs.Turney, 2 Bush , 555 ; Board vs.Jolly,5 Bush ,86);
unless the petitionshows the non -existenceof any ground of avoidance of the
plea(Rankin vs.Turney,supra); or,possibly,unlesstheactionbe foundedupon
a statutewhich requiresitto be broughtwithina certaintime. See noteito
90 , pages 21 , 22 .
f. An answer,pleadinglimitation,presentsa validdefensethough it admitsthat
the debt has not been paid. Gray vs.McDowell , 6 Bush ,475 .
3. PROOF AND ALLEGATIONS MUST CORRESPOND .
a. Evidence thata contractwas illegalcannot be considered, unless the defense be
pleaded . Denton vs.Logan 3 Met ., 434. But quere: if the petitionshows that
the contractwas illegal, will the court enforce it, though the illegalitybe not
pleaded by the defendant? See 15 B. M. , 443 ; 3 Met ., 464-5, 469, 474 ; and
Alfriendvs.Hughes,4 Bush,40; which was an actionformoney allegedtohave
been " paid, advanced , and loaned" by the plaintiffto the defendant. The
answer denied the allegationsof the petition,but did not stateany new matter of
defense. The evidenceshowed that the advance was made , as the plaintiffknew ,
to enable the defendant to pay money loston a horse-race; and itwas held that
the plaintiffwas not entitledto recover.
b. In an actionforan assault, the defendant denied the allegationsof the petition:
proof of threatsmade by the plaintiffand of factsoccurringsome months before,
were held to be inadmissible; though the defendant might have proved any part
of the resgesta, in mitigationof damages . Slatervs. Sherman ,5 Bush ,212.
c. Upon pleas of no consideration, and of failureof consideration, to a petitionon
a note, defendant cannot prove that the note was given in blank,and filledup
with a largersum thanwas authorized. Sanfordvs.Smith,5 Bush,471.
4. STATEMENTS OF LEGAL CONCLUSIONS .
a . It isnot sufficientfor the defendant to deny that he isindebted to the plaintiff;
or to deny that he owes anything to the plaintiff; or to deny " the correctness
of each and every item " of the plaintiff'saccount against him . Haggard vs.
Hays ' adm'r, 13 B. M. , 175 ; Clark vs. Finnell, 16 Id.,335 ; Francis vs.Francis,
18 Id., 60 ; Baum vs. Winston, 3 Met ., 127 ; McElfatrickvs.Taft, 10 Bush , 160 ;
Whitaker vs.Sandifer, I Duv ., 262.
b. As to the plea of non estfactum , see note I to Form 12.
c. In an actionon a bond , itwas held that the defendant, in order to obtain the
benefitof the presumptionof payment, resultingfrom lapseof time, should
have pleaded payment ; and that itwas not sufficient, for that purpose, to plead
that he was not indebted to the plaintiff. Haggard vs.Hays' adm'r, 13 B. M. ,
175 .
d. In an actionagainsta common carrier, fora losscaused by the jettisonof goods ,
an answer allegingthat the losswas occasionedby dangers of the river,and that
the accidentwas unforeseen and unavoidable, was held to present no defense,
because itmerely statedlegalconclusions: itshould have stated,with reasonable
precision and certainty, what the danger was , and the factswhich prevented it
from being foreseen, and which rendered itunavoidable. Bentlyvs.Bustard, 16
В. М. ,686.

32 CIVIL CODE-- NOTES TO SEC . 95 .
e. A statement in an answer, that the defendant was under the age of twenty-one
yearswhen he executedthe notesued on,isa sufficientpleaof infancy. The
defendant need not statethat the note was voidable, nor otherwise statethe
law of the case. Sternvs. Freeman , 4 Met ., 311.
f. An allegation" thatthewritingsued on was obtainedfrom thedefendantsby
thefraud,covin,and misrepresentationof the plaintiff," was not a mere con-
clusion of law ; but was " the averment of a substantive and traversable fact."
Whiteheadvs.Root, 2 Met.,588. But quereas to an allegationof fraudwhich
does not even show whetherthe allegedfraudrelatedto the execution,or the
considerationof the contract. See Barlow vs. Wiley, 3 A. K. Mar. , 460 ; and
Forms 23 and 30.
g . In an action concerning land, a defendant'sallegation, that he was an innocent
purchaser,was held to be immaterial: he shouldhave allegedpayment ofthe
purchase-money without notice, & c. Wing vs.Hayden , 10 Bush , 280.
h . See note 3 to 2 90, page 19.
5. LEGAL PRESUMPTIONS MUST BE REBUTTED : ONUS PROBANDI .
a . A's widow , suing forland which had belonged to him , allegedthat the defend-
ants, who claimed the land as hisheirs, and allof his relations, were foreigners
by birth, and aliensat the time of his death. The answer denied that thede-
fendants were aliensat the time of A's death ,but did not deny that they were
foreignersby birth: held, that, having impliedly admitted that they were for-
eignersby birth, itmust be presumed thattheywere aliensat thetimeofA's
death; and thattheonuswas on them toallegeand provetheirnaturalization.
White vs. White, 2 Met ., 185.
b. The payors of two notes,having given to the payee a writingin which , afterre-
ferringto the notes, they said: " We expectto pay them when theyfalldue.
We have no offsets, nor will have , on those notes;" theirdenial that theyexe-
cutedthewritingtoenablethepayeetosellthenoteswas heldtobe insufficient
tothrow on the assigneetheburthenof proof. The payorsshouldhaveshown
forwhat purpose the writingwas executed. Wellsvs.Lewis,4 Met., 269.
c. " A denial by a married woman that she signed a note with the intentionto
bindher separateestatewas held to be insufficienttothrowupon the plaintiff
theburthenof provingtheintention." Wellsvs.Lewis,4 Met.,273,citingEastin
vs. Fulwiller, MS . opin .
d. A billallegeda saleof a horseby the plaintifftothe defendantfor$70,tobe
creditedon theplaintiff'snote; theanswer admittedthepurchaseof thehorse
atthatprice,but allegedthatitwas tobe creditedon anotherdemand : held,in
theabsenceof proof,thattheplaintiffwas entitledtothecredit. Greenwadevs.
Greenwade , 3 Dana , 498.
e. It seems probable that, in the casesof Wells vs. Lewis and Eastin vs. Fulwiller,
above cited, the court might have gone farther, and have held that the parties
were estopped from denying theirintention. See Lewis vs.Harris, 4 Met.,353,
inwhich itwas held thatthe questionconcerningthe intentionwithwhicha
party executed a note- whether to bind himself, or as trusteefor another- isa
questionof law, to be decidedby the court, in view of the languageofthe
note, the circumstances under which itwas executed, and the situationof the
parties.
6. ANSWERS CURING DEFECTIVE PETITIONS .
a. In an actionfortrespass, the petitionallegedthatthe defendantbrokeinto
theplaintiff'shouse,and demanded her watch,withoutallegingthathe took
thewatch: the defectwas cured by the answer,which deniedthatthedefend.
anttookthewatch. Jewellvs.Mills,3 Bush,66.

CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SECS. 95, 96 . 33
b . So , in an actionforan injurycaused by a defectivebridge, the plaintiff'sfailure
to allege that itwas a public bridge, & c., was cured by the defendant'sdenial
that itwas a public bridge, & c. Louisvilleand PortlandCanal Co.vs.Murphy ,
9 Bush , 529 .
c. " It is a sound rule, that a billought to contain such charges and allegations
as will support the jurisdictionof a court of equity, and warrant the decree of
the chancellorupon itsallegations. But itisa sound exceptionto thatrule,
that many of such charges and allegationsmay be dispensed with, where the
complainant is ignorant of his rights; and , if the answer or proof aids such a
case, reliefmay be granted without regard to the defective,and sometimes incor-
rect, statementsof the bill." Deatlyvs.Murphy , 3 A. K. Mar. , 474. [In that
case,allof theplaintiffs,exceptone,were underdisability.]
d. In Neilsonvs. Churchill, 5 Dana , 338, relief, which was not designated eitherby
the allegationsof the bill, or the prayer for relief, was given under a prayer for
general relief, upon the ground that " the defectsand omissions, being of mat-
terswhich cannot be presumed to be correctlyknown to the complainants, are
aided by the statementsin the answer."
e. See 2 Met .,88 ; 2 A. K. Mar. , 317 ; I Mon. , 237; 7 Dana , 173 ; 8 Dana , 184; 4
B. M. , 199; 367.
96 , subs. I. COUNTER -CLAIMS .
a . In an actionforan assault,thedefendantmay ,by counter-claim,recoverdam-
ages for an assaultcommitted on him , at the same time, by the plaintiff. Slone
vs. Slone, 2 Met ., 339 .
b . In an actionby a mortgagor againsta mortgagee ,forwrongfullytakingpossession
of mortgagedproperty,beforea forfeitureof the condition,the defendantmay
plead the mortgage debt as a counter-claim. Brown vs.Phillips, 3 Bush , 656.
c. The only restrictionmade by the Code , as to a counter-claim, isthat itshallbe a
causeof actionarisingout ofthecontractor transactionsetforthin thepetition,
as the foundation of the plaintiff'sclaim. It isnot necessarythat itbe founded
on contract,or ariseout of the contractsetforthin the petition. It issufficient,
if it ariseout of the transactionsetforth in the petition, or be connected with
the subjectof the action. Tinsleyvs. Tinsley, 15 B. М. , 454 .
d . In an action for trespassby A againstB , the purchaser of A's land, sold by a
sheriffunder a void execution, the defendant'sdemand for the purchase-money
paid by him isnot a validcounter-claim; though itwould be an equitableset-
off if the plaintiffwere insolvent. Geoghegan vs.Ditto, 2 Met ., 433-
e. In an actionfor the contract-priceforbuildinga bridge, the defendants may ,by
counter-claim, demand damages forthe plaintiff'sfailureto buildthe bridge
according to contract. Moore vs. Caruthersand Applegate, 17 B. M. , 669.
f. In an actionby a railroadcompany for money due upon a subscriptionof stock,
the defendant allegedthathe had sustaineddamages by the plaintiff'staking of
a way throughhisfarm; and that,at the timeof thesubscription, the plaintiff
agreed that said damages should go in discharge of his subscription: held, on
demurrer , to be a valid counter-claim,being connected with the subject of the
actionby the agreement . Lou .and Nash .R. R. Co. vs.Thompson ,18 В. М. , 735
g . In an actionon an injunction-bond,givenin a proceedingtoenjoina judgment
recovered on a writ of forcibleentry and detainer, the defendant may demand ,
by way of counter-claim,thevalueof a cropof corn,which was plantedby him
whilst the injunctionwas pending , and which was growing when itwas taken by
the plaintiff, and when the injunctionwas dissolved. Tinsleyvs. Tinsley, 15 B.
M. , 460 .

6. In an action by A and wife, on a note executed to her as administratrixof B ,

c. A being indebted to B $ 100, and C being indebted to A $300, C died; and his

$ 100, itwas held that A could set offhis demand againstB , as executorde son
tortforthe $ 300,because said demand was againstB personally, and not as the
lawfulrepresentativeof C. McKenzie vs.Pendleton'sadm'x , I Bush , 164.

3. INVALID CLAIMS TO SET-OFFS .
a. A set-off, to be available, eitherat law or in equity, against an assignee, must

on which he issued,isthe payee'ssuretyon another note,constitutesno defense,
and gives no right of set-off, in law or equity, againsteitherthe payee or his
assignee, though the payee be insolvent, the surety-debt not having been paid :
to constitutea defense against the action, the surety-debt should have been
paid before noticeof the assignment. Walker vs.McKay , 2 Met ., 294.
b . See notes 2 a and b to ? 96 subs . 2.
