4. GENERAL RULES CONCERNING PLEAS OF SET -OFFS AND COUNTER -CLAIMS .
a. Itwas settled,thatan answerwhich statedfactsconstitutinga set-offorcoun-
ter-claim, with a proper prayer for relief, must be treatedas a set-offor counter-
claim, though not so denominated . Hutchings vs. Moore, 4 Mct ., 113; True vs.
Triplett, Id., 57.
b. But itwas held that an answer, which presentedmatter, constitutinga counter-
claim, merely as matter of defense, could not be treated as a counter-claim.
Thus ,A , being sued for the purchase-money of land, pleaded that the plaintiff
had not conveyed the land according to contract; but assertedno claim founded
on the allegednon-performance,and asked forno reliefin consequenceof it;
but reliedupon it for no other purpose, and ascribedto itno other effect, than
that of defeating the reliefsought by the plaintiff; and itwas held that the
answer was not a counter-claim. Hutchingsvs.Moore, 4 Met ., 110.
c. An answer, which presents merely matter of defense, cannot be treated as a
counter-claim or set-off, though it be so denominated in the answer. Thus , in
an actionby the assigneeof a note, itwas held that a plea of usury embraced
therein, or of payment to the payee, presented matter of defense, and not of
counter-claim or set-off, and that no reply was necessary. True vs.Triplett, 4
Met ., 57 .
d. But an answer claiming usury, paid before the note sued on was given, presents
matter of set-off, and must be taken as trueunlessdenied by a reply. Hays vs.
Goodwin , 4 Met ., 83 .
e. It was held that a plea settingup a counter-claim, though signed and verifiedby
a person not a party to the action, having been filedas the answer and petition
of thedefendants, " must be regardedas thecounter-claimof the defendants;"
and in the absence of a reply itsallegationsmust be taken as true. Lyle&
Eubank vs. Poynter , I Duv ., 357-
f . If the answer statea set-offagainstthe plaintiff, which isalsoa demand against
others, againstwhom the answer ismade cross-petition, the plaintiffisnot
bound to reply to the set-off until the defendants to the cross-petitionare
brought beforethe court, or untilthe cross-petitionisdiscontinuedas to them .
Scottvs.Wilson, 2 Bush , 603.
g . If thedefendant'sanswerpresentno defense,but presentsa counter-claimforas
largea sum as that claimed by the plaintiff, the plaintiffisnot entitledto judg-
ment until aftertrialof the issueupon the counter-claim; and , if the counter-
claimexceedthe plaintiff'sdemand, the defendantisentitledtoa judgment for
the excess. Moore vs. Caruthers, 17 B. М. , 681.
h.To a pleaof set-off,the plaintiffmay replythestatuteof limitation. Banks vs.
Coyle,2 A. K. Mar. , 564.
But limitationagainstthe set-offceasesat the commencement of the action. Hayes
vs. Goodwin, 4 Met ., 83.
i. If an answermerelydeny factsallegedinthepetition,ormerelyallegefactsdeniea
inthepetition,no replyisnecessary,though theanswerconstitutea counter-claim.
Davis vs. Dycus, 7 Bush , 9.

claim,exceptforreliefwhich isspecificallydemanded ; and thatsubsection4 of
97 forbidsjudgment upon a set-offorcounter-claim,presentedin an answer,
unlessitscaptioncontain the words " answer and set-off," or the words " answer
and counter-claim;" or upon a counter-claim, presentedin a reply, unlessits
captioncontainthewords " answer and counter-claim." Thisdoes notseem to

ter -claim .

96 ,subsection3 . CROSS -PETITIONS .
a. The Code of 1854 substitutedcounter-claimsfor cross-petitions, as between
plaintiffsand defendants in equitable actions; and authorized counter-claims
in ordinary actions; but did not authorizeeither counter-claims or cross-peti-
tions,as between co-defendants or co-plaintiffs. Subsection3 of 96 is, substan-
tially, a re-enactment of an act of 1857, as to cross-petitionsby defendants; and
of an actof 1859,as tocross-petitionsby plaintiffs.
b. Under the aforesaidact of 1857, itwas held, that, in an actionbrought by A , as
administratorof B , againstC ,the lattercould maintain a cross-petitionagainstA ,
fora wrong done byhim ; because the bringingof the actionby A ,in hisfiduciary
capacity,did notmake him individuallya partytotheaction. Phillipsvs.Keifer,
2 Met .,478 .
c. In an actionfordebt,by a testator'screditoragainsthisexecutorand devisees,
the executorcannot maintain a cross-petitionagainstthe devisees, for an alleged
balance due to him from the estate; nor can they maintain a cross-petitionagainst
him fora devastavit. Crabtreevs.Banks , I Met ., 482 .
d . A lessee, sued for rent, may , by cross-petition, sue his assignee for payment
thereof. Trabue vs.McAdams , 8 Bush , 80.
e. A sued B to enjointhe enforcement of a judgment , because A was a surety, and
had been releasedby lapse of time. B made his answer a cross-petitionagainst
C , a sheriff, claiming damages for his failureto return an execution upon said
judgment,and hisfailuretosellpropertyleviedon: held, thata demurrertothe
cross-petitionshould have been sustained, because the cause of action therein
stated did not affectthe subject-matter of the action. Royse vs. Reynolds, 10
Bush , 286 .
f. In an actionbrought by A againsthis debtor,B ,the defendant was constructively
summoned ,and a tractof land belonging to him was attached. B mortgaged
the land,pendentelite, toC ; who ,having been made a defendant,filedan answer
contestingthe attachment, and praying foran enforcement of the mortgage . It
was held thatthe attachmentwas void, and that,therefore,the courthad no
jurisdictionas to the cross-petition. Petersvs. Conway , 4 Bush ,571.
But , quere; because, though the attachment was void, it seems that the courthad
jurisdictionof the actionof A ; and might , on finalhearing, have ordered a sale
of the land forthe payment of A's demand , aftersatisfyingthe mortgage -debt.
The court seems to have believedthat an attachment isnecessaryto give juris-
dictionof an action against a defendant constructivelysummoned . This seems
to be erroneous. An attachment isnot an action; nor isa ground for an attach-
ment a cause of action. Such ground need not be stated in the petition; and ,
untilthe decisionwas rendered in Scottvs.Doneghy , 17 B. M. , 321, there seemed
to be good reason forbelievingthat such ground could be statedonly in a sepa-
rate affidavit. " This provisionalremedy is merely ancillaryand incidentalto
the-actionin which itissought. It may be had eitherat or afterthe commence-
ment of the action." Moore vs. Sheppard, I Met ., 97. To make the jurisdiction
of the court, as to an action,depend upon the validityof an attachment obtained
in it, would reversethe rule, that " the incidentfollowsthe principal." Section
449 of the Code of 1854, of which ? 418 of this Code is substantiallya copy ,
declared: " No lien on the property of a defendant constructivelysummoned
shallbe created otherwise than by an attachment,as provided in chapter 3 of
title8,orbyjudgment." It seemsclearthatthe Legislaturecontemplatedthat
an actionagainsta defendant constructivelysummoned might proceed to judg-.
ment againsthim ; and that the judgment might createa lienupon hisproperty,
though the plaintiffhad failedtoattachit. It seems difficultto perceiveupon

liencan affectthejurisdictionof thecourt,or therightsof theplaintiff, astothe
action .
g. Lien-notes,for the purchase-money of land,having been assignedto fourper-
sons, severally; in an action by one of the assigneesagainst the others, and

vicethereof,eitheractualor constructive,isindispensableto authorizeany relief;

Met ., 354-5- a dictum, because the court held that, in view of allthe evidence
in the case, the testimony of the plaintiff'ssinglewitness was insufficientto
overcome the defendant'sdenial. And in Crutchfieldvs. Thurman , 4 Bush ,498,
the court, whilst impliedly conceding the sufficiencyof one witness, refused
reliefon his testimony.
In Worley vs.Tuggle, 4 Bush , 182, the court said: " In Albrovs.Lawson , 17 B. М. ,
644, thiscourt held, that the rulewhich requiredtwo witnesses, or one witness
with strong corroboratingcircumstances, to overcome a denialin chancery, has
been changedby saidsection[142]of theCode ; which was subsequentlyaffirmed
by thiscourtin Endersvs.Williams,I Met., 354-5. So itmay now be regarded
that the oath of the plaintiffand the defendant, making an issue, are equiva-
lents, and the issue[must be] adjudicatedupon the evidence."
The court erred in supposing that thisquestionwas decided in Albrovs. Lawson , as
that was an ordinaryaction. Moreover , the opinion in Worleyvs. Tuggle is a
dictum, because, as the opinion states, the testimony of the witness was " sig-
nallyconfirmed" by other evidence.

EXHIBITS .
a.This sectionisa substitutefor,and has,perhaps,materiallymodified, § 155of
the Code of 1854.
b. Itwas held that said? 155did not apply towritingson which actionsare founded ,
such as a note formoney , or a covenant to perform an act; that itonly applied
to exhibits, such as a deed reliedon by the plaintiffto prove his titleto land, in
an action against an adverse possessor; or an execution, and return thereon,
reliedon by the defendant to justifythe seizureof the plaintiff'sproperty; that
such exhibitsmight be used as evidence,though not reliedupon in pleading,and
need not be so used, though so reliedupon ; that they did not constitutepart of
the record, though filedand referredto in pleadings; that they could be made
part of the record only by a billof exceptions; and that no benefitcould result
from relyingon them in pleadings except that given by § 527 [588]. 3 Met ., 95 ;
18 В. М. ,633 ; Id., 824 ; 15 Id.,451 .
Itwas said,however,inHarman vs.Wilson,& c.,1 Duv .,327,thatan exhibitfiled
with a petitionin equity constitutedpart of the record, without being made so
by a billof exceptions. Itdoes not seem , however ,that the Code of 1854 made

hibits .
c. Section552 of thisCode requires, as a generalrule, that the proof in equitable
actionsshallbe made by depositionsor exhibits; with a proviso" that the gen-
uinenessof exhibitsmay be proved orallyin court,as heretoforeallowed by law."
Of course, exhibitsmay be proved, by depositions, in an equitableaction; or,
vivavoce,upon thetrialof an ordinaryaction. The onlyquestionisas toprov.
ing exhibits,vivavoce, in equitableactions.
In Christvs.Brashears,3 A. K. Mar.,170,itwas heldthatpaperswhich were not
referredto in the pleadings,though they were filedwith a depositionin the case,
could not be proved oretenus, upon the trialof a suitin chancery.
" The practiceinEngland,when a partydesirestoproveexhibitsvivavocein court,
on the hearing, is to procure an order for leave to do so. But to entitlethe
partytomake theproof, a copy of theordermust be servedon theclerkof the
opposite party, at leasttwo days before the hearing. (1 Smith'sPractice, 413.)
The practicehere has been to prove exhibitseitherat or beforethe hearing ; and
there can be no great objectionto indulging the proof, as well beforeas at the
hearing,as witnessesmay ,with most facility,be procuredto attend. But we
think noticeof the day when the proof willbe made , should be given to the op-
positepartyorhiscounsel." Cosby'sheirsvs.Wickliffe,7 Β .Μ ., 121.

the rightof a vendor to goodswhich have been purchasedfrom him withthe

LIABILITY FOR WRONGFUL ATTACHMENT .
A person,whose propertyhas been wrongfullyattached,may obtainredress-
1. By an action on the attachment-bond .
In thisaction,theplaintiffneed not allegemalice,orwant of probablecause; but
must allege that the attachment was " wrongfully obtained "-of which, as a
generalrule, a judgment dischargingtheattachmentisconclusiveevidence. (See cases
cited in the next following paragraph.) For the only exception to that rule,
known by the writerof thisnote, see Coopervs. Hill, 3 Bush , 219.
In thisaction, the plaintiffmay recoveractualdamages ; as forinjurysustainedby
being deprived of the use of his property, or by itsloss, or deterioration(Kaye
vs.Kean , 18 B. M. , 839); or losssustainedby hisprevention from performing a
contract(Carpentervs.Stevenson,6 Bush,259); and he may recoverthecostsof
the attachment,includinga reasonableattorney'sfee(Trapnallvs.McAfee ,3 Met.,
34); but it must appear that he has paid, or agreed to pay , to his attorneya
certainsum . Shultzvs.Morrison,& c.,3 Met.,98.

326. 1. General verdicts.

I. GENERAL VERDICTS .
a . In an actionupon a note,a verdictfinding" forthe plaintiffthe debt in the
petitionmentioned " authorizesthe clerk to enter judgment for the debt with
interest. Brannan & Smith vs.Foree'sadm'rs, 12 B. М. , 506.
6. In an actionon a note, the defendant pleaded usury, and the verdictwas : “ We
of the jury findfor the plaintiff." Held, that judgment was properlyrendered
in favor of the plaintifffor the amount of the note, with interest. Cooper vs.
Poston, I Duv ., 94 .
c. Petitionallegedthat the defendant broke the plaintiff'sclose, and assaultedand
beat the plaintiff. The defendant denied the allegations. The evidence proved
the assaultupon the plaintiff, but did not conduce to prove the breaking of the
close: held, that a verdictfinding the defendant guilty" as charged in the peti.
tion" was sufficient. Noel vs.Hudson , 13 В. М. , 204.
d. In an action for the value of work and labor, the jury have a right, in the
absence of all proof of the value of such labor, and from their own knowledge
of the ordinary transactionsand business of society, and especiallyfrom their
presumed knowledge of the value of labor, to find a verdictfor the priceof the
work done,and forwhich the actionwas brought. Baum vs.Winston,3 Met.,
129 .
e. If the law and factsbe submitted to the court,by consent, the court has allthe
rights and powers of a jury in determining facts, and assessingdamages (Baum
vs. Winston,3 Met ., 129); and itsfindinghas the same effectas the verdictof a
jury would have had . Mulholland & Bros. vs.Samuels, 8 Bush , 66.
2. SEPARATE -GENERAL VERDICTS .
f. The provisionsof thisCode,concerningseparategeneralverdicts, arecontained
in subsection5 of 317, and in sections326 and 327. Those provisionsare, so
faras the writer knows , without precedent. He believesthat theirobject is to
enable parties, and the court, in a case involving severalissues, to know upon
what grounds the verdictis based, without requiringthe detailsof a specialver-
dict. For example , suppose the issuesto be : I. Whether or not the defendant
was an infantwhen he purchased goods from the plaintiff; 2. Whether or not,
if he was then an infant, he ratifiedthe contract afterhe became of age ; and
3. Whether or not, though he was an infantwhen he purchased the goods, they
were necessaries, suitableto his estateand condition. A generalverdictforthe
plaintiffwould leaveituncertain,whetherthe jurywent upon the ground that
the defendant was of age when he purchased the goods,or upon the ground that
he ratifiedthe contractafterbecoming of age,or upon the ground that the goods
were necessaries; and,consequently, the courtcouldnot grant a new trial, if
therewere evidence conducing to sustainthe plaintiff'sallegationsas to any one
of theissues; though theremay have been a preponderanceof evidenceagainst
him upon thatissue,and though he may have failedto sustainhisallegations
upon the other issues. For example , the plaintiffmay have failedto sustainthe
allegationthat the defendant was of age, or the allegationthat the goods were
necessaries; but may have introduced evidence conducing to prove a ratification,
with a preponderance of evidence against him upon that issue; and the jury
might find a general verdictfor the plaintiff, believing, or professingto each
other the belief, that the goods were necessaries, notwithstanding the expressed
opinion of the court to the contrary; and the court could not set aside the ver-
NOTES - 5

66 CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SECS. 326, 330, 331.
dict, because, so far as the court can know , it may have been based upon the
ground that the defendant had ratifiedthe contract.
In the case supposed, the court must , on the motion of eitherparty,directthe jury
to return, with theirgeneral verdict, separate verdictsupon the questions, I.
Whether or not the defendant was of age when he purchased the goods ; 2 .
Whether or not, if he was an infantwhen he purchased the goods,he ratified
the contractafterbecoming of age; 3. Whether or not the goods were necessa-
ries; and , if the jury should find for the defendant upon the firstand second
issues, and for the plaintiffupon the third issue, itwould seem that the court
may setasidethe findingupon the thirdissue; and then, the generalverdict
being inconsistentwith the findingsupon the other issues, may render judgment
for the defendant, notwithstanding the verdict.

I. WHEN AN EXCEPTION IS NECESSARY .
a. No exceptionneed be takentoa finaljudgment upon an issueof law orof fact
(Coffmanvs. Wilson, 2 Met.,543); nor to a void order. Smithvs.Blakeman,8
Bush ,480 .
But,a party,in orderto availhimselfof an errorina preliminarydecision; as,in
givingor refusingto giveinstructionsto the jury(Kennedy& Bro.vs.Cunning-
ham ,2 Met.,538; Lettonvs.Young, Id., 558; Cox vs.Winston,3 Met.,577; Reed
vs.Commonwealth,7 Bush, 643); or,in admittingor excludingevidence(Terrill
vs.Jennings, I Met.,450; Russell'sheirsvs.Mark'sheirs, 3 Id.,42 ; Helburnvs.
Mefford,7 Bush, 169); or, in permittingthe amendment of a pleading(Helburn
vs.Mefford,7 Bush, 169); or,inoverrulinga motiontorequirea verificationof a
pleading (Cobb vs. Stewart, 4 Met ., 255), must except to the decision.
b. The Court of Appeals , in the absence of a billof exceptions, containingthe evi-
dence , and the instructionsto the jury, willpresume that the actionof the court
below was right. Brandiesvs.Stewart, I Met ., 395.
c. If no exceptionbe taken,therecannotbe a billof exceptions; and a deposition,
not excepted to, may be regarded as part of the record, without being made so
by a billof exceptions. Dehoneyvs.Sandford, 2 Bush, 169.
d. The plaintiff'sactionhaving been,notwithstandinga verdictforhim,errone-
ouslydismissed,upon the ground that hispetitiondid not statea causeof action,
the judgment was reversed, though there was no billof exceptions stating the
evidence. Johnson'sadm'r vs.LouisvilleCityRailway Co.,10 Bush , 231.
e.Depositions,takeninan actionagainstA ,arenotcompetentagainstB ,he having
been afterwardsmade a defendant; and he need not,and shouldnot,exceptto
them; but, ifthe plaintiffshouldofferto read them againsthim, he should
object; and , if the court overruled his objection, he should except. Kerr vs.
Gibson, 8 Bush , 129.
2. WHEN A PREVIOUS OBJECTION IS NECESSARY .
f . Subsection3 of 333 of thisCode expresslyforbidsan exceptionby a partytoa
decision made at the instanceof the adverse party, unlessobjectionshallhave
been made to the motion , offer, or requestof the adverseparty.
This provision conforms to what was regarded as settledlaw under the Code of
1854 (Kennedy vs. Cunningham , 2 Met ., 540 ; Long vs.Hughes , I Duv ., 388 ; Hel-
burn vs.Mefford,7 Bush , 169 ; Reed vs.Commonwealth, Id., 641), priorto the decis-
ion in the case of Postonvs. Smith'sex'r, 8 Bush , 589; in which itwas held that
the defendant'sexception to the giving of instructions,which were given on the
plaintiff'smotion , was available, though the defendant had not objected to the
instructions .

of court to the contrary. Kennedy vs. Cunningham , 2 Met ., 538.

3. TIME OF FILING BILL OF EXCEPTIONS .
c. Myers ' Code ,page 481, noter , citesthe case of Singleton'sadm'rsvs. Kennedy,9
B. M. , 222, and a MS . opinion, in support of the propositionthat if an order
allow exceptions to be filedon or before a particularday of the term, the filing
of them at a subsequent day of that term is sufficient, unless excepted to at the
time. Contra, Freeman vs.Brenham , 17 B. M. , 603 ; Tweedy vs.Commonwealth, z
Met ., 378 , Baileyvs.Villier, 6 Bush , 27 ; Meadows vs. Campbell, I Bush , 104. In
the lattercase itwas held that, if time be given untilthe firstday of the next
term to filea billof exceptions, it must be filedon that day , or time must then
be given to fileiton a subsequent day ; and that an order made on a subsequent
day ,allowing the exceptionsto be filednunc pro tunc,was unavailing.
If time be given to prepare and fileexceptions, the record must show the order.
In the absence of such an order, made at the trial-term , exceptionscannot be
filedat the next term nunc pro tunc. Vandever vs. Griffiths, 2 Met ., 425 .
A billof exceptionscannot be filednunc pro tunc,upon the judge's statement that
he had made an order allowing itto be filed. Lynch vs.Reynolds, 6 Bush , 547.
Time was given untilthe third day of the next term to filea billof exceptions;
on that day further time was given, without designating a day : held, that the
latterorder was void, and that the billof exceptions filedwas no part of the
record. Smith vs. Blakeman , 8 Bush , 479 .
A motionfora new trial,made attheMarchterm,1873,was continueduntilthe
Septemberterm,when itwas overruled,and timegivenuntilthefifthday of the
March term, 1874, to filea billof exceptions. On the second day afterthe
making of the order overrulingthe motion for a new trial, that order was set
aside, and the cause was continued to the March term, 1874, without taking any
noticeof that part of the former order which gave time untilthe third day of
the March term , 1874, to filea billof exceptions. At said March term , the
motion for a new trialwas again overruled, and on the sixthday of that term a
billof exceptionswas filed: heldto be in time. Harper vs.Harper, 10 Bush ,451 .
The Jeffersoncourt of common pleashas controlover itsjudgments during sixty
days. Can the courtextendthe time forfilingexceptionsbeyond sixtydays?
Bailey vs. Villier, 6 Bush , 27. At any rate, if time be extended beyond sixty
days, the exceptionsmust be filedon the day fixed, and not afterward. Id.
But if time be extended by that court beyond sixtydays, and the billof excep-
tionsbe filedwithin the time allowed, without objection, itwillbe regarded as
properlyinthe record. Downing vs.Bacon, 7 Bush,684.
The rightof a partyto objecttoa billof exceptions,becauseitisnottenderedin
propertime,isnot waived by reasonof assistancegivenby hiscounseloutof
courtinthepreparationof saidbill. Smithvs.Blakeman,8 Bush,480.
WHAT THE BILL OF EXCEPTIONS MUST SHOW .
1. As to evidence .
2. As to instructions .
I. AS TO EVIDENCE .
a. Accordingto thegeneralpractice,under theCode of 1854,a partyrelying,fora
reversal, onlyupon errorsastoinstructions,statedallof theevidenceinthebill
of exceptions; and this was necessary, according to a dictum in Vandever vs.
Griffith, 2 Met ., 425 .
This isforbiddenby subsection1 of 335,which requiresparties,insuchcases,to
statemerely " the materialfactswhich the evidence conduced to prove."

CIVIL CODE NOTES TO SEC . 335 . 69
b. If, however, the judge refuseto certifythatthe evidenceconduced to prove
facts, as statedin the appellant'sbillof exceptions; or, if the appellantwish to
appeal upon the ground that the verdict is not sustainedby the evidence, the
billof exceptions must state the evidence " in full" (2 335, subs. 2); and , of
course,the billmust show , in some manner , that itstatesthe evidence " in full."
It isusual, and best, to closethe statement of the bill, concerning evidence, with
the words ," and thiswas allof the evidence." But thisisnot necessary, ifthe
bill otherwise show that it states all of the evidence . In the case of Bracken
County Court us. RobertsonCounty Court, 6 Bush , 74, the court said: " Although
the billof exceptionsisinartificial,yet itdoes show the plaintiff'sevidence, the
agreed facts, and defendant's evidence, and then concludes, 'the court, having
heard the evidence, adjudged ;' from allof which itis most apparent thatitcon-
tains all the evidence ."
c. In the case of Walrath vs. Vilcy, I Bush , 268 (which was an ordinary action), it
was held that,though rejecteddepositions,which the defendant had offeredto read,
were copied in the record, and were noted as such by the clerk; and though the
billof exceptionsshowed that the defendant had excepted to the decisionof the
court, rejectingthem , the Court of Appeals would not considerthem ; because
neitherthey, nor the exceptionsto them , were incorporatedin the billof excep-
tions,or so referredto as to be made part thereof.
d. The testimony of a witnesshaving been erroneouslyexcluded, upon the ground
that itwas incompetent upon account of hisinterest, as shown by his statements
upon voirdire,which were contained in the billof exceptions, the judgment was
reversed, though the billof exceptionsdid not containallof the evidence.
Easley vs. Easley, 18 B. М. , 94 .

e. It was held, under the Code of 1854, that exhibitscopied in the record, but not

tra,as to exhibitsin equitableactions. Harman vs.Wilson,1 Duv .,327. See

f . A paper copied in the record,and certifiedby the clerkas an amendment offered,
without any referenceto itin an order rejectingit; or any other order recogniz-

exceptions, making it part of the record, cannot be noticed by the Court of

g . In the case of Young , McDowell & Co. vs. Bennett, & c., 7 Bush , 476, the clerk
having copied in the transcripta paper which he designated an amended peti-

any order of the court. The record shows that the amended petitionwas not
allowed to be filed: itremained , therefore, merely a privatepaper in the hands
of appellants."
h .Although the reason for excluding the evidence of a witness,by the courtbelow ,
be insufficient; yet,if there be no statement in the billof exceptionsof what
the witnesswould have proved,or what the party offeringhim believed he would
prove,the Court of Appeals cannot interpose. Bowler vs.Lane , 3 Met., 311.
i. Exceptions to depositions, forincompetency ,which do not appear to have been
acted on by the court below , are regarded by the Court of Appeals as waived .
Corn vs.Sims,3 Met ., 397 ; Lewis vs. Wright, 3 Bush , 311.

NEW TRIAL .
1. EFFECT OF GRANTING NEW TRIAL .
a. Itwas held (in an equitableaction) that, if a rehearing be improperly granted
to a defendant, againstwhom judgment had been rendered; and ifupon another
trial, judgment be rendered for him ,it must be reversed, with directionsto
enforce the firstjudgment . Phillipsvs. Skinner, 6 Bush , 662. See Babbittvs.
Woolley,& c.,3 Bush , 703.
2. WHEN MOTION FOR NEW TRIAL IS NECESSARY .
b. " On an issueand trialof factby a jury, a motion for a new trialisessentialto
correcterrorsgrowing out of the evidence or instructions, before an appeal can
be entertained." Humphreysvs.Walton,2 Bush,580; Detheragevs.Montgomery,
4 Id ., 46 .
But a motion for a new trialis unnecessary, in an equitableaction; or in an ordi-
nary action, in which the law and factsare submitted to the court. Union Ins.
Co. of Louisvillevs.Groom , 4 Bush , 289 ; overruling a dietum in Humphreys vs.
Walton , 2 Bush , 580.
3. GROUNDS FOR NEW TRIAL .
Subs . 1 and 2. Irregularityin proceedingsofthe court, and misconductofjury, or prevailing
party .
c. Judgment was obtained against a suretyto a note, who satisfiedit; at a subse-
quent term, judgment in the same action was rendered upon the same note
againsttheprincipals; a motionwas made by thelatterfora new trial, upon the
ground that the debt sued forhad been paid by the surety, and that theircoun-
selwas not present at the trial. Held, that to entitlethem to a new trialthey
should show that they had no knowledge of such payment before the trial,nor
could have learned the factby the use of reasonablediligence: that counsel was
not apprised of the fact, cannot availthem . Alexander,etc.,vs Lewis, I Met .,407.
d. In an actionfordamages ,the courtinstructedthejury that " they have the right,
each one, to setdown the sum he believesought to be recoveredby the plaintiff,
and add them alltogether, then divide the sum totalby the number 12, and find
that amount , 12th part, in damages , if they think proper to do so;" held, to be
erroneous. Allardvs.Smith, 1 Met ., 297. But see I Bibb, 389 ; Hardin , 586.
e. After the jury had retired, one of the witnesseswho had been examined on the
trialfor the appellant, was sentfor by them , admitted into the jury room , and
re-examined ,without the knowledge or consent of the court or the parties: held
that, although he was the witnessof the party seeking to reversethe judgment ,
and testifiedonly to the same matters in referenceto which he had testifiedon the
trial,itwas misconduct in the jury,and a new trialfor thisreason ought to have
been granted. Lutrellvs. Maysvilleand LexingtonR. R. Co., 18 B. M. , 294.

f . It issuch misconduct for one juryman to stateto the other jurorshis knowledge
of the subject-matter of the case to be decided,as willauthorizea new trial; but
thiscannot be establishedby the affidavitof a part of the jury. 3 Monroe , 415,
and cases cited .
Subs . 3. Accident or surprise.
g . " It does not appear that a party was prevented from defending the actionby
any unavoidable casualtyor misfortune, although one of his counsel was pre-
vented from attending the court by sickness, when there was a manifest lack of
diligenceon the part of such party in not attending the court or providing other
counsel." Landrum vs.Farmer, 7 Bush ,47 .
Subs .4. Excessive damages .
h . Upon a motion for a new trial, on account of excessivedamages and erroneous
instrctions, if the plaintiffseek to defeat the motion by a remittitur, itshould be
such as willshow clearlythat the damages retainedare sustainedby the proof.
Masterson us.Hagan , 17 BВ. М. , 332.
i. Upon a verdictfor$4,000, in an actionformaliciousprosecution, the judge
announced that he would grant a new trialunless the plaintiffwould accept
a verdict for $ 1,000, which the plaintiffdid under protest. The defendant
appealed,and theplaintifftooka cross-appeal:held,that" thejudgment iscer-
tainlyanomalous. Courtshave oftengrantednew trialson equitableconditions
applied to the applicant; but we know of no precedent for overrulinga motion
for a new trialon terms requiredof the party opposing it. In thisinstancethe
court itselfvirtuallyassessedthe damages , and thereby deprived each party of
his right to have an assessmentby'a jury. This seems to be error to the preju-
dice of the defendant, if he was entitledto a new trial, and to the plaintiff's
prejudice, ifthe defendantwas not entitledtoa new trial." Brown vs.Morris,
3 Bush ,81 .
j. In an actionfordamage causedby takingof plaintiff'sslaveto Ohio, whereby
he escaped,the court said that the value of the slavewas the best criterionof
damage ; and that, " although we might not concur with the jury as to the cor-
rectnessof their estimate, we do not feel authorized to disturb the verdictfor
that reason. McClain vs. Esham , 17 B. М. , 157.
The court refusedto set asidea verdictfor $ 4,000, in an action for slander; and
said that,in such cases, a new trialshould not be granted, " unlessthe damages
be so great as to strikethe mind at firstblush as having been superinduced by
passion or prejudice." Letton, & c., vs. Young , & c., 2 Met ., 560.
In L. & N. R. R. Co. vs. Fox , II Bush , 495, a verdictand judgment for the
plaintifffor$25,000,damages fora personalinjury,were setasideby theCourt
ofAppeals,upon the ground thatthedamages were excessive. See thatcase
fora discussionof the question.

TIME OF APPLYING FOR NEW TRIAL .
a. The three days allowed by 342, for an applicationfor a new trial, are three
juridicaldays, includingthe day on which the judgment was rendered. Long vs.
Hughes , I Duvall, 387 ; White vs. Crutcher, I Bush , 472. ,
2343. GROUNDS FOR NEW TRIAL MUST BE STATED IN WRITING , & C .
a. Itwas held, under theCode of1854, thattheCourtof Appealswould notnotice
an allegederror, which the appellanthad failedto specifyin his grounds fora
new trial, althoughtherecordshowed thathe had exceptedthereto,duringthe
trial. Hopkins vs. Commonwealth, 3 Bush , 480 ; Slatervs.Sherman, 5 Bush , 211 ;
L., C. & L. R. R. Co. vs.Mahony's adm'r,7 Bush , 238.
This Code has not changed the law in that respect; but it has imposed on the
appellantan additionalduty, viz: tospecifythegroundsupon which he reliesfor
a reversal, by an assignment of errors. Sections737, 739-
344. GROUNDS FOR NEW TRIAL DISCOVERED AFTER TRIAL -TERM .
a. After the term when a judgment isrendered,itcannotbe vacated,exceptin
the mode prescribedby sections344 [373],519 [580],or 520 [581],nor forany
causeexceptone of thosementionedinsection340 [369]or 518 [579]. Hocker
vs.Gentry, 3 Met .,469 .
2359 . JUDGMENT BY DEFAULT .
a. If the defendantadmit the plaintiff'sdemand ,or presentan immaterialissue
concerningit, but plead a counter-claimor set-offforan equal amount, the
plaintiffcanhave no judgment untilthepleaisdisposedof. Moorevs.Caruth-
ers,& c., 17 B. M. , 681 ; Geogheganvs.Ditto, 2 Met ., 438 .
b. There isno provisionof the Code, or ruleof practice, which authorizesthe
court, beforean actionisreadyfor finaltrial, to hear and tryit,and render
judgment infavorof one of theparties(whose righttorecoveranythingon the
finalhearingisnot admitted), on the mere probabilitythat he willbe then
entitledtothesum adjudgedinhisfavor. Mattinglyvs.Bosley,2 Met.,443.
c. In an actionagainsttwo of threejointand severalobligors,one ofthedefendants
pleadedpayment by the obligorwho was not sued; and,withoutdisposingof
thatissue,judgmentwas renderedagainsttheotherdefendantby default. Held,
thatthejudgment was erroneous. Rousevs.Howard, I Duv .,31.

TIME OF TRIAL OF EQUITABLE ACTIONS .
a . An equitableactiondoes not stand for trialat the term at which an answer is
filed, unless the plaintiffconsents that its statements may be taken as true.
Gruel vs.Smalley, I Duv ., 358.
b. Infant plaintiffsin an action in equity have no rightto a trialat the same term
at which theirreply, by guardian ad litem, is filedto the defendant's counter-
claim. Smith vs.Ferguson, 3 Met .,424 .
c. Upon a change of venue , if the papers are filedten days before the term of the
court , to which the case is removed , it stands for trialat that term , if it would
have stood for trial at the next term of the court from which it was moved .
Dale vs.Hays , 14 B. M. , 316; Gen. St., chap. 12, art. 1, § 7.
d.As to time of trialin actions against defendants constructivelysummoned , see
Harrisvs.Adams,2 Duv ., 141.

a. A decree is a judgment within the meaning of the Code . Hughes vs.Shreve, 3
Mct ., 547 .
b. " Itis clearlyno part of the judgment of the court to statewhether the process
has been executed or not ;" and a statement in the judgment that processhas

c. As to what ordersarefinal, see notes to 2 514.

againstanother. Burrus vs.Anderson, 3 Met ., 500.

summoned . Patton vs. Shanklin , 14 В. М. , 17 .

have been summoned , see note a to ? 363.

essaryparty,betweenwhom and theplaintiffthereisno litigation,a judgment
against" thedefendants" without discrimination,although erroneous, isa clerical

having been made in the court below ]. Cin., Ham ., and Dayton RailroadCo.
vs. Spratt, 2 Duv ., 4 .
e. If some of the defendants have been , and others have not been , summoned , a
judgmentagainstthe" defendants" appliesonlytothosesummoned. Clarkvs.
Finnell, 16 B. M. , 334; and see Kountz vs.Brown , & c., Id., 585.
f. In an actionagainsttwo defendants, on theirjointnote,itwas held that a
judgment byconfessionagainst" the defendant" was a judgment against both .

JUDGMENTS IN ACTIONS AGAINST SEVERAL DEFENDANTS .
a. Section 373 [402] of the Code " authorizesseveralseparateor jointjudgments
against one or more of the defendants, as the case may require. Bucklesvs.
Lambert, 4 Met ., 333 ; and see II Bush ,265.
b. If the jury finda jointverdictagainst severaldefendants, in an actionfor tort,
the court may set aside the verdict as to one , and render judgment for the

c. A releaseof onejointtrespasserdoesnotbar an actionagainsttheothers. Sellard
vs. Zomes, 5 Bush , 90.
If the Code authorizesseveralactionsagainst co-contractors, as the writer believes
itdoes (see ? 27, and note b thereto), there seems to be no good reason forhold-
ing that the releaseof one joint contractorbars an actionagainstthe others.
d. In an actionagainstco-contractors, theplaintiffmay dismissasto one,and take
judgment againstthe other. Quisenberryvs.Artis, I Duv ., 30.

ants, judgment may be renderedagainstthe others,withoutdisposingof that
issue; but a judgment by defaultcannot be renderedagainstone defendant,
pending an issuepresentedby another which extinguishesthe plaintiff'sdemand ;
as, a plea of payment . Rouse vs.Howard , I Duv ., 31.

FORECLOSURE OF A MORTGAGE IS FORBIDDEN .
a. The Code of 1854declaredthat," in the foreclosureof a mortgage,a saleof
the mortgaged property shall, in allcases, be ordered." ? 404.
The foreclosureof a mortgage is an order which bars the mortgagor's right to
redeem , and isinconsistentwith an order for the saleof the property to pay the
mortgage -debt. Hence , for the sake of consistency, thisCode , whilstitauthor-
izesa saleof the property, as did the Code of 1854, forbidsa foreclosure.
MORTGAGEE'S RIGHTS OF ACTION .
a. The Code , though itexpresslyauthorizesa mortgagee to sue the mortgagor for a
saleof the property, and for a judgment inpersonam (Chambersvs.Keene, 1 Met .,
294), without making any provisionas to the mortgagee's rightto sue forposses-
sion of the property, has not changed the common law concerning that right.
Brown vs. Phillips, 3 Bush , 656 ; Brookovervs.Hurst, I Met ., 665.
b. The assignorof a note executed to hisassigneea mortgage conditionedto be void,
ifthe assignor and payors of the note should pay the same held, that the
assigneelostrecourseupon the mortgage by failingto prosecutethe payors with
diligence. Carlylevs. Chambers, 4 Bush , 275.
SETTING OFF JUDGMENTS .
a. A defendantcannotsetoffa judgment inhis favoragainsttheplaintiff, tothe
prejudiceof theplaintiff'sattorneywho has a lienon thejudgment againstthe
defendant. Robertson& Clearyvs. Shutt,& c., 9 Bush , 659.
JUDGMENT UPON FAILURE TO PLEAD .
a. See notes to ? 126,pp .45 ,46 .
JUDGMENT IN ACTION FOR PERSONAL PROPERTY .
a. A ,having bailed to Ba strayhorse, which A had not posted; upon B's refusal
to return it,A , in an action to recover the horse, was held to be entitledto a
judgment ,not for the value of the horse, but fora returnof it, if to be had ; if
not , for its value , Borrom's adm'r vs. Landes , I Duv ., 299 .
In such case,a judgment forthe value,without allowing a dischargeof itby return-
ing the horse, iserroneous. Rogers vs.Bradford,8 Bush , 163.

1. SETTLEMENT OF DECEDENT'S ESTATES .
a .Jurisdiction. The statutewhich regulatesthe administrationof the estateof any

istrator, and where no duties or liabilitiescan devolve upon him . Thumb vs.

Section429 authorizesthe sale of land descended or devised to infantsas well as
adults; but, as to the former, according to the decisionin Gillvs. Givin'sadm'r,
4 Met ., 197, a sale, under thisproceeding, of more land than isnecessaryto pay
the debts of the decedent, isvoid. That decisionconformed to numerous decis-
ions of the Court of Appeals , concerning the effectof chapter 86 of the R. S.
It was held, however , inThorntonvs.McGrath , I Duv ., 349, that a failureto com-
ply with the provisionsof saidchapter86 renderedsaleof infant'sland only
voidable. See upon thissubjectsections489 to498 of thisCode, and especially
493 , subsection3 .

court, of the debtor's accounts as guardian ; and may recover compensation to
which he was entitled, but which was not claimed by him or his administrator.

broughtbeforethecourtasparties,but toallowthem toprovetheirclaimsbefore
the commissioners,and to distributethe proceeds of the trustestateaccording to
therightsofallconcerned." And suchwas thepracticeundertheCode of1854,
as to creditorsof decedent'sestates. Horner vs.Harris' ex'r, 10 Bush , 359. But
428 of thisCode,followingan actof 1839 (Loughborough'sDig.,240),declares
thatthe creditorsof the decedent, so faras known to the plaintiff, must be made
parties: 438 makes that provisionapplicableto trust-estates.

But an allegationthat the plaintiff" had an execution issued and returned 'no
property found 'by the sheriff" isinsufficient. The petitionshould have shown
that the executionwas directedto the sheriffof the county in which the judg-
ment was rendered , or in which the defendant resided or was summoned . Mad-
dox vs. Fox , 8 Bush ,402 .
g . Propertywhich plaintiffmay subjectupon returnof " no propertyfound .”

a. The provisionsof2444 [479] do not preventa surety,who has paida judgment,
fromhavingittransferredtohim,pursuantto theR. S., ch.97, 8. Alexander
vs.Lewis, I Met ., 407. See G. S., ch. 104, 29 .
b. Suretyin bond given pursuantto? 188cannotbe proceededagainstby motion.
Gay vs.Morgan ,4 Bush , 606 .
c. The provisionsof ? 446 [481], requiringa statement,in the noticeof a motion ,
of the nature and grounds of the motion , do not apply to a motion made pursu-
ant to 648 [716], upon a bond executed pursuant to ? 645 [713]. Smithvs.Wells'
adm'x , 4 Bush , 92 .
d. In a proceeding, by motion , againsta sheriffand his suretiesfor the recovery of
money collectedby him on execution, if the motion be not made in court, nor
enteredon the motiondocket,on theday specifiedinthenotice,themotionshall
be considered as abandoned . CivilCode, ? 447 [482]. And a judgment subse-
quently rendered isa nullity. Fostervs.Wade , 4 Met., 252. Notice of a motion
againsta sheriffwas givenforthefourthday of thenextterm. On thethirdday
of the term this order was made : " Notice filedand ordered to lieover." No fur-
ther stepswere taken at thatterm . At a subsequent term the plaintiff" renewed
hismotion forjudgment which isorderedtolieover;" and,at thesame term,
judgment was renderedby default. Held, thattherewas no motionpending,and
that the judgment was void. Id.
ARBITRATIONS AND AWARDS .
Subs . I. a. Manner of making and effectof submissiontoarbitration.
To authorizethereferenceof a controversytoarbitrationby ruleof court,concern-
ing which thereis no action pending , it isnecessarythat therebe an agreement
inwriting,filedand notedof record,oran entryon the recordstatingthematter
submitted. Civil Code, sec. 499, subdivisionI. A judgment upon the award ,
without these prerequisites, isunauthorized. Carson vs. Carson, 1 Met., 434.
Where thereisan actionpending,and a submissiontoarbitrationby ruleof court,
and the arbitratorsmake an award in respect to matters not involved in the
action, and therewas no submission of such matters in the mode prescribedby
law,the award can onlybe enteredas the judgment of the courtso faras it
decidesupon thecontroversyinvolvedintheaction. Ib.

Suitbroughttosettlea partnership. Beforeanswerfiled,agreementmade toarbi
trate,and suitdismissed. That no answer had been fileddid not prevent the
defendant from relying,before the arbitrators, upon the same mattersof defense
and counter-claimwhich he might havedone had theactionproceeded,inorder
to a settlementof all the matters of controversybetween the parties. Newton
us . West , 3 Met ., 24 .
Subs . 2. a. Qualificationsof arbitrators. A certificatethat they made oath according to law,
or made oaththat" theywouldfaithfullydischargethedutydevolvedon them
as arbitratorsand umpire," issufficient. Snydervs.Rouse, 1 Met ., 625.
If there is no proof to the contrary, itwill be presumed that the arbitratorswere
sworn and gave notice, from theirstatementsof those facts. Carson vs.Carson,
1 Met .,436 .
Subs . 4. a. Submissionby fiduciaries. An executor may submit a controversywith the
same effect, and the same freedom from liability,without as with the interven-
tion of the court. Overly'sex'rvs. Overly'sdevisees, Met ., 121.
Subs . 7. a. Rendering judgment on award ,made within ten days beforethe term, isa cler-
icalmisprision. See sections516, 517. Carsonvs. Carson, I Met ., 436.
Subs .8. Form and effectof award .
a. Merely formal objectionsto awards should be disregarded. The settlementof
controversiesby arbitrationis favored by law, and should be encouraged by
sustainingawards, notwithstanding they may be liableto technicaland formal
objectionswhich do not affectthe substantialrightsof the parties. Snydervs.
Rouse , Met ., 625 .
b. A mere error of arbitrators, eitheras to law or facts, is no ground for setting
asidetheiraward (Rudd , etc.,vs.Jones, etc., 4 Dana , 230) ; hence it isinadmissi-
ble to permit evidence to show such error. Overly'sex'rvs. Overly'sdevisees, I
Met ., 117 .
c. The partiesto an arbitrationhaving been present and heard before the arbitra-
tors, itis no objectionto the award that itdoes not state that the partieshad
noticeof the time and place the arbitratorsmet and proceeded to act. Newton
us. West,3 Met ., 24.
d. That the umpire was chosen by the arbitratorsbefore they proceeded to actunder
the reference,forms no objectionto the award . Newton vs. West, 3 Met ., 24.
452 to 469 . FORCIBLE ENTRY AND DETAINER ,
a. See notes to severalof those sections, repeatedbelow .
b. The provisionsof the Code of 1854, concerning forcibleentry and detainer,were
saidto be substantiallythe same as thoseof the act of 1810 (M. & B.,725).
Belchervs.Barrett, 4 Met ., 307.
That act"was framedtoaccomplishspeedyand cheapcountryjustice," and should
be liberallyand beneficiallyenforced. Swartsweldervs.U. S.Bank,xJ.J. M. ,

452,subs.2. WHEN AND FOR WHAT THE WRIT OF FORCIBLE ENTRY
LIES .
a.The writ of forcibleentry does not liefor a ferry, that being incorporealprop-
erty, nor for the shores and banks which are used as necessaryincidentsto the
ferry. Rees vs.Lawless, Litt. Sel. C., 184.
b. Residenceupon partof a tract,claimingthewhole,thoughpartbe not inclosed,
may givepossessionof thewhole tractwithinthe meaning of the statutecon-
cerning forcibleentry (Henry vs. Clarke, 4 Bibb, 426 ; Smith vs.Morrow , 5 Litt.,
210); and deeds may be introducedto show the extentof the possession,but

CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SEC . 452 . 85
not the rightthereto. Beauchamp vs.Morris, 4 Bibb, 312 ; Carpentervs.Shepherd,
Id., 501 .
And an entry upon such uninclosed land with the intent to clear and fitit for
cultivationwithout actuallyinclosingit, is a forcibleentry within the statute,
though occasionalchopping of wood upon itisnot. Humphrey vs.Jones,3 Mon. ,
262 .
A person may have a possession in fact of land without residing upon it, and
though itbe not cultivated, improved , or inclosed. Brumfield vs. Reynolds, 4
Bibb, 388 ; Wall vs. Nelson, 3 Litt., 395 ; Chilesvs.Stephens, 3 Mon. , 347 ; Young
vs. Ringo , 1 Litt., 226 .
c. The writ of forcibleentrycannot be maintained by an executorwith power to sell
land of which he did not have actual possessionat the time of the defendant's
entry. Prewittvs.Durham's ex'rs, 5 Mon. , 17.
d . It was held under the act of 1810 that a landlord could not maintain a writ of
forcibleentry or detaineragainstone who disseizedhis tenant (Poguz vs.McKee ,
3 Mar. , 128 ; Trabue vs. Talbott, 6J. J. Mar. ,602); but that the writ of forcible
entry might be maintained by the owner of land against one who disseizedhis
agent . Chilesvs.Stephens,3 Mar. , 340. But see now ? 452, subs. 3.
e. Itmay be maintained by a disseizoragainstthe disseizee,if he enterwithout the
disseizor'sconsent; or by a tenant againsthis landlord, if he enter without the
tenant'sconsent, though the landlord might have had the rightto maintain the
writ againstthe tenant. Chilesvs.Stephens, 3 Mar., 345-46.
f. The statuterelatingto forcibleentriesand detainersdoes not affectthe common
law rightof entry, when exercisedin a peaceable manner , except so far as the
statuteitselffurnishesa remedy toregainthe possession. A personhavingthe
right to enter, who makes an entry, in pursuance of that right, can only be
removed by a writ of forcibleentry and detainer. 7 J. J. Mar. , 604 ; Tuckervs.
Phillips, 2 Met ., 416.
452 , subs.3. WHEN WRIT OF FORCIBLE DETAINER LIES .
a. Continuance in possession,afterexpirationof term ,without refusalto surrender,
does not make the tenantguiltyof a forcibledetainer. Shepherdvs. Thompson , 2
Bush , 176; Thompson vs. Marsh , 4 Bush , 423 ; Allisonvs. Thompson , I Litt., 31.
b . But a tenant'sconduct may amount to a refusalto surrender, though there may
have been no formal demand of possession. Beynrothvs.Mandeville,5 Bush , 584.
c. Tenant's inabilityto surrender possessionat the expirationof his term, owing to
landlord's absence from the county, does not excuse the tenant'ssubsequent
refusalto do so. Gray vs.Nesbet,2 Mar. , 35.
d . Tenant's disclaimer to hold under the landlord , and refusal to surrender , author-
izesthe landlord to sue forforcibledetainer, though the term originallyagreed
on may not have expired. Batesvs.Austin, 2 Mar. , 270.
e. It was held in Wheatley vs. Price, 3 J. J. M. , 167, that forcibledetainer lies
againsta tenantwho entersunder an agreement to hold no longer than the use
of the placewillrepay him for repairsmade ,and who attempts to hold longer.
But itdoes not lieagainst a person holding under an agreement to hold and take
care of land forsixyears,with an understanding that he might purchase itwhen
able (Reeder & Klettevs.Bell, 7 Bush , 255); nor against the obligee in a title-
bond ,who assigneditto another,reservingthe rightof possessionuntilthe next
March , and agreeing to tillthe land in a certainmode . Goldsberryus. Bishop, 2
Duv ., 143 .
f . A's tenant,who attorned to B , who had purchased the land under a decretalsale,
refused to give possessionto B afterthe term expired: held, that he was guilty
of forcibledetainer. McMurtry vs.Adams ,3 Bush , 70.

g. The writmay be maintainedthough the leasebe voidwithregardtothe land-
lord'sobligationsto let the tenant remain in possession. Haynes vs.Adams ,3
Mar. , 149 .
h . An acquittalupon a charge of forcibledetainerdoes not bar a writ for a subse-
quent forcibledetainer. Shepherdvs.Thompson , 2 Bush , 176.
i. Though the leasepurportto have been signedby the landlordand tenant,its
executionby the landlordneed not be proved . Gray vs.Nesbet,2 Mar. , 35.
WARRANT FOR FORCIBLE ENTRY OR DETAINER .
a. In a warrant forforcibledetainer, the relationof landlord and tenant must be
alleged, or in some form appear on itsface. Powers vs.Sutherland, I Duv ., 151;
Taylor& Speedvs.Monohan , 8 Bush ,238.
b. Though a forcibleentry and a forcibledetainerare distinctoffenses, they may be
charged in the same warrant ; and the jury may find the defendant guiltyof
either(Swartzweldervs.U. S. Bank , IJ. J. Mar. , 44 ; Cammack vs.Macy , 3 Mar. ,
296); and a charge of a forcibleentryor detainerissufficientto support a verdict
for either (Carpentervs. Shepherd, 4 Bibb, 501); or for both of those offenses
(McBrayer et al. vs. Wash , 6 J. J. Mar. , 464); and , upon a warrant charging a
forcibleentry, a verdict that the defendant is guilty of the forcibleentry com-
plained of is sufficient, without finding him guiltyof a detainer(Brumfieldvs.
Reynolds,4 Bibb, 388); but a verdictfor a forcibledetainerisnot supported by a
charge of a forcibleentry (Gayle vs. Overton, I J. J. M. , 550); nor by a charge
that the defendant forciblyentered on the land, and that he detainsit, without
any allegationapplyingtheforceto thedetention. Lewisvs.Stith,2 Litt.,294.
r. Omission to statethe name of a county in which the land liescannot firstbe
taken advantage of by objection in the Court of Appeals . Rowe , & c., vs.
Powell'sheirs, 4 J. J. Mar. , 153.
d. The writ need not statethe boundaries of the land. A generaldescriptionof it
is sufficient. Moore vs. Massey, 3 Litt., 296.
e. Pendency of another writ cannot be pleaded in abatement ,but the court may
direct an election. Boucher vs. Williamson , I Dana , 227 .
f. A writisnot barredby a judgment dismissinga defectivewrit. Hanelvs.Law-
rence, I Mar. , 330 .
g . Parties. It was held under the act of 1810 that, though coparcenersmight and
should join in a warrant for forcibledetainer, the warrant should not be abated
for theirfailureto do so (Jonesvs. Overton, 4 Bibb, 334); that tenantsin common
cannot join in a warrant forforcibledetainer(Rogersvs.Turley,4 Bibb,355); and
thať a jointwarrant in favor of three persons, only two of whom had a causeof
action, could not be maintained . Thomas vs.Jones, 2.Mar. , 356 ; Haynes vs.
Adams , 3 Id ., 149 .
NOTICE OF WRIT .
a. A defendant,having appeared on the trialin the country, cannot, upon a tra-
verse,make any objectionconcerningnoticeof thewrit. Philipsvs.Harmon,I
Dana , 468 .
b. R sued out a warrant of forcibleentry and detaineragainstM. Cappeared and
consented to become a defendant, but the warrant was not amended so as to
include him . The inquest found M and C both guilty, whereupon C alone
entered a traverse. The circuit court , on motion of R , dismissed the traverse,
and awarded restitutionagainstC. Held that the proceedingswere irregular.
The award againstC was improper, as he was no party to the warrant, and the
inquestastohim was void. The proceedingsshouldhave been remanded tothe
magistrate,thatrestitutionmight have been had againstM. Crawfordvs.Roch-
ester, 4 Bibb,203-

INQUEST AND JUDGMENT .
a. The issueupon a writ of forcibleentry or detainerrelatesmerely to the rightof
possession. Titlepapers showing that the prosecutor, or the defendant, has the
title, legalor equitable, or the rightof entry, are irrelevant, unlessfor the pur-
pose of showing extent of possession. Mansfieldvs.Dun , 2 Bibb, 582 ; Smith,
& c.,vs.Dedman , 4 Id., 192 ; Beauchamp vs.Morris, 4 Id., 312 ; Mattox vs.Helm ,
5 Litt., 185 .
6. That one ,againstwhom the prosecutorisnot entitledto recover, isjoined in the
writ, isno ground for non -suit. Alexandervs.Reed & Myers, 3 Mon. , 246.
c. Verdict of the jury," the defendants not guilty," issufficientlyexplicit. Belcher
vs.Barrett,4 Met ., 307.
d. See further, as to inquest, notes to ? 454.
TRAVERSE .
a . The absence of a judgment upon a verdictin the country, on a writ of forcible
entry and detainer, isnot ground for dismissinga traverse. Belchervs.Barrett,
4 Met ., 307 .
b. In Wayman vs.Taylor, I Dana , 527,itwas held that a recitalin a traverse-bond ,
that an obligor had traversedthe inquisition, estopped him from denying that
fact; and that,the bond having been duly executed, and the papers returned to
the circuitcourt, the proceedings may be presumed , nothing appearing to the
contrary, tohave been regular; and that,ifno traversebe foundin thepapers,
one may be filednunc pro tunc,on motion of the traverser. But see,contra,Bur-
chettvs. Blackburn , 4 Bush , 553 .
c. The traverserneed not sign the traverse. Jones vs.Skiles, I Mar. , 54.
d . A traverse-bond executed by a surety, though not by the traverser, issufficient.
Smith vs. Turley, 3 Bibb, 188.
e. The substitutionof a new traverse-bond , with other surety,does not releasethe
suretyin the firstbond . Ashby vs.Sharp, 1 Litt., 156.
f. If, upon a traverse, the justicestayproceedings, and return a bond , it must be
presumed that the bond was executed before and approved by him , though he
omit so to certify. Smith vs.Dedman , 2 Bibb, 575 ; Stillwellvs.Harris, 1 Mar. ,
342 .
g . The requisitionas to returnof the proceedings to the circuitcourt, within ten
days, is merely directoryto the justice; and his failureto comply should not
prejudicethe rightsof the parties. Wayman vs. Taylor, I Dana , 527.
h. If the originalwritbe returnedto the circuitcourtand lost, thejusticemay
supply itby a sworn or certifiedcopy . Logan vs.Smith, 2 Mar. , 53.
TRIAL AND JUDGMENT UPON TRAVERSE .
a. The circuitcourt may quash a defectivewrit at any stageof the trial. Powers
vs. Sutherland , I Duv ., 151 .
b. Upon an appeal from a justiceof the peace, the same actionmay be triedanew ,
but no new actioncan be tried. Burbage vs.Squires, 3 Met ., 79. The same rule
appliesto a traverse. The proceedingscannot be amended by introducing new
plaintiffs. Powers vs.Sutherland, I Duv ., 151.
c. If thetraverserfailtoappearon thecallingof thecause,judgment may be ren-
dered againsthim by default. Dibblevs.Porter, I Duv ., 190.
d . Upon the traverse, though by the defendant, the burthen of proof is on the
prosecutor (Beauchamp vs. Morris, 4 Bibb, 312); and he shallhave restitution
according to the proof, though it be for lessthan he demands , and lessthan the
jury in the country gave him . Atchleyvs.Lathan, 3 Mar. , 164.

e. Upon a traverse, the defendant cannot be found guiltyof a differentoffensefrom
that of which he was found guiltyby thejury in the country (Cammack vs.Macy ,
3 Mar. , 297 ; St.Clairvs.Sanders, 3 J. J. Mar. , 303); nor can the defendant be
foundguiltyof any offense, upon thetraverseof an inquestwhich failedtofind
him guiltyof any offense. Thus , upon a warrant fora forcibledetainer, thejury
inthecountry,insteadoffindingthedefendantguiltyofthedetainer,found" the
right of possessionin the" prosecutor. A verdict, upon the traverse, that the
defendantwas guiltyof the forcibledetainercomplainedof in thewarrantwas
held to be void. Todd vs.Bates,3 Bibb, 100.
f . A traversetakes issue on the truth of the inquest, and is a virtualwaiver of
objectionsto itsform , to informalitiesof the judgment of the justice, and irreg-
ularitiesin the proceedings beforehim , not affectingthe meritsof the case. I
Mar. , 54 ; 2 Bibb, 434 ; 1 J.J. Mar. , 45 ; 3.Id., 168 ; 1 Dana , 468.
g. Judgment of restitutionmay be given againstpersonsengaged inmaking the
unlawful entry,but who did not detainthe premises afterward,aswell as against
thosewho continued in the occupation of them . Young vs.Ringo , 1 Litt., 225.

a. An order restrainingrestitutionshould not be made because the defendant has
recovereda judgment inejectmentagainsttheprosecutor. Dedman vs.Smith,2
Mar. , 260 .

a. Ifthewarrantbe invalid,and the proceedingsupon itbe sustainedonlyby con-
struingthedefendant'sappearingand traversingthe findingof thejury,intoa
waiver of irregularity, the limitationshould be counted back from the appear-
ance . Humphrey vs.Jones,3 Mon. , 263 .
2470 to 472. SETTLEMENT OF ACCOUNTS OF FIDUCIARIES BY COUNTY
COURT .
a.A guardianmay maintainan actioninequityagainsta formerguardianforsettle-
ment of his accounts,notwithstanding a referenceof them toa commissioner,by
the countycourt. CommonwealthforHoopervs.Henshaw,2 Bush,286.
b. The clerkhavingcertifiedthata copy of a settlementwas a truecopy of settle-
ment made " with theMercercountycourt,which ison filein thisoffice," the
copywas held to be competentevidence, though the settlementpurportedto

1. Mandamus .
WRITS OF MANDAMUS AND PROHIBITION .
a. Nature of thewrit. " Mandamus isnot now , as formerly, a high prerogativewrit,
emanating from the grace or favorof any one ; but itisa statutorywrit,or order
of a courtof competentjurisdiction,and, ina propercase,is tobe grantedon
the motion of any party aggrieved." Maddox vs.Graham & Knox , 2 Met ., 70.
And itisa peremptory,and not an alternative,writ. Cumberland& OhioR. R.
Co.vs.The Judge oftheWashingtonCountyCourt, 10 Bush,564.
b. When mandamus istheproper remedy.
A quarterlyjudge,havingdismissedan appealon theground thathe had no juris-
diction,itwas heldthatmandamus did notlieagainsthim, I,becausethedis-
missalwas a judicialdetermination; 2,becausethe plaintiffcouldappealtothe
circuit court . Goheen vs.Myers , 18 B. M. ,423 .

CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SECS . 474 Το 479 . 89
Mandamus does not lieagainstcityauthoritiesto compel the issuingof a tavern-
license . It cannot be used to correct a decision of the defendant as to a matter
concerning which he has a discretionarypower . Cityof Louisvillevs.Kean , 18
В. М. , 9 .
It does not lieto compel a county courtto pay an unliquidateddemand ; but a suit
to ascertainthe amount of demand is proper (Garrard County Courtvs.McKee ,
II Bush , 239); and an ex parteorder of the circuitcourt allowing a claim of jail-
guards, to be paid by the county court, being only prima facieevidence of the
sum due ,mandamus does not lieto compel the county court to make a levy there-
for. Judge and Justicesof Hickman County Courtvs. Moore, & c., 2 Bush, 108.
Itdoes not lieagainst the officersof a privatecorporation, such as the College of
Physicians and Surgeons of Louisville. Cook vs. Collegeof Physicians, & c., 9
Bush , 541 .
It liesagainst cityauthorities, to compel the repairingof streets(Hammar vs. City
ofCovington,3 Met.,494); and againstcityor countyauthorities, to compel the
levying of taxes for the payment of [ascertained] debts of the city or county
(JusticesClark County Court vs. The Paris, & c., Turnpike Co., II B. M. , 143 ;
Anderson County Courtvs.Stone & Son, 18 Id., 848 ; Maddox and othersvs. Gra-
ham & Knox , 2 Met ., 64); or to compel the county court to qualify a deputy
sheriff. Applegatevs.Applegate,4 Met .,236, citing3 B. М. , 198.
The Court of Appeals may enforceitsmandates by ruleand attachment. See 762,
and notes thereto .
c. Proceedingsin actionsfor mandamus . The defendant may demur to a petition
which does not state a cause of action. Cooke vs.Collegeof Physicians, & c., 9
Bush , 545 .
Mandamus againsta corporationdoes not abate by the electionof a new board of
directors(Maddox vs. Graham & Knox , 2 Met ., 71); and does not assume the
character of a proceeding against individuals until it becomes necessary to
enforce the judgment of the court by rule and attachment. City of Louisville
vs. Kean , 18 В. М. , 13 .
The plaintiffin an action for mandamus may traversethe defendant'sreturn and
produce proof. Maddox vs. Graham & Knox , 2 Met ., 68.
In an action for mandamus , to compel the county court to levy money and pay for
buildinga bridge,a counter-claim cannot be setup by the county court to recover
money paid on the contract; but, as a defense, a defectiveperformance of the
work may be reliedon as a reason why the county court should not pay for the
work done . Anderson County Courtvs.Stone& Son, 18 B. M. , 853.
To an action against cityauthorities, for the purpose of compelling payment of
bonds issuedby the cityto a R. R. Company , and assignedby itto the plaintiff,
the company isnot a necessaryparty. Maddox vs.Graham & Knox , 2 Met ., 67.
A court of equity cannot enjoin an officerfrom doing an act which a court of law
has, by writ of mandamus , required him to do . Cumberland and Ohio R. R.
Company vs.The Judge of the Washington County Court, 10 Bush , 564.
If the court have jurisdictionto award a mandamus ,though itmay errin doing so,
a refusalto obey the mandamus is a contempt of court, which justifiesthe
imprisonment of the party; and no actionismaintainableforsuch imprisonment .
Kaye vs. Kean , 18 B. M. ,847 .
2. Writs of prohibition.
a . A writ of prohibitioncannot issuefrom the Court of Appeals . Sasseenvs.How-
ard, 18 B. М. , 674.

505. REVIVOR OF PERSONAL ACTION AGAINST REAL REPRESENTATIVES .
a. If there be no personalrepresentative,a suitin equitymay be maintained against
the heirs or devisees alone; and where they are infantsitshould be allegedand
proved eitherthat personal estatehas been distributed, or that real estatehas
descended, to them ; and if the claim can be satisfiedin no other way than by a
saleof theirrealestate, the sale should be made under the supervisionof the
chancellor, and not by the sheriffunder execution. Hagan vs. Patterson, 10
Bush , 441 .
b. If a defendant die pending an action, and no personal representativequalify, it
may , under 505 [566], of the CivilCode ,be revivedagainstthe heirsalone; but
the remedy is exclusivelyequitable, and the action, if at law , should be trans-
ferredto equity, and the proper amendments made to the pleadings. Hagan vs.
Patterson, 10 Bush , 441.

516, 517, 519. CLERICAL MISPRISIONS .
1. What are clericalmisprisions.
2. What are judicial errors.
3. Proceedings to correctclericalmisprisions.
1. WHAT ARE CLERICAL MISPRISIONS .
It is a clericalmisprisionto render judgment fortoo much interest, or forcostof
protest not claimed in the petition(Wilson vs.Barnes, 13 B. M. , 332 ; Clarkvs.
Finnell, 16 1d., 334 ;Johnson vs. Bank of Kentucky, 2 Duv ., 521); or not to credit
on the judgment a payment admitted in the petition(Dodds vs. Combs, & c., 3
Met ., 28 ; Hieronymous vs. Mayhall, & c., 1 Bush , 510 ; Long vs. Gaines, Berry &
Co.,4 Bush , 353); or,in an actionupon severalnotes,not to embrace one of them
in a judgmentby default(Smithvs.Mullins,3 Met., 182); or to givejudgment
forA and B on a note allegedto have been sold by A to his co-payee , B , and to
belong to B (Oldham vs. Brannon , 2 Met., 302); or to give judgment for A on a
note,he and B having statedin an amended petitionthatA had assignedthe

92 CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SECS. 516, 517, 519 .
note to B (Coopervs. Poston, I Duv ., 92); or to render a judgment against" the
defendants," two of whom were nominal defendants (Cin., Ham . and Dayton R.
R. Co. and Dayton and Mich .R. R. Co. vs. Spratt, 2 Dur ., 4); or to render judg-
ment upon an award returnedto the courtwithin ten days before the commence-
ment of the term (Carson vs. Carson, I Met ., 434); or to render judgment ,
prematurely, against a defendant constructivelysummoned (Buckner vs. Bush ,
I Duv ., 394 ; Harris vs.Adams , 2 Id., 141); or to render judgment againstpart
of the defendants, upon trial, before all of them are summoned . Breedingvs.
Stamper, 18 В. М. , 175.
In an action brought in the county in which the defendant resided,he was sum-
moned in that county, but not in time, and out of that county in time, for judg-
ment at the next term; and the courtsaidthata judgment by defaultwould
have been a clericalmisprision. Raymon vs.Reed, 16 В. М. ,350.
AS TO INFANTS : It was held, under the Code of 1854, that rendering judgment
againstan infant, before an answer by hisguardian ad litem,was a judicialerror.
Pond vs.Doneghy , 18 B. M. , 558. Consequently, the infant could not obtain
reliefagainsta judgment so rendered, except by an appeal. See subdivision2
of 517- the objectof which isto enable infantsto obtain relief, in such cases,
by motion in the courtbelow , without requiringthem to make themotion within
the firstthreedays of the next term. See 2 519.
2. WHAT ARE JUDICIAL ERRORS .
It is an error of the court to render a judgment afterthe plaintiff'sdeath, and
before an order of revivor is served on the defendant (Amyx vs.Smith'sadm'x,
I Met ., 529) ; or to render a personaljudgment againsta defendant constructively
summoned (Payne vs. Witherspoon, 14 B. M. , 270); or to render a judgment sus-
taining an attachment againsta defendant constructivelysummoned , before the
filingof an affidavitby or for the plaintiff, as isrequiredby the Code (Payne vs.
Witherspoon, 14 В. М. , 270); or to sustainan order of arrestissued by the clerk
before the filingof an affidavitby or for the plaintiff, as isrequiredby the Code
(Pauer vs.Simon, 6 Bush , 514); or to render judgment against a defendant who
has not been summoned (Long vs.Montgomery, 6 Bush,394); overrulingRobin-
sonvs.Mobley, I Bush, 196; or torenderjudgment againsta defendantwho is
summoned outof, and who did not residein, the countyinwhich theactionis
brought. Ruby vs Grace, 2 Duv ., 540; Dyas vs.Lindsey, 4 Bush , 349.
In Pottingervs.Mayfield, 14 B. M. , 647, which was an actionagainsttwo defendants,
itwas held tobe a clericalmisprisionto renderjudgment againsta defendant
who did not residein, and who was summoned outof, the countyinwhich the
actionwas brought . See contra, Ruby vs. Grace, 2 Duv ., 540.
In an actionforpersonalproperty,possessionof which theplaintiffhas obtained
pursuantto the provisionsof the Code, a judgment forthe defendantforthe
value of the property, without allowing the plaintiffto dischargethe judgment
by returningtheproperty,was heldtobe judiciallyerroneous. Rogersvs.Brad-
ford , 8 Bush , 163 .
If a partymove fora continuance,or objecttoa trial,beforethecaseisreadyfor
trial, it is an errorof the court, and not a clericalmisprision, to overrule the
objectionor motion . Raymon vs.Reed,16 В. М. ,344 ; Hedger vs.Downs ,2 Met.,
162 ; Mattinglyvs. Bosley, 2 Met ., 444 ; Smith vs.Ferguson, 3 Met ., 424.
If a questionbe presentedto, and decidedby, the court, thejudgment isnot a
clericalmisprision,but a judicialerror. Dodd vs.Combs,3 Met.,29,30; and see
casescitedin lastparagraph.
In Deshongvs.Cain, I Duv .,309,itwas heldthata judgment infavorof A against
B , for$300, upon confirmationof the reportof a commissionerto whom the

CIVILCODE- NOTES TO SECS. 516, 517, 518, 519. 93
case had been referred, and whose report showed that Bowed A $300, was an
errorof the court, and not a clericalmisprision. Quere.

a. An actionisnot an availableremedy to correcta clericalmisprision. It cannot

Bush , 308); or upon an appeal if the motion be overruled. Dodds vs. Combs, 3
Met ., 29 , 30 .
b. Petitionby A and B againstC , on his note toA ,allegedthat ithad been assigned
to B. A jointjudgment was rendered in favor of A and B , which B moved to
correct: held, that he must give noticeto both A and C. Oldham vs.Brannon , 2
Met ., 305 .
c. There is no limitationof time for correctinga clericalmisprisionexcept the pre-
mature renditionof judgment . Smith vs. Mullens, 3 Met ., 182.

4. Jurisdiction of Court of Appeals .
Sections 15 and 16 of the Code of 1854, gave jurisdictionto the Court of
Appeals , " where the actionor proceeding is for the recovery of money or per-
sonalproperty and the matter in controversydoes not exceed $ 100 in value; or,
in behalfof the defendantwhere the judgment of the inferiorcourtisagainst
him for money or personal property, not exceeding in value $ 100." Those sec-
tionswere so amended by an act of 1857, as to substitute$ 50 for$ 100.
a . It was held under the Code , that the Court of Appeals had jurisdiction, if the
debt and interestexceeded $ 100 at the commencement of the action(Orth& Wal-
lacevs.Clutz'sadm'r, 18 B. M. , 223 ; Bakewellvs. Howell, 2 Met ., 268); or if the
aggregate of severalnotes exceeded $ 100. Bakewellvs.Howell, 2 Met ., 268.
b. Under the act of 1857, itwas held that the Court of Appeals had jurisdictionon
an appealby theplaintiff,ifthematterincontroversyexceeded$50; thoughthe
defendant could not appeal if the judgment against him was for lessthan $ 50
(Brandiesvs.Stewart,I Met.,395); unlessitwas reducedbelow thatsum by a
set-offor counter claim. Tiptonvs. Chambers, I Met ., 565.
c. If severalpersonsbe jointlysuable,on one contract,forseparateamounts,the
aggregate claimed againstalldetermines the jurisdiction. Wilde vs.Haycraft, 2
Duv ., 309.

in an actionforfalseimprisonment . Reviletal. vs.Petit, 3 Mct ., 321.

erence to the sum of money in controversy. Caskeyvs.Lewis, 15 B. М. , 27.

1. ERRORS MUST BE ASSIGNED SPECIFICALLY .
2. ERRORS MUST BE PREJUDICIAL TO SUBSTANTIAL RIGHTS OF APPELLANT .
Errors must be assignedspecifically.
An act of 1799 (M. & B. , 132) declared that " every person, on obtaininga super-
sedeas,shallassigninwriting,theparticularerrororerrorsintherecordon which
he means torely,and which shallbe filedwith theclerkof theCourtofAppeals,

held-
1.Every assignmentof errorshouldmake out a particularcaseor pointtowhich
the defendant can answer ; and errorswhich are properly assignablebelow on a
writ of errorcoram vobis,and not assignedthere,cannot be noticedon an appeal.
Lansdale vs.Finley, Har ., 151.
2. Assignment of errorswill not be regarded in the Court of Appeals , unless they
have been brought before the court below for adjudication. Payne vs.Mattox,
1 Bibb , 164 .
3. A defendantwho suesout a writof error,willnot be permittedto assignfor
error the incorrectnessof the decree as to a defendant who did not join in the
writ. Oldham vs.Rowan , 4 Bibb, 544.
4. If the writ of errorrelatesexclusivelyto the originaljudgment , and the assign-
ment of errorsto the commissioner's reportunder the occupying claimant law,
the errorsassigned cannot be noticed, nor can the writ be amended to make it
applytotheerrors: itisonlyincaseswhere thewritisdefective, thatitcanbe
amended . Smith vs.Hornback, 3 Mar. , 379.
5. Where theassignmentof errorsquestionstheright, and not themode of relief,
the court willonly examine the right. Breckinridgevs. Todd , 3 Mon. , 56.
6. An assignment of error, that the court erred in overrulingthe motion for a new
trial,bringsbefore the court every question made in the grounds of the motion
fora new trial, though made and decided before trial. McAlexander us. Wright,
3 Mon. , 191.
7. An assignmentof erroringeneralterms," thatthecourtbelow erredindismiss-
ing the complainant'sbill," willembrace an errorin dismissingitabsolutely,
when itshouldhave onlybeen withoutprejudice. Payne vs.Richardson, 7 J.J.
Mar. , 240 .
The Court of Appealswillgive a liberalconstructionto assignmentsof error,so
as to reach the justiceof the case. Ibid .
8. " Now , though,when a judgment,or decreeor judicialorder,iserroneouson
the merits,and in no other respect,a generalassignment may not only be proper,
but would be the only one thatcould be made ; yet, whenever there isany
otherand particulargroundsof objection,itmay be speciallyassigned; and itis
theduty of the complainingparty,both tohisadversaryand tothiscourt,ifhe
intendstoavailhimselfof it,topointitoutby a specialassignmentinwriting."
Harvey vs. Cammack , 6 Dana , 247 .

isfinallydisposed of to that extent, and no pleadings can be filedin the circuit

cution of a contract for the sale of a tractof land; no allegationof defectof

NOTE TO FORM 1, PAGE 177.
1.Unsealed agreements(excepting,perhaps, some agreementsof corporations) having
been placedby statuteupon the same footingas sealedagreements(Actof 1812,More-
head & Brown ,page 343 ; GeneralStatutes,ch.22, & 2)-
First. In an actionon a promissory note,itisunnecessary to aver a consideration..
Secondly. The word writingseems sufficientto describe, not only a promissory note, but
any written contract, whether under sealor not (excepting, perhaps, some contractsof

2. 7,ch .22, of the General Statutes, requiring a statement of the considerationof an

NOTE TO FORM 2, PAGE 177.
The editorsof the Code concur in the beliefthat no replyto theplea of payment is
necessary .
The common law not only requiredpartiesto form an issue,but requireda formal tender
and acceptance of the issue. As a general rule, if A B filedan affirmativeplea, he was
bound to conclude itwith " and thishe is ready to verify;" and , if CD traversedthat
plea, he was bound to concludewith " and thishe praysmay be inquiredof by the
country;" and then A. B. was requiredto filean additionalplea, sayingthat " the
plaintiff[or defendant] doth the like." The Code does not requirethose ceremonies. It
expresslyforbidsthe lastnamed plea- the similiter. (@ 112.) It requirespartiesto form
an issue. ( 114.) But whether the issueisformed by thedenialof a factin the peti-
tion, and an averment of the factin the answer, or conversely, seems to be immaterial.
This view is sustainedby the opinions in Davis vs. Dycus, 7 Bush , 9 ; and Carter,
Fisher& Co. vs. Goodman , II Bush , 233 ; and even by I Chitty'sPleadings(editionof 1837,
sidepage, 589) in which it issaid: " When there is a complete issuebetween the parties,
viz: a directaffirmativeand negative; as, if the generalissuebe pleaded; or the defend-
ant simply deny some material fact alleged in the declaration, as where the plaintiff
declareson an award , and the defendant pleads no such award , the plea should conclude
to the country. This rule equallyprevailswhether the affirmativebe firstin
the pleading, and the negative subsequent, or viceversa; and therefore, though the neg-
ative be assertedby the plaintiff, and the affirmativeby the defendant, as where the
plaintiffinhis declarationallegesa breach of non -payment of a sum of money on a par-
ticularday, or in not repairing, & c., and the defendant pleads solvitad diem, or that he
did repair, the plea should conclude to the country."
*
*帝
The only replyrequiredby the common law to a plea which concluded " to the coun-
try" was the similiter, which , as has just been stated, isforbiddenby the Code .
It should, however ,be borne in mind that the Court of Appeals has said,in effect,that
affirmativeallegationsof a petitioncannotbe traversedby inconsistentaffirmativeallegations
of an answer . Corbinvs. Commonwealth ,2 Met ., 380.

NOTE TO FORM 12, PAGE 179.
According to the common law , a plea of a defendant in an actionon a bond , that" it
isnot his deed," not only threw on the plaintiffthe burthen of proving the executionof
the bond , but authorizedthe defendant to prove factsshowing that, though he had exe-
cuted the bond ,itwas void in law- such as coverture, intoxication, unsoundness of mind ,
or fraud as to the executionof the bond . But itseems that the Code , which requiresthe
defendant to statethe factswhich constitutehis defense, forbidsthatgeneralissue; and
that the defendant must specificallytraverseallegationsshowing that he executed the
instrument, or must statefactsshowing that, though he executed the instrument in fact,
itis void in law . This seems to be the meaning of the expressionin Trustees, & c., vs.
Fleming'sex'r, 10 Bush , 237, that" the plea of non estfactum is an affirmativeplea, and
cannotbe made inmerelynegativelanguage."

NOTE TO FORM 17, PAGE 179.
The rejoindermight have omitted the last sentence, and thus have thrown on the

NOTE TO FORM 21 , PAGE 180 .
As has been shown in the note on page 1, unsealedwritings,generally,have been placed
on the same footing as sealedwritings. According to the common law, a sealestopped
the obligorfrom impeaching the considerationof his bond . He was compelled to go into
chancery for reliefupon the ground that his bond was given without consideration, or
that the considerationhad failed,or that the obligeehad practicedfraud as to the consid-
eration. But ,by statute(act of 1801, M. and B. , 331 ; General Stututes, ch. 22, 3), the
considerationof any writing, with or without seal, may be impeached at law.

whose chairman,withinfivedays,shalllaythe same beforetheSenate.
