Minutes of Examining Court.
FRANKLIN COUNTY ,August 10, 1877.
The defendant,John Smith,havingbeen broughtbeforeme , thejudge of the Franklin
countycourt,chargedwiththeoffenseof larceny,and beinginformedof the natureofthe
chargeagainsthim,moved an adjournmentofexaminationuntilthe 12thday of thismonth,
toprocure the aid of counseland the attendanceof witnesses, which motion was sustained;
and,inthemeantime,he iscommittedtothejailof Franklincounty[or,to thecustodyof
the sheriffof Franklincounty].
(Iftheoffensebe a misdemeanor,and thedefendantdesirestogivebail,ordepositmoney
inlieuof bail,an entryof thefactshouldbe made on theminutes.)
AUGUST 12, 1877.
The courtmet pursuanttoadjournment,and thedefendantwas broughtfromjail. De-
fendant filedhisaffidavit,statingthat he did not believehe could securea fairtrialbefore
J. C. Herndon ,judge of the Franklin county court, the presidingmagistratein this case,
and thereupon the saidJ. C. Herndon retiredfrom the case, and John Parker and A. B.
Williams,justicesof the peace for Franklincounty, having been duly notified, appeared for
the purpose of presidingin the case.
The partiesbeing ready,the defendant moved thatthe witnessesbe examined separately:
whereupon ,allthe witnesses, exceptthe one under examination, were removed out of hear-
ingof the witnessunder examination.
John Jones was appointed by me to act as clerk in writingdown the testimonyof the
witnesses .
Thomas Cox being sworn , and examined on behalf of the Commonwealth , testified:
[Here should followthe testimonyof the witnesses.]
The courthaving heard the evidenceand the arguments of counsel, and being satisfied
that there are not sufficientgrounds to believethat the defendant is guiltyof the offense
charged, or any otheroffense, itisorderedthat he be discharged. Or ,
The court having heard the evidence and the arguments of counsel, and being satisfied
thattherearesufficientgroundsto believethedefendantisguiltyof theoffensecharged, it
isorderedthathe be held fortrialin the Franklincircuitcourt,and allowed to give bail,in
the sum of dollars; and not givingbail,he is committedto thejailof Franklin
county .
The witnesseson behalf of the Commonwealth ,A. B. and C. D. , and the witnesseson
behalf of the defendant,E. F. and G. H. , entered into recognizancesfor theirappearance
intheFranklincircuitcourt. The warrantof arrest(thebailbond) and recognizancesof
thewitnessesaccompany theseminutes. JOHN C. HERNDON ,J. F. C. С.
(Ifa magistratebe objectedtoand retirefrom the case,themagistratetakinghisplace
should signthe minutes.)

NOTES TO CRIMINAL CODE .
I to 3 . REPEAL OF FORMER LAWS .
a. Itwas held,under the same provisionsof theCode of 1854, thatthe provision
givingtheCommonwealth therightof peremptorychallenge,applied,aswellto
prosecutionscommenced before, as to those commenced after, that Code went
intoeffect; and thatthis,affectingproceedingsonly,was notan expostfactolaw.
Watson vs. Commonwealth , 16 B. M. , 15.
b. The Code repealsallformerlawsastopenaland criminalproceedingsand, since
itsadoption, no prosecutorisnecessaryto an indictment. Commonwealth vs.Pat-
terson, 2 Met ., 374 .
89 , 11.
Itwas held,however,thattheCode of 1854did not authorizean appealfrom a
judgment rendered in a criminal case before it took effect. Commonwealth vs.
Craig, 15 B. М. ,534 .
HOW FINE RECOVERABLE .
4. Fines are recoverableby indictment or penal action; and the rightof the Com-
monwealth so to recovera finefor usurpationof office, isnot affectedby the pro-
visionsof 483 of the CivilCode . Commonwealth vs.Adams , 3 Met .,6 .
A proceeding by warrant to recover a finein a justice'scourt must conform to the
provisionsof Title 16, CivilCode . Wilson vs. Commonwealth , 7 Bush , 536 .
§ 13, 14.
46 .
49 .
JURISDICTION OF COURTS .
a. Where the indictment charged the maintenance of a nuisance upon a public
highway ,for such a length of time as to aggregate more than ten dollarsfine,
the circuitcourthas jurisdiction. Commonwealth vs. Mills, 6 Bush , 296.
b . The jurisdictionof the circuitcourt of Henderson county, under this section,
was heldnot tobe affectedby a subsequentstatutegivingexclusivejurisdiction
to the Henderson citycourt in allcasesof misdemeanor arisingwithin said city.
This provisionof the Code will not be deemed repealed by a speciallocallaw
unlesssuch an intentionon the partof the Legislatureismanifest. Common-
wealthus.Pointer, 5 Bush , 301.
ARREST WITHOUT WARRANT .
a .A magistratehas no authoritytotakea bail-bond fora prisonerbroughtbefore
him forexaminationof a chargeof felonycommittedinanothercounty; nor has
he any rightto take temporary bailfor the appearance of the accused beforehim .
Commonwealth us. Salyar,8 Bush ,461 .
COMMITTING MAGISTRATES .
a. Judges of thecountycourts,judgesof cityorpolicecourts,mayors,chairmenof
the trusteesof towns,and justicesof thepeace are magistrates(§ 26); and any
one of them may commit or hold tobail,exceptthat,in casesof felony,ifthe
committingmagistratebe a justiceof the peace he must associatewith him

anotherjustice(271). Commonwealthvs.Cummins, 18 B. M. ,26; Ricevs.Com
monwealth, 3 Bush , 14.
b. One justiceof the peace cannot act as an examining court, and commit to jailor
hold to bail, a person charged with felony; another justicemust be associated
with him . Revillvs. Pettit,3 Met .,314.
But a singlejusticemay commit or hold to bail in misdemeanor cases, Tharp us .
Commonwealth , Id ., 411 .
DISPOSITION OF ACCUSED DURING ADJOURNMENT .
a. An examining court has no right, during the progress of the examination, to
admit to bailor releasefrom custody, a party charged with felony; and a bail-
bond taken forhis appearance during the examination is unauthorized and void.
Commonwealth us.Moore , 3 Met .,477.
d . Writs or bail-bonds issued or taken on Sunday are valid . Rice vs. Commonwealth ,
3 Bush , 16 ; Watts vs.Commonwealth , 5 Bush , 309 .
267 ,68 .
271 .
TAKING BAIL AFTER COMMITMENT .
a. A statuteproviding that the citycourt of Lexington " as to committing criminal
offenders, and sending them on for trialshallhave the powers of two justicesof
the peace of Fayette county," does not authorizethe clerk of that court to take
a bail-bond . Dugan vs. Commonwealth ,6 Bush , 305 .
b. The courthaving at the firstterm afterthe commitment of the accused fixedhis
bail, a bail-bond taken by the county judge afterthe adjournment of the court
was held to be void. It should have been taken by the clerk. Branham vs.
Commonwealth , 2 Bush , 3.
But the court having adjourned without fixingthe amount of bail, itwas held that
two justices, the prisonerhaving been brought before them on habeascorpus, had
power to fixthe amount of bail and take bond (see& 423). Creekmorevs. Com-
monwealth, 5 Bush , 312.
c. But the clerk cannot take bail unless the court has previouslymade an order
allowingbail,and fixingthe amount . Wellenwebervs.Commonwealth, 3 Bush , 69 .
HOW JUSTICE TO PROCEED IN FELONY CASES .
a . See notes to ? 49 .
b. The term " larceny" does not under the Code necessarilyimport " felony" (25 ,
6, 7), and where it appeared that a bail bond was taken by a singlejustice, it
willbe presumedthatthecasewas one of pettylarceny,and thatthebond was
valid. Tharp vs.Commonwealth , 3 Met .,411 .
c. A singlejusticehas no power to requireor receivebail from one brought before
him charged with a felony, and such a bond isnot good for any purpose. Com-
monwealth vs. Fisher, 2 Duv ., 376 ; Murphy vs. Commonwealth , ıı Bush , 219.

b. Sheriffs. 28,83 and notes, 143, 144-

a. Although the statuterequiresthe suretyin a bail-bond to be a residentof the

Ramsey , 2 Duv ., 385.

UNDERTAKING OF BAIL .
e. If itappearthatthedefendantwas legallyin custody,thathe was dischargedby
the giving of the bond , and it can be ascertainedfrom the bond , that the bail
undertookthatthe defendantshouldappearbeforea courtfortrial,suchbond

But a bond which does not state the charge which the defendant is required to

b. An undertakingthatthe defendant"willrenderhimselfobedientto theorders
and processof thiscourt," isequivalenttoan undertaking,thathe shall" atall

e. An indictmentagainstA having been abatedby the Commonwealth'sAttorney
under the mistakenimpressionthatthe defendantwas dead and having been

ment terminated the prosecutionand the bond was void. Henry vs. Common-
wealth , 4 Bush ,429 .
f . Where bail-bond was conditioned for the appearance of the defendant at the
ensuing May term and no court was held in May : held, that the suretiesin the
bond were bound for the appearance of theirprincipalat the next ensuing term
of the court. Commonwealth vs.Branch , I Bush , 59.
g . For other irregularitiessee ? 85 and notes.
WHEN SHERIFF MAY TAKE BAIL .
The sheriffarrestinga person by authorityof hisbailisnot authorized to take new
bail; he can only take bail when he has made the arrestunder a warrant, or
other process,in which itshallappear thatthe person isto be admitted to bailin
a specifiedsum . Schneidervs.Commonwealth, 3 Met .,409 .
IRREGULARITIES AS TO BAIL -BONDS .
a. A joint recognizancefor the defendant's appearance to answer to an indictment
for burglary, and an indictment for grand larceny, both charges relating to
the same actsof the defendant, was held to be valid.
As in such case, there could be but one conviction and one punishment , the two
indictments are, in effect, no more than two counts in the same indictment.
Baird & Price vs. Commonwealth , 2 Duv ., 78 .
b. A defendant who voluntarilyappears in court on the returnof an indictment, is
in legal custody of the court, and a recognizance entered into by him and his
sureties, whilst in such custody, is valid. Baird & Pricevs. Commonwealth , 2
Duv ., 78 .
c. Where a bail-bond bore thisindorsement : " M. W. King signed thisbond in my
presence,on thedate above; itwas leftat the groceryof Mr. J. R. Covington

monwealth, 3 Bush ,478.
d. Where a blank sheetof paperwas signedand leftwith the committingmagis
trate, with authorityto him to fillit up as a bail-bond , and itwas afterwards
filledup,not by him, but by the countyattorney,itwas held tobe void. Com.
monwealth us.Ball, 6 Bush , 292.
. A bond taken by an unauthorizedofficerisvoid under the statuteand at the com-

477 ; Same vs.Roberts, 1 Duv ., 199 ; Same vs.Fisher, 2 Id., 376 .
And a bond taken by officersauthorized to take bail-bonds , but under circum-
stanceswhich did not authorizethe taking of a bail-bond , was held to be void;
as,a bond takenby two justicesduringadjournmentof examination. Common-
wealthus.Moore , 3 Met .,477 .
f. The factthatan indictmentwas defectiveand liabletobe quashed,doesnotren-
der a recognizancevoid. Commonwealth vs.Skeggs, 3 Bush , 19.

f. It isnot a sufficientdefenseby bail thathis principalisunder arrestin another
State . Withrow vs. Commonwealth , I Bush , 18 .
g . The Code does not authorize the defendant in an indictment to enter into a
bail-bond or recognizance; and such a bond or recognizancecannot be enforced
againsthim by the statutoryremedies. Commonwealth vs.Radford, 2 Duv ., 9.
h. An answer by bailin the Bourbon circuitcourt that the principal" was necessa-
rilyprevented from appearing in saidcourt on said [appearance] day because he
was then on said day in the custody and control of the law of this State, and
legallyconfinedby legalprocessinthe jailof Carrollcounty, Kentucky,upon a
charge of felony, where he yet is," was held to be insufficient. Kirby vs. Com .
monwealth, I Bush , 113 ; but see Withrow vs. Commonwealth , Id., 18.
3. DISCHARGE .
a. A suretyina bail-bond cannotbe made responsibleforthefailureofhisprincipal
toappearwhen suchappearancewas preventedby thearrestof theprincipalby
a United Statesprovost marshal. Commonwealth us. Webster, I Bush , 616.
b. Suretiesin a bail-bond are dischargedby the re-arrestof the accused and hiscom-
mitment tojailfrom which he made hisescapeduringthependencyof a second
examiningtrialforthesame offense. Medlinus. Commonwealth, Bush,605.

154 CRIMINAL CODE - NOTES TO SECS. 93 TO 98 .
c. A releaseof the principalupon a writ of habeascorpusby a judge having jurisdic-
tion, though improper, dischargesthe bail. Commonwealthvs.Bronson, 14 B.
M. , 361 .
d. Where therecordshowsthatthedefendanttoan indictmentforfelony" appeared
in discharge of a recognizance" and was put upon trial, during the progressof
which he escaped; itwas held thathis bailwas discharged. Askins vs.Common-
wealth, I Duv ., 275 ; overrulingCommonwealth vs.Coleman, 2 Met ., 382.
Aliter as to misdemeanor cases. I Duv ., 275 , supra .
But itwas held, in a bastardy case, that where the defendant appeared in court
" to answer the charge" but was not surrendered, nor taken into custody,his
bailwas not discharged. Wintersollvs.Commonwealth, I Duv ., 177.
e. Pending a proceedingon a forfeitedrecognizance,by summons in the circuit
court, on motion of the defendant,who was suretyin the recognizance,the court
quashedthe indictment,and forthwithrenderedjudgment forexecutionforthe
amount of the adjudged forfeiture. By quashing the indictment, the pending
prosecution was closed; but the recognizance did not thereby become functus
officio, and the judgment of forfeiturewas not thereby stifled. The quashal of
the indictment did not bar a continuance or renewed prosecutionfor the same
offense; nor did itdeprive the suretyin the bail-bond of his rightto surrender
or arrestthe accused, and put him in the custody of the court,subjectto trial
under such prosecution, and thus bring himself within the 98th sectionof the
Criminal Code . Littlevs.Commonwealth , 3 Bush , 22.
f . The accused being under hond in the sum of $ 500 forhis appearance,and having
failedto appear, a bench warrant issuedwith an indorsement allowingbailin the
sum of $ 250 : held, that thesefactsdid not releasethe suretiesin the bail-bond .
Commonwealth vs.Branch , 1 Bush , 59.
g . For dischargeof bailby failureto indict, see & 117 and notes.
4. REMISSION .
a . 98 of the Criminal Code merely confers a judicialdiscretionand power upon
the court to render judgment in an action on a bail-bond forsuch part of the
penalty of the bond as may be just and proper, afterhearing proof of defend-
ant's factsin defense- not a power to remit a fineor forfeiturein the sense in
which those terms are used inthe Constitution-- and isthereforeno infringement
of the rightsof the executive department, and is not unconstitutional. This
discretionistobe exercisedjudiciallyupon considerationof thefactsreliedupon
in the defense, and is not an arbitraryand uncontrollablediscretion. Common-
wealth us. Thornton, I Met., 380 ; Commonwealth vs. Coleman, & c., 2 Met ., 384 ;
Same vs.Rowland , 4 Met ., 225.
b. Under 298 of the CriminalCode thecourthas power to remitthepenaltyof a
bail-bond,althougha judgment had been renderedupon the bond,which was
setasideon the same day ,and the remissionthen made . Commonwealth vs.Run-
nion & Hay , 3 Met ., 2.
c. Afterjudgment isenteredagainsthailthecourthasno power toremitthefor-
feiture. Commonwealth vs.Radford, 2 Duv ., 9.
d . The fact that the defendant had either been surrendered or arrested must be
allegedand shown in the defense before the court can remit. Commonwealth vs.
Rowland , 4 Met ., 225 .
e. The discretionof the inferiorcourt in remittingthe penalty of a bail-bond will
not be controlledby the Court of Appeals unless flagrantlyabused. Common-
wealthus. Davidson, I Bush , 133 : Same vs. Coleman, 2.Met ., 382 ; Same vs.Rouw-
land,4 Met ., 225.

f . The Governor may remit the whole forfeiture, including the per centage of the
attorneyforthe Commonwealth, at any time beforea validjudgment against
the bail. Commonwealth vs.Ramsay , 2 Duv ., 385 ; Same vs.Spraggins, 18 B. M. ,
512 .
g . That the Governor remits, not the forfeiture, but the judgment itselfexcept as
to " fees and costs," furnished no ground for settingaside the judgment so in
part remitted. Brown vs. Commonwealth, 4 Met ., 221.
FORMATION OF GRAND JURY .
a . The summoning of bystanders to serve as grand jurors, when properly ordered,
isa duty in the performance of which the accused in a criminalcase isentitled
to the services of the sheriff, or coroner where there is no sheriff. If summoned
by one speciallyappointed by the court, the indictment may be quashed . It is
a substantialerror. Commonwealth us. Graddy, 4 Met ., 223.
b. Section 193 of the Criminal Code ,which authorizesthe court, forsufficientcause,
to designatesome other officeror person to summons jurors, relatesonly to petit
jurors. Id.
c. Where the grand jury iscomposed ofjurorsselectedby commissioners, as directed
by the Revised Statutes, the court say they inclineto the opinion that the sum-
moning of them by a person other than the sheriffor coroner, would not be " a
substantial error ." Id .
d . An order of the circuitcourt showing that the grand jury was empanneled , the
Court of Appeals will presume that it was regularlyempanneled , though the
order did not show that the court appointed a foreman , or that the jurorswere
sworn . Commonwealth us. Pullan, 3 Bush ,47 .
e. That a civilofficer, to-wit, a trusteeof the public schools, was a member of the
grand jury isno ground for settingasidean indictment. Commonwealthus.
Pritchett, 11 Bush , 277.
112 , 113 .
117.
PROCEEDINGS TO ВЕ КЕРТT SECRET .
The testimony of a grand juror is not admissibleto explain or impeach theirfind.
ing. Commonwealth vs.Skeggs,3 Bush , 19.
DISCHARGE OF DEFENDANT IF NO INDICTMENT FOUND .
a. The Code of 1854, 116, provided that if the grand jury failedto indictat the
term next afterthe submission of the charge to them , the defendant should be
discharged, " unlessfor causeshown " the court should otherwise direct. It was
held under thissectionthat the cause and directionmust appear upon the record
or bail would be exonerated. Rion vs. Commonwealth, I Duv ., 235 ; Common-
wealthus. Roberts, 4 Met ., 219 ; but aliterunder thisCode (§ 117, 115) as to the
necessityforshowing in the order the cause of the actionof the court.
b. Where the grand jury failsto indict, bail is exonerated though no order be
entered discharging the bond . Commonwealth vs. Roberts, 4 Met ., 219 ; Common-
wealthus. Blincoe, 3 Bush , 12 ; Bryant vs. Commonwealth , 3 Bush , 9.
122 to 137 . FORM AND REQUISITES OF INDICTMENTS .
I. Form of indictments. Page 156.
2. Name of defendant. Page 156.
3. Joinder of offenses. Page 156.
4. Certaintyin indictments. Page 156.
5. Averments under statutescontainingprovisosand exceptions. Page 158.
6. Unnecessaryallegationsin indictments. Page 158.

156 CRIMINAL CODE - NOTES TO SECS . 122 ΤΟ 137-
7. Averments in indictmentsfor obtainingmoney ,& c., underfalsepretenses. Page 158.
8. Avermentsinindictmentsunderstatutesdescribingoffenses. Page 159.
9. Averments in indictmentsgenerally. Page 161.
1. FORM OF INDICTMENTS .
a . Though an indictment be founded on a statuteitisnot necessary,sincethe adop-
tionof the Code of Practice, that it should conclude, " againstthe form of the
statute." Under theCode an indictmentissufficientifitshows intelligiblythe
offenseintended to be charged and is not deemed insufficientby reason of any
defect which does not tend to the prejudice of the substantialrights of the
defendant on the merits. Commonwealth vs.Kennedy , 15 В. М. , 531.
b . It isnot necessaryto the validityof an indictment that it should show upon its
facethat ithad been presented on oathby the grand jury,and , in casesof felony,
that the word "feloniously" should be used in charging the intent with which
the offensewas committed . Jane (a slave) vs. Commonwealth, 3 Met ., 18.
c. The clerk'sindorsement, describing the character of the indictment, forms no
part of the indictment. Commonwealth us. English,6 Bush , 431 .
2. NAME OF DEFENDANT .
a . The omission to set out the first or Christian name of the defendant in the indict-
ment willnot vitiateit. Commonwealth vs.Kelcher, 3 Met .,484.
3. JOINDER OF OFFENSES .
a.An indictmentformurder containedthreeparagraphs. The firstchargedthat
the murder was committed by fivedefendants jointly; the second, that H com-
mitted the murder , and that the other defendants " did, with malice afore-
thought, counsel, advise, and persuade the said H to kill and murder " the
deceased; the third, that T committed the murder , and that the other defend-
ants in like manner counseled and advised the commission of the act. Held,
that there is no misjoinderof offenses; that but one offenseis set forth in the
indictment, though charged to have been committed in differentmodes . That ,
although the particularagency of each defendant is unnecessarilystated, itdoes
not vitiatethe indictment. Thompson vs. Commonwealth, I Met ., 13.
b. Itwas held thatan indictment, charging" thatthe defendantunlawfullyand
without feloniousintentdestroyed, injured, and carriedaway timber from four
treeson the premisesof John W. Wash ,which saidpropertybelonged to the
saidWash , and was taken without his consent," was sufficient; that the indict-
ment chargedbut one offense,thatof unlawfullytakingand carryingaway the
timber of four treesbelonging toWash ; the charge of carryingaway necessarily
involving the taking of the timber; the words " destroyed" and " injured," as
used in the indictment, if not appropriatelydescriptiveof that offense, being
too generaland indefiniteto constitutewith sufficientcertaintyeitherof the
otheroffensesintendedto be embracedby ?7,art.25,chap.28,of the Revised
Statutes . Commonwealth vs. Powell , 8 Bush , 6 .
c. See 168 as to misjoinder.
4. CERTAINTY IN INDICTMENTS .
a. An indictment, found in May , 1865, averred the commission of an offensein
July, 1865,but alsoaverredthattheoffensewas committedbeforethefindingof
the indictment : Held sufficient. Commonwealth vs.Jones, 1 Bush , 34.
b. An indictmentforburglaryissufficientindescribingthehouse enteredas that
of A B. It isnot necessaryto statethe streetand number . Olivevs. Common-
wealth, 5 Bush ,377-
c. A warrant under an ordinanceprohibitingthe saleof liquoron Sunday, is
sufficient, without averring the quantitysold, on the place where itwas drunk .
Megowan vs. Commonwealth, 2 Met .,3 .

CRIMINAL CODE - NOTES TO SECS . 122 ΤΟ 137 . 157
d . In an indictment for murder the person murdered was described as " Barbara
Kriel, the wife of the defendant," and upon the trialitappeared that her name
was Margaret Kriel. Held to be an errorof descriptionwhich did not vitiate
the indictment. Krielvs. Commonwealth, 5 Bush , 375.
e. An indictment must set forth the offensewith such certaintyas to apprise the
defendant of the nature of the accusation upon which he is to be tried, and to
constitutea bar to any subsequent proceeding for the same offense.
An indictment which charges that the defendant sufferedcertainnamed persons
" to play in a house or on premises in the county aforesaid, then in the occupa-
tion and under the control of the said P , a game of cards, at which game of
cards,played as aforesaid,money or propertywas won and lost," was held insuffi-
cient upon demurrer because in the alternative. Commonwealth use Perrigo, 3
Met ., 5 .
But an indictment charging that the defendant carried concealed deadly weap-
ons, to-wit : a bowie -knife, and alsoa dirk or dagger, is sufficient, and it isnot
necessary, in order to conviction, to prove that he carriedboth a bowie -knife
and a dirk or dagger. Commonwealth vs.Howard , 3 Met .,407 .
f. An indictmentcharging that the defendant " did unlawfullysellwhiskyand brandy,
and otherspirituousliquors, & c.," lacks the ordinary certaintyrequired by the
Criminal Code, to give the defendant noticeof the particularcharge constituting
his offense. Commonwealth us. White, 18 В. М. , 492 .
g . An indictment charging, in substance, that the defendant did " peddle and sell"
in the county of M. , buggies, pleasure carriages, & c., not having a licenseto
peddle the same , and said articlesnot being the product or manufacture of the
State of Kentucky , is insufficientfor uncertainty. Commonwealth vs.Dudley, 3
Met ., 222 .
h .An indictmentchargingthatdefendant" did feloniouslytenderin payment to
Mrs. Margaret Boyle an alteredbank billof the Dayton Bank " isbad for uncer-
tainty. The billand the alterationshould have been describedwith such cer-
taintyas to enable the defendant to plead the indictment and judgment in bar
of a subsequent prosecution. Mount vs. Commonwealth, I Duv .,90 .
i. An indictment charging that " the said William White heretofore,to-wit : on the
day of April, 1871, in the Commonwealth and county aforesaid, did unlaw-
fully,willfully,maliciously, feloniously,and of hismaliceaforethoughtkilland
murder hiswife,one Nancy White,againstthe peace and dignityof theCom-
monwealth of Kentucky ," was held to be insufficient, because itwholly failedto
statethe particularfactswhich were necessaryto show the manner in which the
crime may have been committed , or the means of itsperpetration. White vs.
Commonwealth , 9 Bush , 178 .
j. 131 of the Code of 1854,which was omitted in thisCode ,provided that,if the
indictment contained no statement of the place in which the offensewas com-
mitted, itshould be consideredas charged in the indictment that itwas commit-
ted within the localjurisdictionof the court. Under that sectionit was held
that an indictment charging the renting of a house to be kept as a bawdy -house
was sufficientwithout describingitslocality. Harlow vs.Commonwealth, 11 Bush ,
610 .
k . It was held, under the Code of 1854, that an indictment, charging the defendant
with having stolenbank notes, was insufficient, because itdid not describetheir
denomination , number , or value. Rhodus vs. Commonwealth, 2 Duv ., 159. But
see ? 135 of thisCode .
1.It was held, under the Code of 1854, that an indictment for larcenymust aver
the ownership of the stolenproperty, or an excuse forthe omission, as that the
owner isto the grand jurors unknown . Reed vs. Commonwealth , 7 Bush , 642.

158 CRIMINAL CODE - NOTES TO SECS . 122 ΤΟ 137 .
Also that an averment that property stolenwas " the property of the Tennessee
River Packet Company , D. W. Swan , LittleBrothers, and others," was suffi-
cientlycertain. Reed vs. Commonwealth , 7 Bush , 643.
Also that a conviction on an indictment for stealingStephenDaniel'shog , upon
proof that the defendant stolePhilipDaniel's hog , was erroneous. Hensley vs.
Commonwealth , I Bush , 11 .
Also thata mistake in spelling, in the indictment, the name of the person robbed,
was immaterial. Olivevs. Commonwealth , 5 Bush , 379 .
128 of thisCode , extending the provisionsof 127 of that Code , declaresthat
an erroneous allegationas to the ownership of the property taken shall not be
material,provided the offensebe otherwisedescribedwith sufficientcertainty.
5. AVERMENTS UNDER STATUTES CONTAINING PROVISOS AND EXCEPTIONS .
a. It iswell settledthat where provisos and exceptions are contained in distinct
clausesof a statutedefining an offense, itis not necessaryto aver in the indict-
ment that the defendant does not come within the exceptions, or to negativethe
provisos it contains. Nor is it necessaryto allegethat he is not within such
provisos, even though the purview should expresslynotice them ; as by saying
thatnone shalldo the act prohibited, except in the cases thereinafterexcepted.
These are properly matters of defense. Commonwealth vs. McClanahan , 2 Met ., 8 .
b. An indictment charging that thedefendant " having a wife then livingdid unlaw-
fullymarry Laurena Farley" is sufficient. It is not necessary to aver that he
was not within any of the saving clauses of the statute. Commonwealth us.
Whaley , 6 Bush , 266.
6. UNNECESSARY ALLEGATIONS IN INDICTMENTS .
a. In a warrant,forsaleof liquoron Sunday ,itwas averredthat the defendant sold
liquor" on Sunday,the 10thday of February, 1859." Held,thatthe averment
of the day of the month was surplusage; and that proof of sale, on any Sunday
previous to the issuingof the warrant, was sufficientto authorize conviction.
Megowan vs. Commonwealth ,2 Met ., 3 .
b. Where a personor thingnecessarytobe mentionedinan indictment,isdescribed
with circumstancesof greater particularitythan is requisite, yet those circum-
stancesmust be proved,otherwiseitwould not appear that the person or thing is
the same described in the indictment. Clarkvs. Commonwealth , 16 В. М. , 213 ;
Commonwealth vs.Magowan , I Met ., 368.
But the decisions in the cases of Clark vs. Commonwealth and Commonwealth vs.
Magowan , and the authoritiesreliedon in support of them , go no fartherthan
to establishthe doctrinethat " no allegation, whether necessaryor unnecessary,
which isdescriptiveof the identityof thatwhich islegallyessentialto the charge
in the indictment, can ever be rejectedas surplusage;" and allegationsof value
in an indictment are in no proper sense descriptiveof identity, and need not be
proved as laid. They serve, in general, only to distinguishbetween different
degrees of the offenseof larceny. Commonwealth vs.Garland, 3 Met ., 478 .
7. AVERMENTS IN INDICTMENTS FOR OBTAINING MONEY , &C ., UNDER FALSE PRE-
TENSES .
a. In prosecutionsfor obtainingmoney or goods under falsepretenses, the indict-
ment must contain allthe materialfactsand circumstancesnecessaryto be proved
upon the trialin order to convict. It will not do to charge that the defendant
obtained the goods or money by falsepretenses, without statingwhat the false
pretenseswere . And some care must be observed in settingforththe pretenses,
becausetheymust be provedaslaid. Glacken,etc.,vs.Commonwealth,3 Met.,232.
b. It isessentialto a convictionforobtainingpropertyor money underfalsepre-
tensesto allegeand prove that the pretense, whereby the money or propertywas

CRIMINAL CODE - NOTES TO SECS . 122 ΤΟ 137 . 159
obtained,was a statement of some pretended past occurrence or existingfact,
made forthe purposeof inducingthe partyinjuredto partwith hisproperty.
No statement or representationof anything to take place in futureisa pretense
within the meaning of the statute,whether itbe in the form of a promise or not.
Ib ., 232 .
c. Where the falsepretenses charged were, that, if the owner would permit the
defendants to take her cow and calf to market and sellthe same , they would
account for and pay over to the owner , or her daughter for her, the money
received therefor; and that they, the defendants, would at their own costs and
expense remove the owner from the house in which she then resided, and pro-
vide her with another house freeof rent; the indictment was held insufficient,
the promises being to do futureacts. Ib., 232.
d. The offenseof " feloniouslyobtainingmoney by falsepretenses" consistsin
obtainingthe money , with a fraudulent intent; the falsepretense employed is
only the means by which the offenseisperpetrated. Therefore,where V sold
and delivered to Ja negro man , in theStateof Ohio, falselyrepresentinghim to
be a slave, and receivedthe purchase and executed the billof salein Kentucky,
and the negro turned out to be a freeman , the court in Kentucky had jurisdic-
tion to try V for the offenseof feloniouslyobtainingmoney by falsepretenses, as
the offenseof so obtainingthemoney was committed in Kentucky . Commonwealth
vs. Van Tuyl , I Met ., I.
e. An indictment for obtaininga note by falsepretenses, charged,in substance, that
thedefendantfalselyrepresentedtohiscreditor,thathe had a specifiedquantity
of tobacco,worth 14 centsper pound ; that itwas of a particulardescriptionand
quality, according to a sample which he exhibited; and that, by these falsepre-
tenses and representations, with intent to cheat and defraud the creditor, the
defendant obtained, and the creditorwas induced to deliverhim , a note which
he held upon the defendant,upon which therewas a balancedue of $ 122 64,
and to credit said note for said sum ; when in truth said lot of tobacco was not a
good articleof tobacco,and not of the qualityand descriptionof the sample,
etc., and was of no value, which the defendant knew . Held, that the facts
charged did not amount to a falsepretense within the meaning of the statute.
Commonwealth vs. Haughey , 3 Met ., 223 .
8. AVERMENTS IN INDICTMENTS UNDER STATUTES DESCRIBING OFFENSES .
a. An indictment for robbery, which followed the language of the statute(? 2, art.
5, chap . 28, R. S.) was held to be sufficient, although it did not describe the
property allegedto have been taken, nor aver that the assaultwas made against
the willof the person robbed . Commonwealth vs. Tanner, 5 Bush , 316.
b. An indictment charging that the defendant " did, with force and arms , feloni-
ouslyassault, beat, shake, and hold, and restrainSusan L. Rogers, with his
hands and arms; and, with a largeknifeand weapon, puttingher, the said
Susan, in greatfearof some seriousand immediateinjurytoher lifeand limb,
with thefeloniousintenttorob her, thesaidRogers,contrary," & c.,was heldto
be sufficient; though the offensewas not charged in the precisewords of the
statute. Taylorvs.Commonwealth , 3 Bush , 508.
c. Under the statuteprovidingthat" ifany personshallmaliciouslyshootat and
wound another, with an intentionto killhim , so that he does not die thereby,
with a gun or other instrument, loaded with a leaden bullet, or other hard sub-
stance," it was held, that an indictment which charged that the defendant,
" with a certainpistol,which he then and therehad and held,feloniouslyand
maliciouslyshotand wounded one John Bailey,thenand therebeing,with the
intentthen and there to killand murder the said John Bailey;" but failedto

160 CRIMINAL CODE - NOTES TO SECS . 122 ΤΟ 137 .
allege that the person wounded did not die thereby, and that the pistolwas
loadedwith a leadenbulletor otherhard substance,was sufficient,eitherupon
demurrer or upon motion to arrestthe judgment . Burns vs. Commonwealth, 3
Met ., 13 .
d. An indictment charging defendant with permitting " a game of chance called
pigeon-hole" to be played for " greenbacks " was held to be sufficienton demur-
rer,the demurrer admitting itto be a game of chance. Commonwealth vs.Bran-
ham , 3 Bush , I.
e. An indictment charging “ malicious mischief, in the shooting and killingby
appellee of the sheep of Allen Collins, willfullyand unlawfully, without the
owner's consent," was held to be sufficientunder 27 , art. 25, ch. 28, R. S.,
which prohibited the " unlawful" injury of personal property. Commonwealth
vs.Smith,6 Bush , 263.
f . An indictmentchargingthe defendantwithbeing theowner of a ten-pin alley,
at which games were played, and money bet, won and lost, and that he and
othersplayed at such games , was held to be sufficientwithout an averment that
defendant knew of such betting. Commonwealth vs. Fraize, 5 Bush , 325.
g . An act of 1864 (Myers' Sup ., 247) declared that if any person, being the owner
of a billiard-table, " where money or any thing of value is bet," shallsuffera
minor , without the permission of his parent, & c., to play a game thereon, he
shallbe fined,& c. In indictment under thisstatutecharging the defendant with
permitting a minor to play on defendant's billiard-table, " kept for hire and
profit," was held to be sufficient; the court saying that a reversalwas sought
" only because the indictment does not explicitlycharge that 'money was bet or
lost' on the game played by the minor ." Green vs. Commonwealth , 5 Bush , 328.
Quere: An avermentof bettingby the minor was clearlyunnecessary; but it
seems equallyclear that the indictment was defectivein not averring that the
tablewas one " where money or any thing of value isbet."
h. Under ?19, ch.42,R. S.,which made ita penaloffensetokeep any billiard-table;
and an amendment of 1858, which permitted private tables" not kept or used
forhire or profit;" itwas held that an indictment, for keeping a billiard-table,
must aver thatitwas kept forhire or profit. Holt vs. Commonwealth , 2 Bush , 33.
And see Rittevs. Commonwealth , 18 B. М. , 35.
i. When the words of the statute are descriptiveof the offense,the indictment
should follow the language of the statute: thus, a statutehaving made it an
offenseto killany horse " willfully," an indictment,charging the defendant with
having killeda horse " unlawfully," was held to be defective. Commonwealth vs.
Turner , 8 Bush , 2 .
j . An indictment, charging a saleof liquor to a white minor " without the written
consent and requestof the father," & c., isdefectiveunder the statuteforbidding
such sale" without the written consent or request," & c. Commonwealth vs.Hod-
craft, 6 Bush , 91 .
k. A statutehaving imposed a penalty for sellingliquorto a white person under
twenty-one years of age,an indictment,charging the defendant with having sold
liquor to a person under twenty-one years of age, was , on demurrer held to be
defective,because itdid not statethat the said person was white. Commonwealth
vs. Ewing , 7 Bush , 105.
1. Under the statuteprohibitingthe counterfeitingof the notes " of any bank or
company incorporatedby law inany partof theUnitedStates," itwas heldthat
an indictment, charging the counterfeitingof notes of " the Merchants ' Bank of
Massachusetts," was defective, because it did not aver that the bank was incor-
porated. Kennedy vs. Commonwealth , 2 Met ., 36.

CRIMINAL CODE - NOTES TO SECS . 122 ΤΟ 137 . 161
m . An indictmentchargingthe keepingof a " keno-table, ** itbeing a con-
trivanceforbetting,at which and whereby money and propertywas bet,won
and lost," was held insufficentunder chap . 16, § 6 , R. S., because the statutedid
not denouncekeno-tablesby name ,and theindictmentfailedtochargethatthey
were contrivancesordinarilyused for purposes of gaming . Commonwealth us.
Monarch ,6 Bush , 268. Acc ., Rittevs.Commonwealth , 18 B. M. , 35 .
But, an indictmentchargingthe keeping of a " faro-bank," was held to be suffi-
cient,withoutan averment thatitwas ordinarilyusedforpurposesof gaming;
becausefaro-banks were expresslydenounced by the statute. Commonwealthus.
Monarch ,6 Bush ,301.
n . Under ? 1,art.17,chap.29,of theGeneralStatutes,providingthatany person
who should, in sudden heat and passion, " cut, thrust, or stabany other person
with a knife,dirk, sword , or other deadly weapon ," itwas held that an indict-
ment charging that the defendant did " strikeand wound George Gregory with a
pairof blacksmith'stongs,which were then and therea deadlyweapon," was
defective. Commonwealth us.Hawkins , 11 Bush ,603 .
Under thestatutemaking itpenalto " maliciouslyshootat andwound another," it
was held thatan indictment,chargingthatthe defendant" willfullyand with
maliceaforethoughtshotatAsa Oliver,with intenttokillhim, and,missinghis
aim, wounded W. T. Buntin," was insufficient. Commonwealth vs.Morgan , II
Bush ,601.
An allegationthatthedefendantaided anothertoescape" beyondthecustodyof
the jailerof Scott county " is not equivalent to an allegationthat defendant
aided him to escape from said custody ; and is insufficientunder 5, art. 16,
chap.29,GeneralStatutes. Tullyvs.Commonwealth, 11 Bush, 161.
9. AVERMENTS IN INDICTMENTS GENERALLY .
a . Murder . An indictment, found by a grand jury charged in substance, that the
saidJane, the slaveof Ed .Smith,in the countyaforesaid, on the 24th day of
December , 1857, did willfullyand maliciously, and with maliceaforethought,
killand murder one Jane Porter, by administeringto her a deadly and corrosive
and destructivepoison,calledstrychnine,from theeffectsof which thesaidJane
Porterdied,& c.:held, upon motion in arrestofjudgment,thattheindictment
was sufficient; though itdid not charge that death occurred within a year and a
day afterthe poison was administered, or that the prisonerknew that strychnine
was poisonous; and though itattributedthemalicetothekillingand not tothe
administrationof thepoison. Jane vs.Commonwealth,3 Met., 18.
b. Burglary. In an indictment forburglary, itis necessaryto aver a breaking with
intenttorob,but where the indictmentchargesa breakingand robbery, thatisa
sufficientavermentof intent. Olivevs.Commonwealth,5 Bush,376.
Itisnot necessaryin such an indictmentto chargethat thebreakingwas done
" burglariously." Ibid.
c. Robbery. An indictment charging the defendant with having taken a mare , the

beryat thecommon law. Commonwealthvs.Brooks, I Duv ., 150.
Assaultwith intenttorob. An indictment charging an assaultwith intentto rob is

2 Bush , 1 .
d. Duelling. An indictmentagainstone forchallenginganothertofightinsingle

essary. Moody vs.Commonwealth ,4 Met ., 1 ; Heffrenvs. Commonwealth, 4 Met ., 5 .
e. Bribery. A charge in an indictment for briberyat an electionthat defendant
" bribed" another to vote issufficientwithout furtherdescriptionof the act, the

Stephenson,3 Met ., 226.
Commonwealth us .
That an electionwas held at the time and place statedin an indictment forbribery,

didates for the offices . Ibid .

h . Disorderlyhouse. An indictment charging thatdefendant " did sufferand permit

ant kept the house, or had leasedit to another, knowing the purposes forwhich

ant. Taylorvs. Commonwealth , I Duv ., 160.
i. Extortion. In an indictment againsta jailer, for extortingmoney from a person

oned . Commonwealth vs.Mitchell, 3 Bush , 25 .

vs.Mitchell,3 Bush , 30 .
k. Keepinguncleanjail. A jailercannotbe proceededagainstby indictmentfor
failingto keep the jailclean, & c.. Commonwealth vs.Mitchell, 3 Bush , 39.
1. Exhibitingtheatricalperformance. An indictment charging that the defendant

monwealth vs.Harvey , 16 B. М. , 1.

I. TESTIMONY OF DEFENDANTS FOR OR AGAINST EACH OTHER .
a . According tothecommon law one of severaljointdefendantswas incompetent
to testifyfor or againsta co-defendant though triedseparately,under the ruleof
evidence forbiddingpartiesto testify. That rulewas recognizedby ? 232 of the
Code of 1854, providing, that where two or more persons were included in the
same indictment, and the court is of opinion that the evidence as to one is not
sufficienttoput him on his defense,itmust,on motion of eitherpartydesiring
to use him as a witness, order him to be discharged from the indictment, and
permithim to be examinedby the partysomoving. Adwellvs.Commonwealth,
17 В. М. , 318 ; Thompson vs. Same , I Met ., 16 ; Chandler vs.Same , I Bush , 41 .
Edgerton vs.Same , 7 Bush , 142. 232 of the Code of 1854 has been substituted
by ? 234 of thisCode, providingthatin such caseeachshallbe a competentwit-
ness forthe othersunlessthe indictment charge a conspiracy.
2. TESTIMONY OF DEFENDANT'S WIFE FOR CO -DEFENDANT .
a. Upon the separatetrialof one defendant,undera jointindictmentagainsthim
and severalothers, charging them with murder , the wives of the latterare com-

ecution, would be concluded by any verdicttherein. Corneliusvs.Commonwealth,

a. The admissionof proofof dying declarationsisnot an infringementof thecon-

. Dying declarationsare only admissible in evidence where the death of the

c. In Caricovs. Commonwealth, 7 Bush , 129,evidence was offeredthat a man named
Offert, then dead , had told the accused that the deceased had armed himself
with a shot-gun to killhim : held, that, though " what Offert saidwas not legal
evidence of Smith's arming himself to killappellant, itwas competent to prove
that the appellant had so heard and may have had a right so to believe, and
to that extent, and for that purpose, itwas admissible."
17. CIRCUMSTANTIAL EVIDENCE .
Circumstantial evidence, when competent , should be leftto the jury, like other
evidence, without caution or suggestionby the court as to itsvalue or the neces-
sityof scrutinizingitclosely. Brady vs. Commonwealth, 11 Bush , 282.
18. PREPONDERANCE OF EVIDENCE .
A mere preponderance of evidence does not authorize a verdict of guilty, in a
criminalcase. The evidence should be such as to produce a convictionof guilt,
beyond a reasonabledoubt. Jane vs.Commonwealth,2.Met.,33.
19. IMPEACHING WITNESS .
Questions which are intended to ascertainthe feelingsof the witnessto the prisoner
may be asked; they may have an important bearing in determining the guiltor
innocence of the accused. Corneliusvs.Commonwealth , 15 B. M. , 539.

a . INSTRUCTING JURY AFTER COMMENCEMENT OF ARGUMENT .

6. ORAL INSTRUCTIONS .
A remark by the court to thejury that " the defendant'sattorneyhad letdown the
fence and thatallisnow beforethe jury," amounted to an oral instruction, and
was therefore erroneous . Coffagevs.Commonwealth , 3 Bush , 532.
In Payne vs. Commonwealth , 1 Met .,477-8, itwas held that the court had no right
tomake the juryoralexplanationsof writteninstructions; and thatthe error
was not cured by reducing the verbal explanationsto writing, and sending them
to the jury on the day afterthey had retiredto considerthe case. And see? 249,
Criminal Code .
c. RIGHTS AND DUTIES OF COURT AND JURY WITH REFERENCE TO INSTRUCTIONS .
It is the duty and right of the court to instructthe jury, in criminalcases, as to
the law of the case,and itis the duty of the jury to regard such instructionsas
containingthe law; but if they disregardthem and acquit the accused, the court
couldnot, forthatreason, granta new trial. Thus farjurieshave theultimate
decisionof the law as well as the facts. Commonwealth us. Van Tuyl, I Met ., I.
d . ENUMERATION OF COLLATERAL FACTS .
" The instructionsgiven are nearlyallobjectionablebecause of an attempt made
to enumerate the collateralfactswhich the evidence tended to prove, insteadof
being hypothecated upon the factsnecessaryto constituteguilt or make out a
defense. The objectionto an attempt to enumerate the factswhich the evidence
tendsto prove,insteadof basingthe instructionson the factsnecessaryto be
found by the jury, is, that it unnecessarilylengthensthe instructions, and ison
that account calculatedto confuse and mislead the jury; and it is liableto the
furtherobjection, that, by giving prominence to the factsenumerated , other
factsnot recitedseem to be subordinated, and may on that account be overlooked
by the jury; or they may conclude that as the court has referredto a part, and
omitted to mention other factswhich the evidence tends to prove, such facts
were deemed by the court of no importance . Instructionsought , therefore, as
a generalrule,tobe based onlyupon such factsasmust be foundby the juryin
order to establishguilt, or to make out a defense; thus leaving to counsel to
argue the evidence tending to establishthe essentialfacts, and to the jury to
decidehow farthe evidenceestablishesthem." Coffmanvs.Commonwealth, 10
Bush , 497 .
e . GROUPING OF PROMINENT FACTS .
Concerningthefollowinginstruction: " If thejuryaresatisfiedfrom theevidence,
that the accused, Williams, went offand armed himself, and returning renewed
the attackupon Childs, who , seeingWilliamsapproach, fledforhislife, and
Williams ,following afterwith his missile, overtook and killedhim , then Wil-
liamsisguiltyof murder," thecourtsaid: " This instructionis,inouropinion,
liableto the objection that, in it, certainprominent factswhich the evidence
conducedtoprovearegroupedtogether; and,irrespectiveof others,[are]stated
hypotheticallyto the jury as constitutingsufficientgrounds forfindingthedefend-
ant guiltyof murder . But a stillmore seriouserrorin the instructionisthat the
jurywere therebytold, ineffect,notmerelythatthefactsthereinsupposedwere
evidence from which they might find that the killingof Childs was both mali-
cious,and without legal excuse,and thereforemurder ; but that those facts, if

And see Holloway vs. Commonwealth , II Bush , 351 .
f. ASSUMING FACTS .
Concerningthefollowinginstruction: " If thejurybelievefrom the evidence,to
theexclusionof a reasonabledoubt,thattheaccused,not inhisapparentlynec-

cation given at the time he assaultedand struck the deceased with a piece of

g . PROLIXITY .
In Moore vs.Commonwealth ,7 Bush , 192, the court,without statingwhat instructions
were given, said: " The instructions, as given at the instance of the Common-

sentationof the law of the case." See Butlervs. Commonwealth , 2 Duv ., 434 ;
and Smith vs. Commonwealth , I Duv ., 224 .
h . ABSTRACT INSTRUCTIONS .
Concerning the followinginstruction: Where there isa conflictin the testimony
of witnesses,the one side being of an affirmative, and the other of a negative
character, the affirmativecharacterof testimony is preferred, and isentitledto
the greaterweight by the jury in making up theirverdict," the courtsaid:
" This instructioncontainsa mere abstractprincipleof law thatisoftenapplied
by the judicial, discriminatingmind to the factsof a case, in order to arrive at
the truth, without doing injusticeto eitherparty; but we are satisfiedthat the
effectof such an instruction, and particularlyin a case like this, would be to
mislead the jury." LouisvilleChemical Works vs. Commonwealth , & Bush , 182.

j . WEIGHT OF EVIDENCE .
The Englishpracticeofchargingthejuryuponthefacts,and commentingupon
the weight of the evidence, was abolished by the Criminal Code of Practice,
which requiresthe courtto instructthe jury upon the " law applicabletothe
case," and which , " by a clear implication, prohibitsthe court from making sug-
gestions to the jury as to the weight of any particularspecies of evidence, or
what considerationthey should give it." Brady vs. Commonwealth , 11 Bush , 285.
[In a civilcase,decided in 1847, itwas held that, independently of any statute,
the right to charge the jury upon the factsdid not existin Kentucky . Howara
etal.vs. Coke etal., 7 B. M. , 655.]

In Adwell vs. Commonwealth, 17 B. M. , 319, the court said: " The positionassumed
on the part of the defendant, in the sixth instructionasked for, was obviously
erroneous,and was thereforeproperlyoverruledby the court. The proposition
itcontained was , that if, in the opinion of the jury, one of the witnessesforthe
Commonwealth , by the name of Lloyd , was incapable from age, blindness, or
other cause, of so observing the accused as to enable him to identifyhim , with
undoubted certainty,they should acquitthe prisoner, unlessthey believe,beyond
a reasonable doubt , that he was identifiedby other proof in the cause, inde-
pendent of Lloyd's testimony. The effectof the instruction, as asked, would
have been to exclude Lloyd's testimony from the considerationof the jury alto-
gether, if italone was not sufficientto identifythe accused, with undoubted cer-
tainty, as one of the persons who committed the murder , although the jury
might have regarded it as entirelysufficientfor that purpose, in conjunction
with the other testimony in the cause. The jury had an undoubted right to
considerall the testimony together, and to give to each part of it such weight
as, in theiropinion, it was justlyentitledto. The propositionthat each part
of itshouldbe separatelyconsideredand passedupon by thejury,had neither
the sanction of law nor reason to sustain it."

1. PROVOCATION .
" Mere words or gestures, though they may excitepassion, do not constitutesuch
provocation as will of itselfextenuate a homicide committed with a deadly
weapon , and make itmanslaughter." Rapp vs. Commonwealth, 14 В. М. , 621.
But in Donnellan vs. Commonwealth , 7 Bush , 678, the court gave the following
instruction: " If the killingwas done without malice, and in sudden heat and
passioncausedby legalprovocation,itwas manslaughter; and to reduce the offense
from murder to manslaughter the jury must believe,from the evidence, thatitwas
committed in sudden heat and passionarisingfrom a legalprovocation; and no
words or oaths are in law sufficientprovocation to cause or excuse such sudden
heat and passion; but that legalprovocationcan onlyarisein caseswhere such words
areaccompanied by, or succeedsuch an assaultmade by thedeceasedon the defendant
priorto,or at thetime of, theshooting, as put thelatterin realor apparentdanger of
his lifeor great bodi'yharm ;" and the Court of Appeals said, with referenceto
that instruction: " Is it true that such provocation can only arisewhen such
words were accompanied by or succeeded such an assaultas to put the slayerin
real or apparent danger of losing his life, or of great bodily harm ? We think
not . Without resorting,forillustration, to particularinstancesmentioned in the
argument , we are of the opinion that, however great the provocationmust be to
lower the grade of the crime from murder , itwas erroneous and misleading to so
instructthe jury as to restrictitto a stateof case constitutingan excuse forkill-
ing in self-defense."

" Circumstantialevidence,being conceded to be competent , and when of a satis-

n . INCOMPETENT EVIDENCE .
In a proceeding,by warrant from a citycourt, against the defendant for selling
liquoron Sunday,evidencewas erroneouslyadmittedshowing salesof liquor
afterthe issuingof the warrant; but the errorwas held to be cured by an instruc-
tion to the effectthat, unless the jury believed, from the evidence, that the
defendant sold liquor on Sunday , within the time covered by the warrant, they
must acquithim . Megowan vs. Commonwealth , 2 Met ., 5.
But in Dye vs. Commonwealth , 3 Bush , 3, it was held that an error in admitting
hearsay evidence was not cured " by instructingthe jury to disregardsuch here-
say evidence, exceptsofar asitharmonizedwithfactsafterwardsdeveloped.”
0. REFERRING LAW TO JURY .
The followinginstruction: " That , if they have a reasonabledoubt of the truthof
any fact, any seriesof facts, or propositionsnecessaryand essential, in their
judgment,to the conclusionof guilt,the prisonerisentitledto the benefitof
thatdoubt , and they must acquithim ," was held to be erroneous, because, inde-
pendently of any mere verbalcriticism, itreferredto the jury to determine what
factswere essentialto the conclusionof guilt,and then to determinewhether
such factswere establishedby the evidence beyond a reasonabledoubt , which , in
effect, referred the whole law and facts of the case to them . Sparks vs. Common-
wealth , 3 Bush , 112 .
In Payne vs.Commonwealth, 1 Met ., 370, the court gave this instruction: " If the
jury believefrom the evidence that Payne killedWhite upon legal provocation,
and without malice, and in sudden heat and passion, and not in self-defense,
theymust findhim guiltyof manslaughter;" and the Court of Appeals saidthat
this instructionleftthe jury to determine for themselveswhat constituteslegal
provocation, and in that respectwas clearlyerroneous.
And seeBeckervs.Crow,7 Bush, 200,as toleavingthejurytodeterminethelegal
effectof pleadings.

wealth ,14 B. M. , 614 ; Meredith vs. Commonwealth , 18 B. M. , 49 ; Young vs. Com-
monwealth, 6 Bush , 312 ; Holloway vs. Commonwealth , II Bush , 344 ; Smaltz vs.
Commonwealth , 3 Bush , 32.

Bush .

a. An attempttoshootanotherisbut an inferiordegreeof theoffenseof shootingat

vs. Commonwealth , 2 Duv ., 394 .
b. What is included. The indictment charged a shooting with intent to killand
murder . The jury found the defendant guiltyof shootingwith intentto killor
wound ; both of the offensesbeing equallypenal, and the charge of intent to
killand murder being the higher offense,includes that of an intent to killor

c. Upon an indictment for maliciousstabbing, with intent to kill, the defendant
may be foundguiltyeitherof thefelonycharged,or of any lowerdegreeof that
offense. Tyra vs. Commonwealth ,2 Met., I.
d. On an indictmentforan offenseconsistingof differentdegrees,the defendant
may be found guiltyof any degree not higher than that charged in the indict-
ment , and may be found guiltyof any offenseincluded in the offensecharged.
Commonwealth vs.Garland, 3 Met .,479 .
The firstand secondsectionsof the actof 1858,againstgaming with negroes(I
Rev. Stat., 572), define an offenseconsistingof two degrees. To convictof the
firstdegree it is necessaryto show that " money or other thing of value" shall
have been bet, won , or lost, whatever may have been the amount or the value
of the money or thing so bet ; and it is not necessary to prove the value as
alleged. To convictof thelowerdegree,asdefinedby thesecondsection,itis
only necessary to prove the gaming . And , under an indictment charging the
factsnecessaryto constitutethe higher degree of the offense, the defendant may
be convicted of the lower degree. Ib.

a. This sectionis the same as & 327 of the Criminal Code of 1854, which , as was
held in Commonwealthvs.Craig, 15B. M. , 534,didnotauthorizean appealfrom
a judgment rendered before that Code took effect.
APPEAL BY DEFENDANT .
a. Itwas held under the CriminalCode of 1854,upon generalprinciples,thatan
escaped prisonerhad no right to prosecute an appeal from a judgment of con-
viction. Wilson vs. Commonwealth , 10 Bush , 526.
That ruledoes not seem to have been changed by thisCode , though itallows an
appealforany " errorof law apparentupon the record" (§ 340)," as a matter
of right," and without leave of a judge of the Court of Appeals .
APPEALS BY COMMONWEALTH .
a. Itwas held that the Code of 1854 did not authorize appeals by the Com non-
wealth from judgments of acquittalin misdemeanor -cases. Commonwealth vs.
Dobbins, 9 Bush , 1. This Code does not seem to have made any change in that
respect .
340. FOR WHAT ERRORS A JUDGMENT OF CONVICTION MAY BE RE-
VERSED .
a. Section334 of theCode of 1854 authorizeda reversalof a judgment of convic
tionfor the followingerrors, only, apparent in the record-
1. In admittingor rejectingimportantevidence.
2. In instructingor refusingtoinstructthejury.
3. Infailingtoarrestjudgment .
4. In allowingor disallowinga peremptorychallenge.
Itwas heldthata judgment of convictioncouldnotbe reversedbecausethejudge
refusedto grant a new trial, on the ground that a juror unknown to the defend.
ant had expressedan opinion against him beforethe trial(Cornflinsvs. Common
wealth, 15 B. M. , 544 ; Comely vs. Commonwealth , 17 B. M. , 403); nor because
the verdictwas found by 11 jurors (Tyra vs. Commonwealth , 2 Met ., 2); nor
becausethe casewas triedbeforea specialjudge,not qualifiedas requiredby
law (Comelyvs.Commonwealth,17 B. M. ,408); nor fora refusaltogranta new
trial(Walston vs.Commonwealth , 16 B. M. , 42); nor for disallowing a challenge
forcause(Moore vs.Commonwealth,7 Bush, 191); nor becausea casewas improp-
erlytransferredfrom one court to another (Tipper vs. Commonwealth , 1 Met ., 6);
nor because the verdictwas not sustainedby the evidence. Tippervs. Common
wealth , I Met ., 6 .
Observe that § 340 of thisCode authorizesa reversalof a judgment of conviction,
forany errorapparent in the record.
b. As tothegroundsfor reversalunder theCode of 1854, which are statedabove-
I. ERROR IN ADMITTING OR REJECTING IMPORTANT EVIDENCE .
To authorizethereversalofa judgment of convictionforfelonyupon theground
of the rejectionof evidence offeredon the part of the defendant, it isnot suffi
cientthat the rejectedevidence be shown to have been merely pertinentor rele.
vant,or technicallyadmissible- it must be importantfor the defendant, in view
of the whole case as presented. Champ vs. Commonwealth , 2 Met ., 17.
The answerexpectedfrom thewitnessmust appear,inordertoshow thatthecourt
erred in not permittingit. Chrystalvs. Commonwealth , 9 Bush , 669 ; Nicholsvs.
Commonwealth , 11 Bush , 575.

Ifincompetentevidencewas exceptedto,itisground forreversal,thoughnot stated
asa ground fornew trial. Johnsonvs.Commonwealth,9 Bush,224.
In Hopkins vs. Commonwealth, 3 Bush , 480, it was held that the defendant could
not availhimself of an errorof the court, in erroneouslygiving to the jury an
oral instruction, because he had failedto rely upon that error in his written
grounds for a new trial. In Johnson vs. Commonwealth , supra, the court, refer-
ring to the admission of incompetent evidence against the defendant, to which ,
as the record showed , he had taken exceptions, said: " We see no reason, when
thiserrorismade to appear from the record before us with the proper exception,
thatthiscourtshouldrefusetoreversethe judgment becausethe errorwas not
assignedas one of the grounds fora new trialin the court below , when itisnot
one of the errorsmentioned by the Code forwhich a new trialmay be granted.
It istruethatby ? 277 of theCriminalCode billsof exceptionareto be regu-
lated by the provisionsof the Civil Code ; but thisdoes not require that the
accused should, on his motion for a new trial, relyon any other grounds than
those designated by the Criminal Code in order to enable him to be heard in
this court. It is well settled, in the trialof civilcauses, that when an error
complained of in thiscourt was not made the ground fora new trialin the court
below , it cannot availthe party here. The grounds specifiedfor a new trialin
a civilcase includeall errorsof law committed during the progress of the trial,
and thisappliesas well to the admission or rejectionof testimony as to the
givingor refusinginstructions. In a criminalcasetheonlyerrorsof law speci-
fiedas grounds for a new trialconsistin giving or refusinginstructions, and
theadmissionof incompetenttestimonyisby no provisionof theCriminalCode
requiredto be insertedas one of the grounds for a new trial, even if reliedon ."
Section340 of thisCode authorizesa reversalof a judgment of conviction, for
any error of law apparent on the record. It would seem to follow, from the
reasoningin Johnson vs.Commonwealth ,and from the decisionin Hopkins vs.Com-
monwealth, thatthe defendant should state, in his grounds for a new trial, every
errorupon which he means to relyfor a reversal.
4. ERROR IN ALLOWING OR DISALLOWING A PEREMPTORY CHALLENGE .
a. It was held that, however the disallowance of a challengefor cause by the
defendantmay have influencedhiscourseintheuseof theperemptorychallenges
allowed him , itcannot be regarded as a refusalto allow a peremptory challenge,
within the meaning of ? 334 of the Criminal Code ; and , regarded as the disal-
lowanceof a challengefor causemerely,itcan constituteno ground of reversal
in the Court of Appeals . Moore vs. Commonwealth , 7 Bush , 191.
b. The provisionof 2 340, authorizing a reversalof a judgment of conviction,for
" any errorapparent on the record," does not authorizethe Court of Appeals to
considerthe sufficiencyof the evidence to support the verdict. Murphy vs. Com-
monwealth , I Diet., 365

Section335 of theCriminalCode of 1854declared,asdoes? 341 of thisCode,that

APPEALS IN MISDEMEANOR CASES .
a. It was held under the Code of 1854-
I. That ? 342 only applied to cases provided for by the Criminal Code itself, and
in which the punishment authorizedwas fineor imprisonment or both ; that it
did not apply to a judgment disbarringan attorney; and thatan appeal could be
prosecuted from such a judgment , though itdid not inflicteitherfineor impris-
onment . Turner vs. Commonwealth , 2 Met .,619.
2. That no appeal could be prosecuted by the Commonwealth from a judgment for
the defendant, who , under an act of 1854, which prescribed a penalty of $ 100
for betting on elections, and a forfeitureof the thing won and received, was
indictedfor that offense- the indictment failingto allegethat the defendant had
receivedanything by reason of the bet ; and the defendant being,therefore, sub-
jectonlyto a fineof one hundreddollars. Commonwealthvs.Crump, & c., 18 B.
M. , 470 .
3. That theCourtof Appeals had no appellatejurisdictionover a judgment fora
finefor lessthan $ 50, rendered by the citycourt of Louisvillein a prosecution
formisdemeanor . Holden vs. Commonwealth , 2 Bush , 36.
WHEN AND HOW APPEAL MUST BE TAKEN .
a. An appealmust be takenduringthetermatwhichthejudgmentisrendered
(Commonwealthvs.Adams, 16 В. М. , 338); althoughtime be given, by consent
of parties,untilthe next term, to preparea billof exceptions. Commonwealth

be done by withholding or suspending judgment . Ib.
b. Motion for a new trialsuspendsthejudgment; and,ifitbe continuedto the
next term, and be then overruled, an appeal may be then taken, and the record
may be lodgedin the clerk'sofficeof the Court of Appealswithinsixtydays
thereafter. LouisvilleChemical Works vs. Commonwealth , 8 Bush , 179.

FOR WHAT ERRORS JUDGMENT MAY BE REVERSED .
a . Upon an appeal from a judgment in a misdemeanor -case, the Court of Appeals
cannot considerthe sufficiencyof the evidence to support the verdict(Murphy vs.
Commonwealth , I Met ., 365); nor willitreversea judgment because,in pursuance
of agreement the case was triedby less than twelve jurors(Ibid); nor forerro-
neous instructionsnot prejudicialto the defendant. Robinsonvs. Commonwealth ,
16 В. М. , 618 .
DAMAGES UPON AFFIRMANCE OR DISMISSAL .
a . Both the Civil and Criminal Codes of 1854 provided for 10 per cent.damages
upon the affirmanceof an appeal. Neither of them expresslyprovided fordam-
ages upon the dismissalof an appeal. It was held, however , under both of them ,
that the appelleewas entitledto 10 per cent. damages upon a dismissalof an
appeal. Madison, Indianapolisand Peru Railroad vs.Briscoe& Levi, 18 B. M. ,
570 ; Evans vs. Commonwealth , 3 Bush , 161.
BOND TO KEEP THE PEACE .
a. In the absence of proof to the contrary, itwillbe presumed that a bond to keep
the peace was properly required. Rankin & Zahn vs. Commonwealth , 9 Bush ,
553-
2391 . BREACH OF BOND TO KEEP THE PEACE .
a . A convictionof the offenseof drunkenness and disorderlyconduct is not neces-
sarilya convictionof an offenseinvolving a breach of the peace; and does not
authorize a forfeitureof a bond " to keep the peace, and be of good behavior,"
without a directproceeding to forfeitthe bond . Rankin & Zahn vs. Common-
wealth,9 Bush , 553.

a. See notes to sections67, 68 .
430 to 441 . IMPEACHMENT .
a. There are two modes prescribedby the Constitutionwhereby a civilofficermay be
removed from officebeforethe expirationof histerm : I. By impeachment ,a mode
thatappliestoand embraceseverycivilofficerintheState; and,2. By indictment
or presentment,which only appliestojudges of the county court,justicesof the
peace, sheriffs, coroners, surveyors, jailers, countyassessor, attorneyfor the
county, and constables. Constitution, art. 5 ; Ib., art. 4, sec., 36 ; Lowe vs. Com-
monwealth, 3 Mct ., 237.
b. Section 36 of article4 of the Constitution, of itself, and without other legisla-
tion, authorizesan indictment againstany of the officersnamed thereinfor the
offensesmentioned , and a conviction and judgment vacating the offender's
office . Ib .
c. Wherever the Constitutionhas createdan officeand fixeditsterms, and has also
declared upon what grounds and in what mode an incumbent of such officemay
be removed before the expirationof his term,itisbeyond the power of the Leg-
islatureto remove such officeror suspendhim from officeforany other reasonor
in any other mode than the Constitutionitselfhas furnished. Ib.
